Ayahs

حمٓ (١)
Ha, Meem. (1)
تَنزِيلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ (٢)
The [gradual] sending down of the Book is from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-knowing, (2)
غَافِرِ ٱلذَّنۢبِ وَقَابِلِ ٱلتَّوْبِ شَدِيدِ ٱلْعِقَابِ ذِى ٱلطَّوْلِ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ إِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٣)
forgiver of sins and acceptor of repentance, severe in retribution, [yet] all-bountiful; there is no god except Him, [and] toward Him is the destination. (3)

Content of Surah al-Mu’min

Surah al-Mu’min is the first of the "Hawamim" (the Hawamim are seven surahs of the Quran that begin with "Ha-Mim," are placed consecutively, and are all Meccan surahs).

The nature of this surah, like other Meccan surahs, involves discussions on various theological issues and the foundations and principles of religion, as the primary need of Muslims during that era was the strengthening of their ideological pillars.

The content of this surah is a collection of "wrath" and "grace," "warning" and "glad tidings," a decisive and crushing logical struggle against tyrants and the arrogant, and kindness and mercy toward truth-seeking and truth-demanding believers.

A distinctive feature of this surah is a passage from the story of Musa عليه السلام and Pharaoh concerning the "Believer of the Family of Pharaoh," which is mentioned only in this surah and nowhere else in the Quran. It is the story of that man of faith—highly intelligent and resourceful—who was among the Pharaonic elite but had inwardly believed in Musa عليه السلام. He served as a secure stronghold for the defense of Musa عليه السلام and his faith. As we shall see, at the moments when Musa عليه السلام was in danger of death, he rushed to his aid with a very clever and subtle method and saved him from death!

The naming of this surah as Surah "al-Mu’min" (The Believer) is for this very reason: the account of his struggles spans more than "20 verses" of this surah, which is about one-fourth of the total verses of the surah.

It appears that the description of the state of the Believer of the Family of Pharaoh in this surah was a calculated educational program for a group of Muslims in Mecca who, while believing in the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, outwardly maintained their friendly relations with the stubborn and persistent enemies to serve as a stronghold for days of danger. It is said that Abu Talib, the Prophet's uncle, was among this group, as this meaning has been narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in Islamic traditions. [^1] In any case, the content of this surah can be summarized into six sections:

The first section, which forms the beginning of the surah, is an attention toward Allah and some of His Beautiful Names (Asma al-Husna), especially those that stir both fear and hope in hearts, such as "Forgiver of sin and Severe in punishment."

The second section consists of threats against the arrogant disbelievers regarding worldly punishments—similar to what befell previous rebellious nations—and the punishments of the Resurrection, mentioning their characteristics and details.

The third section, after presenting the story of Musa عليه السلام and Pharaoh, shifts the discourse to the Believer of the Family of Pharaoh and devotes a large portion of the surah to describing the dialogues of this intelligent and brave man with the people of Pharaoh.

The fourth section again draws the subject toward scenes of the Resurrection to awaken the hearts of the heedless.

In the fifth section, the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and Polytheism (Shirk)—the most important issue of human life—is brought forward, presenting some signs of Monotheism and arguments for the vanity of Polytheism.

In the sixth section, which is the final part of this surah, while inviting the Prophet to patience

[^1]: Al-Ghadir, Vol. 8, p. 388.

and steadfastness, it presents a summary of what passed in other sections of this surah regarding issues of the Origin and the Return (Ma'ad), taking lessons from the fate of predecessors, threatening the stubborn polytheists, and mentioning glimpses of Divine blessings, thus concluding the surah.

We mentioned that the naming of the surah as "al-Mu’min" is because of the section it states regarding the Believer of the Family of Pharaoh, just as its naming as "Ghafir" is because of the beginning of its third verse.

The Virtue of Reciting this Surah

In Islamic traditions narrated from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, many virtues have been mentioned for all the "Ha-Mim" surahs in general and Surah "al-Mu’min" in particular.

In the first part, it is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that He said: "The Hawamim are the crown of the Quran!" [^1] Ibn Abbas also has a statement that he likely heard from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or Ali عليه السلام, saying: "Everything has a core, and the core of the Quran is the Ha-Mim surahs." [^2] And in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"The Hawamim are the fragrant herbs (Rayhan) of the Quran; so praise Allah and thank Him by memorizing and reciting them. Indeed, when a servant stands to recite the Hawamim, there comes out of his mouth a fragrance more pleasant than the finest musk and ambergris. Verily, Allah has mercy on its reciter and reader, and He has mercy on his neighbors, his friends, his acquaintances, and every close or distant relative of his. And on the Day of Resurrection, the Throne ('Arsh), the Pedestal (Kursi), and Allah's near-stationed angels seek forgiveness for him." [^1]

In another Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"The Hawamim are seven, and the gates of Hell are seven. Each Ha-Mim will come and stand at one of these gates, saying: 'O Allah! Do not let anyone who believed in me and recited me enter through this gate!'" [^2]

In the second part, in a Hadith from the Prophet, we read:

"Whoever recites Surah Ha-Mim al-Mu’min, there remains no soul of a Prophet, a truthful one (Siddiq), or a believer except that they send blessings upon him and seek forgiveness for him." [^3]

It is clear that these great virtues are linked to that prominent content—content that, if implemented in a person's life in its "ideological" and "practical" dimensions, undoubtedly makes one deserving of these immense virtues. If recitation is mentioned in these traditions, it refers to a recitation that serves as a prelude to faith and action.

The meaningful expression found in one of the Prophetic traditions, which says whoever recites "Ha-Mim" and believes in it, is a clear witness to this statement.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful

Ha-Mim (1) The revelation of the Book is from Allah, the Almighty, the All-Knowing (2) The Forgiver of sin, the Accepter of repentance, the Severe in punishment, the Bestower of bounty. There is no deity except Him; to Him is the final return (3)

Commentary

Hope-Inspiring Attributes!

At the beginning of this surah, we again encounter the "Disjointed Letters" (Muqatta'at), which here appear in a new form: "Ha" and "Mim."

Regarding the interpretation of these letters, we have had extensive discussions at the beginning of Surahs al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, al-A'raf, and some other surahs. What should be added here is that in some traditions, as well as in many words of the commentators, the two letters at the beginning of this surah have been interpreted as names of God that begin with these two letters. As in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, they were interpreted as "Hamid" (Praiseworthy) and "Majid" (Glorious). [^1] Some have also interpreted "H" as names like "Hamid," "Halim" (Forbearing), and "Hannan" (Compassionate), and "M" as names like "Malik" (King), "Maalik" (Master), and "Majid."

It is also possible that "H" refers to the Sovereignty (Hakimiyyah) and "M" refers to the Ownership (Malikiyyah) of Allah.

It is also narrated from Ibn Abbas that "Ha-Mim" is among the Greatest Names of Allah. [^2] It is clear that these interpretations are not mutually exclusive and all may be encompassed within the meaning of the verse.

In the following verse, as is the method of the Quran, after mentioning the "Disjointed Letters," it speaks of the greatness of the status of the "Quran," pointing out that this Book, with all its greatness, is composed of simple letters of the alphabet—such a magnificent structure from such small materials—and this itself is a proof of its miraculous nature.

He says: "This is a Book revealed from Allah, the Almighty, the All-Knowing" (تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ مِنَ اللَّهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْعَلِيمِ).

His Might and Power have made it such that no one can rival it, and His Knowledge has caused its content to be at the highest degree of perfection, encompassing all the needs of humans on the path of perfection.

The next verse describes Allah with five other attributes from His great attributes—some inspiring hope and some inspiring fear—saying: "The One who forgives sins" (غافِرِ الذَّنْبِ). "Accepts repentances" (قابِلِ التَّوْبِ). [^1] "His punishment is severe" (شَدِيدِ الْعِقابِ). "His bounty is abundant" (ذِي الطَّوْلِ). [^2] "The God besides whom there is no deity" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ). "And the return of all of you is toward Him" (إِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ).

Indeed, the One who possesses these attributes is alone worthy of servitude and is the Master of reward and punishment.

Key Points

1- In the two verses above (Verses 2 and 3), after mentioning the name "Allah" and before mentioning the "Return" (إِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ), seven attributes of the Divine attributes are stated, some of which are "Attributes of Essence" and some "Attributes of Action." They express a collection of Monotheism, Knowledge, Power, Mercy, and Wrath. "Almighty" and "All-Knowing" are the foundation for the revelation of this Heavenly Book, while "Forgiveness of sins," "Acceptance of repentance," "Severity of punishment," and "Bestowal of bounties" are a prelude to the cultivation of souls and the worship of the One God, besides whom there is no deity.

2- Among these attributes, "Forgiver of sin" is prioritized, and "Possessor of bounty" (Dhi al-Tawl) comes at the end, with "Severe in punishment" placed in between. In reality, His wrath is placed between two mercies! Furthermore, alongside this attribute which indicates Allah's wrath, three of His attributes indicating His mercy are mentioned, and all of this is proof that "His mercy precedes His wrath" (Ya man sabaqat rahmatuhu ghadhabahu).

3- The phrase "To Him is the final return" (إِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ) not only indicates that the return of everyone in the Resurrection is toward Him, but its absolute nature suggests that the return of all affairs in this world and the next is toward Him, and the chain of all beings is in His hand.

4- It is noteworthy that the phrase "There is no deity except Him," which is mentioned as the last attribute and indicates the station of "Monotheism in Worship" and the unworthiness of others for worship, is stated as the final attribute and the ultimate result. Therefore, in a Hadith from Ibn Abbas, it is said: He is "Forgiver of sin" for the one who says "La ilaha illa Allah," "Accepter of repentance" for the one who says "La ilaha illa Allah," "Severe in punishment" for the one who does not say "La ilaha illa Allah," and "Possessor of bounty," Rich and Self-Sufficient from the one who does not say "La ilaha illa Allah."

Thus, the axis of all these attributes are those who are believers in Monotheism and do not deviate from this original Divine line in speech and action.

5- Causes of Forgiveness in the Holy Quran

In the Holy Quran, many things are introduced as causes for forgiveness and the erasure of sins, some of which are mentioned below:

1- "Repentance": "O you who have believed, repent to Allah with sincere repentance. Perhaps your Lord will remit from you your misdeeds..." (At-Tahrim: 8).

2- Faith and Righteous Deeds: "And those who believe and do righteous deeds and believe in what has been sent down upon Muhammad—and it is the truth from their Lord—He will remit from them their misdeeds..." (Muhammad: 2).

3- Piety (Taqwa): "If you fear Allah, He will grant you a criterion and will remit from you your misdeeds..." (Al-Anfal: 29).

4- Migration, Jihad, and Martyrdom: "So those who emigrated or were evicted from their homes or were harmed in My cause or fought or were killed—I will surely remit from them their misdeeds..." (Al-Imran: 195).

5- Secret Charity: "If you disclose your charitable expenditures, they are good; but if you conceal them and give them to the poor, it is better for you, and He will remit from you some of your misdeeds..." (Al-Baqarah: 271).

6- Giving a Goodly Loan (Qard al-Hasan): "If you loan Allah a goodly loan, He will multiply it for you and forgive you..." (At-Taghabun: 17).

7- Avoiding Major Sins, which leads to the forgiveness of minor sins: "If you avoid the major sins which you are forbidden, We will remit from you your misdeeds..." (An-Nisa: 31).

In this way, the doors of Divine forgiveness are open to the servants from every side, seven of which were mentioned above based on seven Quranic verses—whichever door we may enter through, and how much better it is to enter through every door.

Ayahs

مَا يُجَـٰدِلُ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَلَا يَغْرُرْكَ تَقَلُّبُهُمْ فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ (٤)
No one disputes the signs of Allah except the faithless. So do not be misled by their bustle in the towns. (4)
كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَٱلْأَحْزَابُ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ ۖ وَهَمَّتْ كُلُّ أُمَّةٍۭ بِرَسُولِهِمْ لِيَأْخُذُوهُ ۖ وَجَـٰدَلُوا۟ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ لِيُدْحِضُوا۟ بِهِ ٱلْحَقَّ فَأَخَذْتُهُمْ ۖ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عِقَابِ (٥)
The people of Noah denied before them and the [heathen] factions [who came] after them. Every nation attempted to lay hands on their apostle, and disputed erroneously to refute the truth. Then I seized them; so how was My retribution?! (5)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ (٦)
That is how the word of your Lord became due concerning the faithless, that they shall be inmates of the Fire. (6)

Commentary

The Lord’s Decisive Decree!

After mentioning the revelation of the Quran from Allah and describing Him with attributes that inspire both fear and hope, the text speaks of a group who rise in dispute and aggression against these divine verses. It clarifies the fate of this group through short and crushing sentences, stating: "None dispute regarding the signs of Allah except those who have disbelieved" out of enmity and stubbornness (مَا يُجادِلُ فِي آياتِ اللَّهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا).

It is true that this group may occasionally possess power, numbers, and strength, but "let not their strutting about through the land deceive you!" (فَلا يَغْرُرْكَ تَقَلُّبُهُمْ فِي الْبِلادِ).

For a few days, they may have pomp, show, and clamor, but soon they will vanish like bubbles on the water, or be scattered like ashes before a violent wind.

"Yujadilu" (يُجادِلُ) is derived from the root jadal (جدل), which originally means to twist a rope and tighten it. Later, it was used regarding buildings, armor, and the like. For this reason, the actions of those who stand against one another in debate—each seeking to strengthen the foundations of their own speech to overcome the other—is called "mujadalah" (disputation).

However, it must be noted that "mujadalah" is not always blameworthy in the context of Arabic linguistics (even if it carries that connotation in everyday Persian). Rather, if it is in the path of truth, based on logic, and for the purpose of clarifying realities and guiding the uninformed, it is praiseworthy. But if it relies on baseless arguments and stems from prejudice, ignorance, and pride, intended to deceive others, it is blameworthy. Incidentally, both usages are found in the Holy Quran.

In one place we read: وَ جادِلْهُمْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ: "And dispute with them in a way that is best" (An-Nahl: 125).

Yet in other instances, such as the verse above and the one following it, it is used in the blameworthy sense of disputation.

Regarding "jadal and mujadalah," we have a discussion that you will read in the Key Points.

"Taqallub" (تَقَلُّبُ) is from the root qalb (قلب), meaning to transform or turn over. Here, "taqallub" means exercising control and authority over various regions and lands, ruling and dominating them, as well as traveling to and fro within them.

The aim of the above verse is to remind the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the early Muslims—who were mostly from the deprived classes—not to consider the financial resources and the political or social power of the tyrannical disbelievers as evidence of their righteousness or actual strength. The world remembers many such individuals, and history shows how weak and helpless they were against divine punishments, like withered autumn leaves before a violent gale.

Just as today, the arrogant and oppressive disbelievers engage in a series of efforts, propaganda, conferences, political visits, military maneuvers, and the signing of contracts and treaties with their peers to assert their presence or intimidate the oppressed and deprived of the world, aiming to create a favorable atmosphere for their sinister goals. However, the believers must remain vigilant and not be deceived by these old theatrics, and they must never be intimidated or infatuated.

Therefore, in the next verse, the fate of some of the previous misguided and rebellious nations is described in short and crushing phrases: "The people of Noah denied before them, and the factions after them" (كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَ الْأَحْزابُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ).

The term "Al-Ahzab" (the factions) refers to the people of 'Ad, Thamud, the faction of Pharaoh, Lot, and the like, who are referred to as "Al-Ahzab" in verses 12 and 13 of Surah Sad, where it says: كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَ عادٌ وَ فِرْعَوْنُ ذُو الْأَوْتادِ وَ ثَمُودُ وَ قَوْمُ لُوطٍ وَ أَصْحابُ الْأَيْكَةِ أُولئِكَ الْأَحْزابُ.

Yes, they were factions who joined hands to deny the messengers of Allah, whose call did not align with their illegitimate interests and whims.

Then it adds that they were not content with this much; rather, "every nation plotted against their messenger to seize him," to harass him, imprison him, or kill him (وَ هَمَّتْ كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ بِرَسُولِهِمْ لِيَأْخُذُوهُ).

Again, they did not stop there, "and they disputed by means of falsehood to refute the Truth thereby," insisting on misleading the people (وَ جادَلُوا بِالْباطِلِ لِيُدْحِضُوا بِهِ الْحَقَ) [1]. But these matters did not continue forever, and in due time, "I seized them, and how [terrible] was My penalty!" (فَأَخَذْتُهُمْ فَكَيْفَ كانَ عِقابِ).

The ruins of their cities are visible on your travel routes, and their grim fate and dark end are recorded on the pages of history and in the hearts of the wise. Look and take heed.

[1]: "Liyudhidu" (لِيُدْحِضُوا) is from the root idhad (ادحاض), meaning to nullify and invalidate.

These rebellious disbelievers of Mecca and the oppressive Arab polytheists will have no better end than them, unless they come to their senses and reconsider their ways.

The above verse summarizes the program of the rebellious factions into three parts: "denial and rejection," "plotting to destroy the men of Truth," and "continuous propaganda to mislead the masses."

The Arab polytheists repeated all these programs against the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله; therefore, it is no surprise that the Quran threatens them with the same fate as the previous nations.

The last verse under discussion, in addition to worldly punishment, refers to their punishment in the hereafter, saying: "Thus has the word of your Lord come true against those who disbelieved, that they are the companions of the Fire" (كَذلِكَ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّهُمْ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ).

The apparent meaning of the verse is broad and inclusive, encompassing stubborn disbelievers from all nations, and is not specific to the disbelievers of Mecca—as some commentators have imagined.

It is obvious that the fulfillment of the Lord's decree regarding this people follows their continuous sins and wrongful acts which they performed by their own will. It is strange that some commentators—such as Fakhr al-Razi—have imagined that this verse is evidence for the existence of a forced and mandatory destiny for various nations and the removal of their will and choice. Had sectarian prejudices allowed, a little reflection on these very verses would have made it clear to them that Allah ordains this grim fate for them only after they have walked the paths of oppression and crime by their own feet.

Key Points

1- The Apparent Power-Display of the Disbelievers

Repeatedly in the verses of the Quran, we encounter the statement that deprived believers should never imagine that the vast resources sometimes available to oppressive, tyrannical, and faithless individuals or groups are evidence of their prosperity or a sign of their ultimate victory.

Specifically, to invalidate this notion—which usually occurs to short-sighted individuals who might take material resources as evidence of spiritual righteousness—the Quran turns the pages of the history of previous nations before the eyes of the believers. It points to clear examples, such as the powerful Pharaonic forces in Egypt, the Nimrodites in Babylon, and the peoples of Noah, 'Ad, and Thamud in Iraq, Hijaz, and the Levant, lest the believers who are empty-handed and deprived feel a sense of deficiency or weakness, or become intimidated or discouraged by the "pomp and show" of the faithless oppressors.

Of course, Allah's law is not to immediately punish everyone who commits a wrong, as we read in verse 59 of Surah Al-Kahf: وَ جَعَلْنا لِمَهْلِكِهِمْ مَوْعِداً: "And We appointed for their destruction a fixed time."

Elsewhere He says: فَمَهِّلِ الْكافِرِينَ أَمْهِلْهُمْ رُوَيْداً: "So allow the disbelievers a delay; leave them for a little while" until the end of their affair becomes clear (Surah At-Tariq, verse 17). And in another place it is said: إِنَّما نُمْلِي لَهُمْ لِيَزْدادُوا إِثْماً: "We only extend it for them so that they may increase in sin!" (Al-Imran: 178).

In short, the purpose of these respites is either to establish the proof against the disbelievers, to test the believers, or to increase the sins of those who have closed all paths of return to themselves.

A similar feeling of inferiority in the face of powerful, materialistic, and oppressive countries occurs among some believing nations who are materially underdeveloped; this must be vigorously combated using the same Quranic logic mentioned above.

Furthermore, it must be made clear to them that your deprivation and backwardness are primarily the result of the oppression of those tyrants, and if you break the chains of oppression and captivity, you can compensate for the backwardness through continuous effort and striving.

2- Disputation (Mujadalah) in the Holy Quran

In this Surah, "mujadalah" is mentioned five times, all of which refer to "false disputation" (verses 4, 5, 35, 56, and 69). On this occasion, it is appropriate to have a discussion about disputation from the perspective of the Holy Quran.

"Jadal" (disputation) and "Mira'" (wrangling) are two terms discussed extensively in both Quranic verses and Islamic narrations. In this regard, "firstly," the concept of these two words must be clarified; then the types of disputation (rightful and false) and the signs of each should be explained; and finally, the harms of false disputation and the factors for victory in rightful disputation should be detailed and analyzed.

A- The Concept of "Jadal" and "Mira'"

"Jadal," "Mira'," and "Mukhasamah" are three terms with closely related concepts, yet there are differences between them [1]. "Jadal" originally means twisting a rope, then it was used for twisting the opposing party and conversing to overcome them.

"Mira'" (rhyming with hijab) means conversing about something in which there is "miryah" (doubt).

"Khusumah" and "Mukhasamah" originally mean two people grappling with each other, each grabbing the other's side, and then it was applied to verbal arguments and disputes.

According to the late Allamah Majlisi in Bihar al-Anwar, "jadal" and "mira'" are mostly used in scientific and intellectual matters, while "mukhasamah" is used in worldly affairs.

Also, the difference between jadal and mira' is sometimes stated as: in "mira'," the goal is to show off one's excellence and perfection, while in "jadal," it is to belittle and incapacitate the opponent.

Sometimes it is said: "jadal" is in scientific matters and "mira'" is broader than that.

And sometimes it is said: "mira'" has a defensive aspect against the attacks of the adversary, while "jadal" is both defensive and offensive [1].

B- Rightful and False Disputation

We said that from the usage of this word, especially in the Holy Quran, it is well understood that "jadal" has a broad concept and includes any type of discussion and dialogue between two parties, whether it be for truth or falsehood. In verse 125 of Surah An-Nahl, we read that Allah commands the Prophet of Islam: وَ جادِلْهُمْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ: "And dispute with them in a way that is best." Regarding Abraham عليه السلام in verse 74 of Surah Hud, we read: فَلَمَّا ذَهَبَ عَنْ إِبْراهِيمَ الرَّوْعُ وَ جاءَتْهُ الْبُشْرى‌ يُجادِلُنا فِي قَوْمِ لُوطٍ: "When the fear had left Abraham and the good news had reached him, he began to dispute with Us concerning the people of Lot." These are of the rightful type of disputation.

However, in most cases in the Holy Quran, this term is used for false disputation, as in this Surah (Al-Mu'min), where the word is used five times in this sense.

In any case, discussion, reasoning, and debating the words of others—if intended to clarify the truth, show the way, and guide the ignorant—is a commendable act worthy of appreciation; indeed, in many cases, it is obligatory. The Quran has never opposed enlightening discussion and reasoning to clarify the truth; rather, it has practically established it in many verses.

In many instances, it demands proof and evidence from the opponents, saying: هاتوا برهانكم "Bring your proof" [1]. In many cases, in response to the demand for proof, it has provided various arguments, as we read at the end of Surah Ya-Sin regarding that Arab man who took a decayed bone and asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: مَنْ يُحْيِ الْعِظامَ وَ هِيَ رَمِيمٌ: "Who can give life to these bones while they are decayed?" (Ya-Sin: 78). It provides several proofs for the issue of the Resurrection and Allah's power to revive the dead.

Also, in verse 258 of Surah Al-Baqarah, it reflects the dialogues of Abraham عليه السلام and his crushing arguments against Nimrod, and in verses 47 to 54 of Surah Taha, the arguments of Moses عليه السلام against Pharaoh, which are clear examples of worthy disputation. Similarly, the Quran is full of various arguments that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله presented against idolaters, polytheists, and excuse-seekers.

However, in contrast, it narrates many instances where the supporters of falsehood engaged in ugly disputations to establish their baseless claims, using various sophistries and excuses to invalidate the truth and deceive simple-hearted people. Mockery, ridicule, threats, slander, and denial without proof were the common methods of misguided and rebellious nations against the prophets, while logical reasoning mixed with love and kindness was the method of the divine prophets.

In Islamic narrations, many discussions are found regarding the debates of the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام against opponents. If these discussions and debates were collected, they would form a very thick book (indeed, parts of them have been collected).

Not only the infallible leaders but also their companions and followers, with their encouragement, held debates and disputations with opponents. However, they allowed this only to those who possessed sufficient ability and logical power; otherwise, instead of the front of Truth being strengthened, it would be weakened, and the opponents would become bolder and more stubborn.

Thus, we read in a narration that one of the friends of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام named "Tayyar" (Hamzah ibn Muhammad) says: I said to the Imam, "It has reached me that you dislike people debating."

The Imam عليه السلام replied: "As for someone like you, it is not disliked; one who, when he flies, knows how to land well, and when he lands, knows how to fly well. For whoever is like this, we do not dislike it!" [1]

[1]: All three terms are infinitives of the Bab Mufa'alah. [1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 73, p. 399. [1]: Al-Baqarah 111, Al-Anbiya 24, An-Naml 64, Al-Qasas 75. [1]: Rijal al-Kashshi, p. 298.

This beautiful expression, which is a clear reference to the power of soaring in reasoning and then summarizing and concluding the discussion, shows that individuals must be present in these arenas who possess sufficient mastery over argumentative discussions, lest the weakness of their logic be attributed to the weakness of their school of thought.

C- The Evil Effects of False Disputation

It is true that discussion and dialogue are the keys to solving problems, but this is only when both parties are seekers of truth and searching for the way, or at least if one party enters through stubbornness, the other party is thinking of establishing the truth and reaching reality. However, whenever a dialogue occurs between those who rise in strife and clamor for self-interest, to show superiority over the other, and to impose their own word, the result will be nothing but distancing from the truth, darkness of the heart, and the rooting of hostilities and grudges.

For this reason, Islamic narrations have forbidden mira' and false disputation, and meaningful references to the harms of such disputations have been mentioned in these narrations.

In a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever is concerned for his honor should leave off disputation" [1], because in such discussions, matters gradually lead to disrespect, insults, and even profanity and various ugly and foul words and false attributions.

In another narration from the same Imam عليه السلام, we read: "Beware of wrangling and contention, for they sicken the hearts against brothers and cause hypocrisy to grow upon them!" [1]. This is because such aggression, which is often devoid of correct principles of discussion and reasoning, strengthens the spirit of stubbornness and prejudice in individuals to the point where each wants to use any means—even lies, slander, and disrespect—to overcome the other, and this results in nothing but harboring grudges and nurturing the seeds of hypocrisy in the hearts.

Another great evil of "false disputation" is that both parties become harder and more entrenched in their deviation and error, because each strives to cling to any false argument to prove their point and ignores the truthful words of the opponent as much as possible, or looks at them with disapproval and rejection, and this itself causes the strengthening of error and deviance.

D- The Method of the Best Disputation

In rightful disputation, the goal is not to belittle the opponent or to prove superiority and victory over him; rather, the goal is to influence his thoughts and the depths of his soul. For this reason, the method of the "best disputation" differs from false disputation in everything.

Here, the disputant must use the following means for spiritual influence over the other party, to which interesting references are made in the Holy Quran:

1- He should not insist that the truth be accepted as his own statement; rather, if he can act in such a way that the opponent considers that point to be the result of his own thought, it will be much more effective. In other words, the party should think this idea has bubbled up from within his own being and is the child of his own soul, so that he becomes attached to it!

The reason why the Holy Quran presents many important truths—from monotheism and the negation of polytheism to other issues—in the form of interrogation, for example, after mentioning the proofs of monotheism it says: أَ إِلهٌ مَعَ اللَّهِ "Is there a deity with Allah?" (An-Naml: 60), might be this very point.

2- One must avoid anything that triggers the opponent's sense of stubbornness. The Holy Quran says: وَ لا تَسُبُّوا الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ: "And do not insult those they invoke other than Allah" (Al-An'am: 108), lest they become stubborn and insult the Great Allah.

3- In discussions, one must observe the utmost fairness toward everyone and every group so that the party feels the speaker is truly seeking to clarify the realities. For instance, when the Quran speaks of the harms of wine and gambling, it does not ignore the minor material and economic benefits that accrue to a group; it says: قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَ مَنافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَ إِثْمُهُما أَكْبَرُ مِنْ نَفْعِهِما: "Say, 'In them is great sin and [yet, some] benefit for people. But their sin is greater than their benefit.'" This manner of speaking certainly leaves a deeper impact on the listener.

4- One should not retaliate against evils and grudges in kind; rather, one should take the path of love, kindness, and forgiveness. This "countering with the opposite" in such cases has an extraordinary effect in softening the hearts of stubborn enemies, as the Holy Quran says: ادْفَعْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ فَإِذَا الَّذِي بَيْنَكَ وَ بَيْنَهُ عَداوَةٌ كَأَنَّهُ وَلِيٌّ حَمِيمٌ: "Repel [evil] by that [deed] which is better; and thereupon the one whom between you and him is enmity [will become] as though he was a devoted friend!" (Fussilat: 34).

In summary, whenever we carefully examine the dialogues of the prophets with tyrannical and stubborn enemies reflected in the Quran, and the dialogues of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the infallible Imams عليهم السلام in dealing with enemies during ideological discussions, we find instructive lessons that express the most precise psychological issues that smooth and pave the way for influencing others.

Specifically, the late Allamah Majlisi narrates a detailed tradition from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله in this regard, which includes a long debate of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله with five groups: Jews, Christians, Atheists, Dualists, and Arab polytheists, where He compels them to accept and submit with an attractive and captivating tone—an instructive debate that can serve as a model for our own debates [1].

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, Word 362. [1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, "Chapter on Wrangling and Contention," Hadith 1. [1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 9, p. 257 onwards.

Ayahs

ٱلَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ ٱلْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُۥ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ رَبَّنَا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ رَّحْمَةً وَعِلْمًا فَٱغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ سَبِيلَكَ وَقِهِمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٧)
Those who bear the Throne, and those who are around it, celebrate the praise of their Lord and have faith in Him, and they plead for forgiveness for the faithful: ‘Our Lord! You embrace all things in mercy and knowledge. So forgive those who repent and follow Your way and save them from the punishment of hell. (7)
رَبَّنَا وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ ٱلَّتِى وَعَدتَّهُمْ وَمَن صَلَحَ مِنْ ءَابَآئِهِمْ وَأَزْوَٰجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٨)
Our Lord! Admit them into the Gardens of Eden, which You have promised them, along with whoever is righteous among their forebears, their spouses and their descendants. Indeed You are the All-mighty, the All-wise. (8)
وَقِهِمُ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ ۚ وَمَن تَقِ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمْتَهُۥ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٩)
Save them from the ills [of the Day of Retribution]; and whomever You save from the ills that day, You will have had mercy upon him, and that is the great triumph.’ (9)

Commentary

The Bearers of the Divine Throne Constantly Pray for the People of Faith

The tone of the preceding verses indicated that they were revealed at a time when Muslims were in the minority and deprived, while their enemies were at the peak of power, possessing authority and abundant resources.

Following that, the verses under discussion were in truth revealed to give glad tidings to the true believers: that you are never alone, and you should never feel like strangers; the bearers of the Divine Throne and His nearest and greatest angels are in harmony with you, loving you and supporting you. They constantly pray for you and seek your victory from Allah in this world and the next. This is the greatest source of encouragement for the believers of today, that day, and tomorrow.

He says: "Those [angels] who bear the Throne and those around it exalt [Allah] with praise of their Lord and believe in Him and ask forgiveness for those who have believed" (الَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ الْعَرْشَ وَ مَنْ حَوْلَهُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ وَ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا).

Their speech is that they say: "Our Lord, You have encompassed all things in mercy and knowledge (You are aware of the sins of Your servants and are Merciful toward them). O Allah! Forgive those who have repented and followed Your way and protect them from the punishment of Hellfire" (رَبَّنا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ رَحْمَةً وَ عِلْماً فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تابُوا وَ اتَّبَعُوا سَبِيلَكَ وَ قِهِمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ‌).

This statement, on one hand, tells the believers: you are not the only ones who worship Allah and proclaim His praise and glory; before you, the nearest angels of Allah and the bearers and circumambulators of His Throne proclaim His praise and glory.

On the other hand, it warns the disbelievers: whether you believe or not is of no consequence; Allah has no need for anyone's faith. His angels proclaim His praise and glory to an extent beyond imagination, and even then, He has no need for their praise and glory either.

And thirdly, it informs the believers: you are not alone in this world—even if you are in the minority in your living environment—the most powerful unseen forces of the universe and the bearers of the Lord's Throne are your supporters and well-wishers. They constantly ask the Great Allah to include you in His vast forgiveness and mercy, to overlook your errors, and to protect you from the punishment of Hellfire.

Once again in this verse, we encounter the subject of the "Throne" (Arsh), where mention is also made of its bearers and the angels surrounding it. Although we have discussed this in the commentary of various Surahs thus far [1], we will provide further explanation in the "Key Points" section.

In the following verse, continuing the prayers of the bearers of the Throne for the believers, it adds:

"Our Lord, and admit them to gardens of perpetual residence which You have promised them" (رَبَّنا وَ أَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ الَّتِي وَعَدْتَهُمْ‌).

"And whoever was righteous among their fathers, their spouses and their offspring" (وَ مَنْ صَلَحَ مِنْ آبائِهِمْ وَ أَزْواجِهِمْ وَ ذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ‌) [2].

[1]: Including Volume 6, page 204 (under verse 54 of Surah Al-A'raf), Volume 9, page 25 (under verse 7 of Surah Hud), and Volume 2, page 200 (under verse 255 of Surah Al-Baqarah).

[2]: The phrase "wa man salaha" is conjoined to the pronoun in the phrase "wa adkhilhum."

"Indeed, it is You who is the Exalted in Might, the Wise" (إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ‌).

This verse, which begins with "Our Lord" (Rabbana), is the pleading supplication of the bearers of the Throne and the near angels of Allah, who rely once again on His attribute of Lordship (Rububiyyah) to draw His grace. They seek not only salvation from Hellfire for the believers but also entry into the eternal gardens of Paradise for them. They request this not only for the believers themselves but also for their fathers, spouses, and children who followed their path, seeking aid from His attributes of Might and Power.

The Divine promise referred to in these verses is the same promise that Allah has repeatedly given to the people through the Prophets.

The division of believers into two groups reflects the reality that one group is in the first rank, striving perfectly in following Allah's commands, while the other group is not at that level but is included in the angels' prayers due to their relative following of the first group and their association with them.

Then, in their fourth prayer for the believers, they say: "And protect them from evil consequences. And he whom You protect from evil consequences that Day—You have given him mercy" (وَ قِهِمُ السَّيِّئاتِ وَ مَنْ تَقِ السَّيِّئاتِ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمْتَهُ‌).

And finally, they conclude their prayer with this meaningful sentence: "And that is the great attainment" (وَ ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ‌).

What attainment or salvation is greater than this: that a person's sins are forgiven, punishment and evils are distanced from him, he is included in Divine mercy, he steps into His eternal Paradise, and his beloved relatives are joined with him?

Key Points

1- The Fourfold Prayers of the Bearers of the Throne

A question arises here: what is the difference between these four prayers? Are some of them repetitive?

However, with a little reflection, it becomes clear that each one addresses a separate matter.

First, they request forgiveness and the cleansing of the believers from the effects of sin. This, besides being a prerequisite for attaining every great blessing, is desirable in itself. What bounty is higher than for a person to feel purified, that his Lord is pleased with him, and he is pleased with his Lord? Indeed, regardless of the issue of Paradise and Hell, this feeling is the most honorable and magnificent sensation for the servants of Allah.

In the second stage, they request to keep them away from the punishment of Hell, which is the most important means of psychological peace for them.

In the third stage, they request Paradise, not only for the believers themselves but for their relatives, whose presence by their side is a source of soul-soothing peace and joy of the heart.

And since, besides Hell, there are other significant and noteworthy distresses on the scene of Resurrection—such as the terror of the Gathering, disgrace before the creation, the length of the Reckoning, and the like—in their other prayer, they ask Allah to keep the believers away from every kind of evil and unpleasantness on that Day, so they may enter eternal Paradise with peace of mind, honor, and dignity.

2- The Etiquette of Supplication

In these verses, the bearers of the Divine Throne teach the believers the way and method of supplication.

First, clinging to the name of the Lord (Rabbana).

Then, praising Him with His attributes of Beauty and Majesty and seeking help from His station of infinite Mercy and Knowledge (وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ رَحْمَةً وَ عِلْماً).

Finally, entering into the supplication, asking for matters in order of importance, and coupling them with the conditions that provide the ground for acceptance (فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تابُوا وَ اتَّبَعُوا سَبِيلَكَ‌).

Then, concluding the prayer by mentioning His attributes of Beauty and Majesty and re-appealing to His Mercy.

It is noteworthy that the bearers of the Throne rely on five of the most important Divine attributes in this prayer: His Lordship, Mercy, Power, Knowledge, and Wisdom.

3- Why do the Prayers Begin with "Rabbana"?

A study of the verses of the Holy Quran shows that the "Auliya Allah" (Friends of Allah), including Prophets, angels, and righteous servants, would begin their speech with "Rabbana" (Our Lord) or "Rabbi" (My Lord) when supplicating.

Adam says: رَبَّنا ظَلَمْنا أَنْفُسَنا: "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves."

Noah petitions: رَبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَ لِوالِدَيَ‌: "My Lord, forgive me and my parents."

Abraham says: رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لِي وَ لِوالِدَيَّ وَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْحِسابُ‌: "Our Lord, forgive me and my parents and the believers the Day the account is established."

Joseph عليه السلام says: رَبِّ قَدْ آتَيْتَنِي مِنَ الْمُلْكِ‌: "My Lord, You have given me something of sovereignty."

And Moses عليه السلام says: رَبِّ بِما أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ فَلَنْ أَكُونَ ظَهِيراً لِلْمُجْرِمِينَ‌: "My Lord, for the favor You have bestowed upon me, I will never be an assistant to the criminals."

Solomon عليه السلام says: هَبْ لِي مُلْكاً لا يَنْبَغِي لِأَحَدٍ مِنْ بَعْدِي‌: "My Lord, grant me a kingdom such as will not belong to anyone after me."

Jesus عليه السلام petitions: رَبَّنا أَنْزِلْ عَلَيْنا مائِدَةً مِنَ السَّماءِ: "Our Lord, send down to us a table [spread with food] from the heaven" (Al-Ma'idah: 114).

And the great Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله petitions: رَبِّ أَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنْ هَمَزاتِ الشَّياطِينِ: "My Lord, I seek refuge in You from the incitements of the devils" (Al-Mu'minun: 97).

And the believers, according to the final verses of Surah Al-Imran, repeat this expression several times, including saying: رَبَّنا ما خَلَقْتَ هذا باطِلًا: "Our Lord, You did not create this [vast heaven and earth] aimlessly!"

From these expressions, it is well understood that the best supplication is that which begins with the matter of the Lordship of the Creator. It is true that the blessed name "Allah" is the most comprehensive of God's names, but since requesting from His grace-filled presence is proportionate to the matter of Lordship—a Lordship that begins from the first moments of human existence and continues until the end of his life and beyond, drowning man in Divine favors—calling upon Allah by this name at the beginning of prayers is more appropriate and worthy than any other name [1].

[1]: "Tafsir al-Kabir" by Fakhr Razi, under the verses discussed.

4- What is the Throne of Allah?

We have said many times that our words, which were established to express the characteristics of our limited and insignificant lives, cannot accurately express the greatness of Allah or even the greatness of His large creations. For this reason, by using the metaphorical meanings of these words, we draw a shadow of all that greatness.

Among the words that have met this fate is the word "Arsh" (Throne), which literally means "ceiling" or "a high-legged throne," as opposed to "Kursi," which means a short-legged throne. Subsequently, this word has been used regarding the throne of Allah's power as the "Throne of the Lord."

Regarding what is meant by the Throne of Allah and what meaning this word is a metaphor for, commentators, traditionists, and philosophers have said much.

Sometimes the Throne has been interpreted as "the infinite knowledge of the Lord."

And sometimes as "the ownership and sovereignty of Allah."

And sometimes as each of His attributes of perfection and majesty, because each of these attributes expresses the greatness of His station, just as the throne of monarchs is a sign of their greatness.

Indeed, Allah possesses a Throne of Knowledge, a Throne of Power, a Throne of All-Mercifulness, and a Throne of All-Compassion.

According to these three interpretations, the concept of "Throne" refers back to the attributes of the Pure Essence of the Lord, not to another external existence.

Some narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام also confirm this meaning, such as the hadith narrated by Hafs ibn Ghiyath from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

They asked the Imam عليه السلام about the interpretation of "His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth" (وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ‌). He said: "It refers to His knowledge" [1]. In another hadith from the same Imam عليه السلام, he interpreted "Arsh" as the knowledge He has made the Prophets aware of, and "Kursi" as the knowledge He has made no one aware of [2]. Meanwhile, some other commentators, inspired by other narrations, have interpreted "Arsh" and "Kursi" as two magnificent entities among the creations of the Lord.

Among them, some have said: the Throne refers to the entirety of the universe.

And sometimes they have said the entirety of this earth and heaven is contained within the Kursi; rather, the heaven and earth compared to the Kursi are like a ring in a vast desert, and the Kursi compared to the Arsh is also like a ring in a vast desert.

And sometimes "Arsh" has been applied to the hearts of Prophets, Successors, and perfect believers, as it is stated in a hadith: "Indeed, the heart of the believer is the Throne of the All-Merciful" (ان قلب المؤمن عرش الرحمن) [3]. It is also narrated in a Hadith Qudsi:

"My heaven and My earth do not encompass Me, but the heart of My believing servant encompasses Me" (لم يسعنى سمائى و لا ارضى و وسعنى قلب عبدى المؤمن) [4].

However, to understand the reality of the meaning of the Throne—of course, as far as human discernment allows—the best way is to carefully examine its usage in the Holy Quran:

In many verses of the Quran, this expression is seen: "Then He established Himself upon the Throne" (ثُمَّ اسْتَوى‌ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ‌) [1] (after the creation of the world was completed). In some verses, following this expression, the phrase "He directs the affair" (يدبر الامر) or expressions indicating the knowledge and management of the Lord are seen.

In some other verses of the Quran, descriptions for the Throne are seen, such as the description "Great" (Azim), where He says: "And He is the Lord of the Great Throne" (وَ هُوَ رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ‌) (At-Tawbah: 129).

Sometimes the talk is of the bearers of the Throne, like the verse under discussion.

And sometimes the talk is of the angels who surround the Throne: "And you will see the angels surrounding the Throne" (وَ تَرَى الْمَلائِكَةَ حَافِّينَ مِنْ حَوْلِ الْعَرْشِ‌) (Az-Zumar: 75).

And sometimes it says "Allah's Throne was upon the water" (وَ كانَ عَرْشُهُ عَلَى الْماءِ).

From the sum of these expressions and other expressions found in Islamic narrations, it can be well concluded that "Arsh" is applied to various meanings, although they share a common root.

One of the meanings of Arsh is the station of "governance, ownership, and management of the universe," because even in common usage, the word Arsh is used as a metaphor for a ruler's control over the affairs of his country. We say: "So-and-so's throne was toppled," meaning his power collapsed.

Another meaning of Arsh is "the entirety of the universe," because all of it is a sign of His greatness. Sometimes "Arsh" is used to mean the "upper world" and "Kursi" to mean the "lower world."

And sometimes "Arsh" is used to mean the "metaphysical world" and Kursi to mean the entirety of the material world, including the earth and heaven, as stated in Ayat al-Kursi: "His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth."

And since the creations and knowledge of Allah are not separate from His Pure Essence, sometimes Arsh is applied to "Allah's knowledge."

And if the pure heart of believing servants is called "the Throne of the All-Merciful," it is because it is the place of the knowledge of His Pure Essence and a sign among the signs of His greatness and power.

Therefore, in each case, one must understand from the context which of its meanings is intended for Arsh, but in any case, they all share the fact that Arsh expresses the magnitude and greatness of Allah.

In the verse under discussion, which speaks of the bearers of the Divine Throne, it is possible that Arsh refers to Allah's governance and His management of the universe, and the bearers of the Throne are the executors of His sovereignty and management.

It is also possible that it means the entirety of the universe or the metaphysical world, and its bearers are the angels upon whose shoulders the pillars of the management of this world rest by Allah's command.

[1]: Al-A'raf 54, Yunus 3, Ar-Ra'd 2, Al-Furqan 59, As-Sajdah 4, Al-Hadid 4. [1] and [2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 58, p. 28 (Hadith 46 and 47). [3] and [4]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 58, p. 39.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُنَادَوْنَ لَمَقْتُ ٱللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ مِن مَّقْتِكُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ إِذْ تُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى ٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ فَتَكْفُرُونَ (١٠)
It will be proclaimed to the faithless: ‘Surely, Allah’s outrage [towards you] is greater than your outrage towards yourselves, as you were invited to faith, but you disbelieved.’ (10)
قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ أَمَتَّنَا ٱثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا ٱثْنَتَيْنِ فَٱعْتَرَفْنَا بِذُنُوبِنَا فَهَلْ إِلَىٰ خُرُوجٍ مِّن سَبِيلٍ (١١)
They will say, ‘Our Lord! Twice did You make us die, and twice did You give us life. We admit our sins. Is there any way out [from this plight]?’ (11)
ذَٰلِكُم بِأَنَّهُۥٓ إِذَا دُعِىَ ٱللَّهُ وَحْدَهُۥ كَفَرْتُمْ ۖ وَإِن يُشْرَكْ بِهِۦ تُؤْمِنُوا۟ ۚ فَٱلْحُكْمُ لِلَّهِ ٱلْعَلِىِّ ٱلْكَبِيرِ (١٢)
[They will be told,] ‘This [plight of yours] is because, when Allah was invoked alone, you would disbelieve, but if partners were ascribed to Him you would believe. So the judgment belongs to Allah, the All-exalted, the All-great.’ (12)

Commentary

We Confess Our Sins; Is There a Way to Make Amends?!

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the encompassment of Divine "Mercy" toward the believers. In the verses under discussion, the focus shifts to the nature of the Lord's "Wrath" upon the faithless, so that through the principle of contrast, both subjects become clearer.

First, He says: "Indeed, those who disbelieve will be addressed on the Day of Resurrection: 'The hatred and wrath of the Lord toward you is greater than your hatred and wrath toward yourselves, for you were invited to faith but you chose the path of disbelief'" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يُنادَوْنَ لَمَقْتُ اللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ مَقْتِكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ إِذْ تُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى الْإِيمانِ فَتَكْفُرُونَ).

Who addresses them in this manner? It appears that the angels of punishment raise this cry to reproach, rebuke, and disgrace them, while the angels of mercy are always ready to honor and respect the people of faith and righteousness.

The possibility has also been suggested that this call comes from some disbelievers toward others, but the first meaning seems more appropriate. In any case, this is a call given on the Day of Judgment, and the subsequent verses are clear evidence of this meaning.

"Maqt" (مقت) linguistically means intense hatred and enmity. This verse shows that although the faithless develop a severe hatred toward themselves, Allah's wrath toward them is even more severe.

But what is meant by the disbelievers' wrath and enmity toward themselves? There are two interpretations here:

First, that they committed the greatest enmity against themselves in the world, for they rejected the callers to monotheism. Not only did they turn away from the lamps of Divine guidance, but they also shattered them. Is there any enmity toward oneself more severe than a person closing the path to eternal bliss and opening the doors of everlasting punishment for the sake of following carnal desires and utilizing the fleeting goods of this world?

According to this interpretation, the phrase "إِذْ تُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى الْإِيمانِ فَتَكْفُرُونَ" (when you were invited to faith and you disbelieved) is actually an explanation of the nature of their wrath and enmity toward themselves.

The second interpretation is that it refers to their enmity and wrath toward themselves in the Hereafter. When they witness the result of their deeds there, they become deeply regretful and distressed; their moans and roars rise, and out of intense distress, they bite their own hands: "And the Day the wrongdoer will bite on his hands" (Al-Furqan: 27).

They wish they were dust: "And the disbeliever will say, 'Oh, I wish I were dust!'" (An-Naba: 40).

And they writhe in intense agony. Because they have acquired sharp vision according to "so your sight today is sharp" (Qaf: 22), and all inner realities and secrets have become manifest according to "The Day when secrets will be put to the test" (At-Tariq: 9), and the records of deeds have been opened according to "And when the pages are spread out" (At-Takwir: 10), and they have been called to audit themselves according to "Sufficient is yourself against you this Day as an accountant" (Al-Isra: 14), they condemn themselves severely and loathe and flee from themselves with their entire being.

It is here that the call is made: "Allah's enmity and wrath upon you is even greater than this, for the callers to truth and the messengers of Allah invited you to faith, but you chose the path of disbelief and persisted in it."

According to this interpretation, the phrase "إِذْ تُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى الْإِيمانِ فَتَكْفُرُونَ" explains the reason for the greatness of Allah's wrath toward them. [1] Both interpretations are suitable, but the first seems more appropriate in several respects.

In any case, upon witnessing the circumstances of the Resurrection and becoming aware of Allah's wrath toward them, the criminals wake up from their long sleep of negligence and think of a remedy. They "say: 'Our Lord! You have made us die twice and given us life twice, and through these deaths and lives, we have understood everything. Now we confess our sins; is there any way to exit (from Hell and return to the world to make amends for what has passed)?!'" (قالُوا رَبَّنا أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَ أَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ فَاعْتَرَفْنا بِذُنُوبِنا فَهَلْ إِلى‌ خُرُوجٍ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ).

Yes, there the veils of pride and negligence are cast aside, and the eye of truth-seeking man is opened; thus, he has no choice but to confess his sin.

In this world, they insisted on denying the Resurrection and mocked the Prophets in this regard. However, when they see their successive deaths and lives, no room remains for denial. Their emphasis on the repetition of death and life is perhaps because they want to say: "O Allah, Who is the Master of death and life, You have the power to return us to the world once more so that we may strive to make amends."

Two Deaths and Two Lives

Regarding what is meant by "making us die twice" and "giving us life twice," commentators have mentioned several interpretations, among which three possibilities are noteworthy:

  1. The two deaths refer to death at the end of one's lifespan and death at the end of the Barzakh (the intermediate world). The two instances of giving life refer to the life in Barzakh and the life on the Day of Resurrection.

To explain: when a person dies, they acquire another type of life known as the life of Barzakh—the same life that the martyrs possess according to "rather, they are alive with their Lord, receiving provision" (Al-Imran: 169); the same life that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams عليهم السلام possess, hearing our greetings and responding; and also the same life that the rebellious and tyrants like the people of Pharaoh possess, being punished morning and evening according to "The Fire; they are exposed to it morning and evening" (Ghafir: 46).

On the other hand, we know that at the end of this world, at the first blast of the Trumpet, not only humans but all angels and the souls of the dead who are in "imaginal bodies" (qālab-e mithālī) will die according to "and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will fall dead" (Az-Zumar: 68), and none shall remain except the Pure Essence of Allah (of course, the death and life of angels and souls are not like the death and life of us humans, as explained under verse 68 of Surah Az-Zumar).

Thus, we have one physical life and one Barzakh life. At the end of our lifespan, we die from the physical life, and at the end of this world, we die from the Barzakh life. We also have two lives following these two deaths: the Barzakh life and the life of the Day of Resurrection.

Here, the question arises: besides these two lives, we have a third life in this world, and we also had a state of death before entering this world because we were previously dead matter. Thus, there would be three lives and deaths.

The answer to this question becomes clear by looking closely at the verse itself. The state of death before the life of this world (when we were dust) is "mawt" (death), not "imātah" (making one die). As for the life in this world, although it is an instance of "ihyā" (giving life), the Quran does not refer to it in the above verse because this life was not much of a lesson for the disbelievers. What caused their awakening and confession of sin was first the Barzakh life and then the life at the Resurrection (reflect on this).

  1. The two lives refer to being brought to life in the grave for certain questions and being brought to life at the Resurrection. The two deaths refer to death at the end of one's lifespan and death in the grave.

Therefore, a group of commentators has considered this verse as evidence for temporary life in the grave. Regarding the nature of life in the grave—whether it is physical, Barzakh-related, or semi-physical—there are discussions that do not belong here.

  1. The first death refers to the state before human existence in the world, as he was previously dust. Therefore, the first life is the life of this world, the second death is at the end of this world, and the second life is at the Resurrection.

Those who chose this interpretation also argued using verse 28 of Surah Al-Baqarah, which says: "How can you disbelieve in Allah when you were lifeless and He brought you to life; then He will cause you to die, then He will bring you to life [again], and then to Him you will be returned."

However, the verse under discussion speaks of two instances of "imātah" (making one die), whereas in the verse of Surah Al-Baqarah, there is one "mawt" (state of death) and one "imātah" (making one die). [1] Among these interpretations, the first seems more appropriate.

It is also noteworthy that some proponents of reincarnation have tried to use this verse to argue for the repeated life and death of humans and the return of souls to new bodies in this world. However, the above verse is one of the living proofs refuting reincarnation, as it limits death and life to two parts, whereas proponents of reincarnation speak of numerous and successive deaths and lives, believing that a human soul may inhabit new bodies and new embryos several times and return to this world.

In any case, it goes without saying that the response to this request of the disbelievers—to come out of Hell and return to the world to supposedly make amends for their dark past—is negative. Its negativity is so clear that it is not even mentioned in the verses under discussion. Only in the following verse is a point mentioned that serves as its reason. It says: "That is because when Allah alone was invoked, you disbelieved, but if partners were associated with Him, you believed" (ذلِكُمْ بِأَنَّهُ إِذا دُعِيَ اللَّهُ وَحْدَهُ كَفَرْتُمْ وَ إِنْ يُشْرَكْ بِهِ تُؤْمِنُوا).

Yes, wherever there was talk of monotheism, purity, piety, and the command of Truth, you would frown; and wherever there was talk of disbelief, hypocrisy, polytheism, and pollution, you would become happy and joyful. For this reason, you have no fate other than this.

Here, the question arises: how does this response relate to the request to return to the world?

The expressions in the verse indicate the reality that such actions of theirs were not occasional or temporary, but rather they were always like this. Therefore, if they were to return, they would continue the same program, and this faith and submission in the Resurrection is out of necessity, not genuine. Furthermore, their past beliefs, actions, and intentions necessitate that they remain eternally in Hell; thus, returning to the world is impossible.

In any case, this situation is specific to those in whom disbelief, polytheism, and sin have taken root—those who, according to the Quran, were disgusted by hearing the name of the One God and rejoiced at hearing the names of idols: "And when Allah is mentioned alone, the hearts of those who do not believe in the Hereafter shrink with aversion, but when those [worshipped] other than Him are mentioned, immediately they rejoice" (Az-Zumar: 45).

This is not exclusive to the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; in our time, too, there are blind-hearted people who flee from faith, monotheism, and piety, and wherever the scent of disbelief, hypocrisy, and corruption is smelled, they turn toward it.

Therefore, in some narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, this verse has been interpreted regarding the issue of "Wilayah," where some become upset upon hearing it and happy upon hearing the names of their opponents (it is clear that this interpretation is of the type of applying a general principle to a specific instance, not restricting the entire concept of the verse to this instance).

At the end of the verse, to make these polytheistic, dark-hearted people despair forever, it adds: "So the judgment belongs to Allah, the Most High, the Grand" (فَالْحُكْمُ لِلَّهِ الْعَلِيِّ الْكَبِيرِ).

There is no judge, seeker of justice, or redresser in this court except Him. And because He is the Most High and the Grand, He is neither overcome by anyone, nor do recommendations affect Him, nor can His judgment be overcome through ransom, fine, or the help of this or that. He is the Absolute Ruler, all are subject to His command, and there is no way to escape His judgment.

Key Point: A Prayer Far from Being Granted!

This is not the first time in the verses of the Quran that we encounter the request of the people of Hell or the disbelievers to return once more to this world, only to be met with a negative response. This subject has been raised many times in the verses of the Holy Quran.

In Surah Ash-Shura, verse 44, we read: After witnessing the punishment, the wrongdoers say, "Is there any way to return?"

And in Surah Az-Zumar, verse 58, regarding sinful and faithless humans, it says: When they see the Divine punishment, they say: "If only I had another turn [at life] so I could be among the doers of good" (أَوْ تَقُولَ حِينَ تَرَى الْعَذابَ لَوْ أَنَّ لِي كَرَّةً فَأَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ).

And in Al-Mu'minun, verse 107, it is quoted from these same people: "Our Lord, bring us out of it, and if we return [to evil], then we are indeed wrongdoers" (رَبَّنا أَخْرِجْنا مِنْها فَإِنْ عُدْنا فَإِنَّا ظالِمُونَ).

A group also makes such a request to Allah when they see the angels of death, saying: "My Lord, send me back that I might do righteousness in that which I left behind" (Al-Mu'minun: 99-100).

But everywhere they are met with "Kalla" (By no means! It is impossible) or similar expressions.

In this way, the Quran says: The life of this world is an experience that is never repeated for a single person. So, one must discard the naive thought that if we face a severe reaction after death, the way to return and make amends is open. No, it is never so.

The reason is clear: in the law of evolution and its process, regression and return are impossible. Just as it is impossible according to this law for a newborn to return to the mother's womb—whether it has completed its evolutionary period in the womb or is miscarried prematurely—return is impossible. Death is like a second birth and a transition from this world to another; there, too, such a return is impossible.

Furthermore, emergency awakenings are never evidence of genuine awakening. When the factors causing them subside, forgetfulness returns and the same actions are repeated. As we have tested this meaning in the life of this very world regarding many people: in the straits of life, they cling to Allah's grace and enter through the door of repentance, but as soon as the storm subsides, everything is consigned to oblivion.

[1]: According to the first interpretation, "idh" (إذ) is "zarfiyyah" (adverbial) and relates to "maqtukum anfusakum." According to the second interpretation, it is "ta'liliyyah" (causal) and relates to "maqt Allah." It is noteworthy that regarding the two instances of "maqt" in the above verse, there are four possibilities, each chosen by some commentators: first, that the time for both is the Resurrection; second, that the time for both is the world; third, that the first maqt is in the world and the second in the Hereafter; and fourth, the reverse. But best of all, according to the above commentary, is that the first relates to the Hereafter and the second to the world, or both relate to the Hereafter (reflect carefully).

[2]: Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that the above verse refers to "Raj'ah" (the Return), but given the generality of the verse regarding all disbelievers and the lack of established generality of Raj'ah for all of them, this interpretation is debatable.

Ayahs

هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَيُنَزِّلُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ رِزْقًا ۚ وَمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ إِلَّا مَن يُنِيبُ (١٣)
It is He who shows you His signs and sends down provision for you from the sky. Yet no one takes admonition except those who return penitently [to Allah]. (13)
فَٱدْعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (١٤)
So supplicate Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him, though the faithless should be averse. (14)
رَفِيعُ ٱلدَّرَجَـٰتِ ذُو ٱلْعَرْشِ يُلْقِى ٱلرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِۦ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ لِيُنذِرَ يَوْمَ ٱلتَّلَاقِ (١٥)
Raiser of ranks, Lord of the Throne, He casts the Spirit of His command upon whomever of His servants that He wishes, that he may warn [people] of the Day of Encounter. (15)

Commentary

Call Upon Allah Alone, Even if the Disbelievers Dislike It

These verses are, in fact, an argument for the matters that passed in the previous verses in the form of advice, counsel, threats, and warnings; they are an argument for Monotheism (Tawhid), the Oneness of Allah, His Lordship, and the negation of polytheism and idol worship.

First, it says: "He is the One who shows you His signs" (هُوَ الَّذِي يُرِيكُمْ آياتِهِ).

These are the same signs within the horizons and within the souls that have filled the entire universe, and the wondrous patterns manifest upon the doors and walls of existence—patterns such that "he who does not reflect, to him they are but paintings on a wall!" Then, proceeding to mention one of these signs, it adds: "and sends down for you provision from the sky" (وَ يُنَزِّلُ لَكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ رِزْقاً).

The life-giving drops of rain, the sunlight that revitalizes all beings, and the air which is the source of life for all animals and plants—all descend from the sky. We know that these three elements are the most vital means of life and existence, and everything else is secondary to them.

Some commentators have interpreted "the sky" to mean the "Unseen World" (Alam al-Ghayb) and "the earth" to mean the "Manifest World" (Alam al-Shuhud), considering the descent of divine provision from the sky to the earth as its manifestation from the Unseen to the Manifest. However, besides this interpretation being contrary to the literal meaning of the verse, there is no necessity for it.

It is true that revelation and the numerous verses that serve as food for the soul descend from the "sky" of the Unseen and meaning, while rain and sunlight, which are food for the body, come from the physical sky, and both are harmonious with one another. However, one should not imagine that the term "signs" (Ayat) in the verses under discussion refers to a more general concept or specifically to legislative signs (revelation), because the expression "He shows you His signs" (yureekum ayatih) is repeatedly applied in the Holy Quran to the monotheistic signs of the physical universe. For instance, toward the end of this very Surah al-Mu'min, after mentioning Allah's blessings such as livestock and ships, He says:

وَ يُرِيكُمْ آياتِهِ فَأَيَّ آياتِ اللَّهِ تُنْكِرُونَ: "And He shows you His signs; which, then, of the signs of Allah will you deny?" (Mu'min: 81), as well as other verses.

Fundamentally, the expression "yureekum" (He shows you/presents to you) is consistent with cosmic signs (Ayat Takwini), whereas regarding legislative signs (Ayat Tashri'i), other expressions such as "He sent revelation" or "It came to you" are usually seen.

In any case, there is no evidence for the view held by some earlier commentators and some prominent contemporary commentators who have taken "signs" here to mean "legislative signs" or a combination of both legislative and cosmic.

It is also noteworthy that among the vast signs present in the heavens, the earth, and the human being, the Quran here points specifically to the issue of human provision (rizq). This is because it constitutes the greatest part of man's mental preoccupations, and sometimes, to increase provision or escape from financial straits, he reaches out to idols. The Holy Quran states: all provision is in the hand of Allah, and idols can do nothing.

At the end of the verse, it adds: despite all these signs across the expanse of existence, blind eyes and hearts upon which a veil has been cast see nothing; "none reminds himself except he who turns [to Allah] in repentance" and washes his heart and soul from impurities.

(وَ ما يَتَذَكَّرُ إِلَّا مَنْ يُنِيبُ).

In the next verse, it draws the following conclusion: "Now that it is so, call upon Allah, making the religion pure for Him" (فَادْعُوا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ). Rise up and, with the axe of faith, strike at the idols of the polytheists, and erase them all from the pages of your thought, culture, and society.

Of course, this action of yours will severely upset the stubborn and prejudiced disbelievers, but do not allow fear or dread into yourselves; purify your faith "even if the disbelievers dislike it" (وَ لَوْ كَرِهَ الْكافِرُونَ).

In an environment where the majority consists of misguided idolaters, Monotheism (Tawhid) initially appears terrifying to them, just like the rising of the sun among a colony of bats. But pay no heed to these ignorant and fleeting reactions; move forward decisively, and wave the banner of Monotheism and sincerity everywhere.

The next verse describes Allah with several of His important attributes, saying: "He is the Exalter of Ranks" (رَفِيعُ الدَّرَجاتِ).

He raises the ranks of the righteous servants, as stated in verse 11 of Surah al-Mujadilah: يَرْفَعِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجاتٍ: "Allah will raise those who have believed among you and those who were given knowledge, by degrees."

Even among the Prophets, He has given virtue and preference to those who endured more trials and brought the station of sincerity to a higher level: تِلْكَ الرُّسُلُ فَضَّلْنا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ (Baqarah: 253).

He has placed humans as His successors and representatives on earth, and according to their merits, He has preferred some over others: "وَ هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَكُمْ خَلائِفَ الْأَرْضِ وَ رَفَعَ بَعْضَكُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجاتٍ" (An'am: 165).

If the previous verse called for sincerity in religion, here it says that Allah raises your ranks in proportion to your sincerity; indeed, He is the "Exalter of Ranks."

All this is if we interpret "Rafee'" as "the One who raises" (Raafi'). However, some have said "Rafee'" here means "Exalted." Therefore, "Rafee' al-Darajat" refers to the high and sublime attributes of Allah: He is exalted in His knowledge, and He is exalted in His power. All His attributes of perfection and beauty are so elevated and high that the high-flying bird of human intellect and knowledge can never reach their peak.

Since "Rafee'" in the language carries both meanings, the above verse can be interpreted in either way. However, since the context of the verses relates to the issue of reward and granting high ranks to righteous servants, the first meaning seems more appropriate. Nevertheless, in our view—as we consider it permissible to use a word in more than one meaning—there is no obstacle to combining both interpretations, especially regarding the verses of the Quran whose words possess vast and expansive concepts.

Then it adds: "He is the Owner of the Throne" (ذُو الْعَرْشِ).

The entire universe is under His power and sovereignty, and His rule is undisputed. This itself is proof that the determination of the servants' ranks according to their merits is in the hand of His power.

Since we discussed the "Throne" (Arsh) in detail in the previous verse, we see no need for repetition here.

In the third description, it says: "He sends the Spirit by His command upon whom He wills of His servants" (يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلى‌ مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ).

This "Spirit" (Ruh) is the Quran, the station of Prophethood, and revelation, which is the source of life for hearts, just like the soul in the human body.

His power on one hand, and His being the Exalter of Ranks on the other, necessitate that He announces the program of legislation and duty through revelation. And what a beautiful expression He has used for it: the term "Spirit"—a spirit that is the source of life, movement, activity, struggle (jihad), and progress.

Although commentators have offered various possibilities in explaining the meaning of "Spirit" here, the evidence present in the verse, as well as verse 2 of Surah al-Nahl which says: يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلى‌ مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ أَنْ أَنْذِرُوا أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاتَّقُونِ, and also verse 52 of Surah al-Shura which addresses the Prophet of Islam and describes the descent of the Quran, faith, and the Spirit upon him: وَ كَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ رُوحاً مِنْ أَمْرِنا ما كُنْتَ تَدْرِي مَا الْكِتابُ وَ لَا الْإِيمانُ—all of these prove that "Spirit" in such contexts means revelation, the Quran, and divine duties.

The expression "by His command" (min amrih) indicates that if the Angel of Revelation is commissioned to convey this Spirit, he too speaks from Allah's side, not his own.

And the expression "upon whom He wills of His servants" (ala man yasha'u min 'ibadih) does not mean that He gives the gift of revelation to someone without account, for His will is identical to His wisdom. Whomever He finds worthy of this station, He includes in this command, just as we read in verse 124 of Surah al-An'am: اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِرسالَتَهُ: "Allah knows best where to place His message."

And if we see in some narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام that "Spirit" in the above verse is interpreted as "the Holy Spirit" (Ruh al-Qudus) and identified specifically with the Prophet and the Infallible Imams, it does not contradict what we have said. This is because the "Holy Spirit" is that sacred spirit and high spiritual station that exists perfectly in the Prophets and Infallible Imams, and often a ray of it manifests in the existence of other individuals; whenever the "grace of the Holy Spirit" assists them, they utter extraordinary words or perform great divine tasks. [1] It is interesting that in the preceding verses, the talk was of the descent of rain and physical provisions, and here the talk is of the descent of revelation and spiritual provision.

Now let us see what the purpose of sending the Holy Spirit upon the Prophets is. And for what goal do they pursue this path full of ups and downs, long and arduous?
