In the final sentence of the above verse, this question is answered: "The goal is to warn people of the Day of Meeting" (لِيُنْذِرَ يَوْمَ التَّلاقِ).

The day when servants meet their Lord through inner vision. The day when the people of the past and the future all meet together. The day of the meeting of the leaders of truth and falsehood with their followers. The day of the meeting of the oppressed (mustad'afin) and the arrogant (mustakbirin). The day of the meeting of the oppressor and the oppressed. The day of the meeting of humans and angels. And finally, the day of man's meeting with his deeds, words, and actions, and with the Court of Allah's Justice.

Yes, the goal of all heavenly books and divine programs is to warn the servants of the "Day of the Great Meeting," and what a wondrous name has been chosen for the Resurrection in this verse: "The Day of Meeting" (Yawm al-Talaq).

[1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 1 under verse 87 of Surah al-Baqarah.

Ayahs

يَوْمَ هُم بَـٰرِزُونَ ۖ لَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَىْءٌ ۚ لِّمَنِ ٱلْمُلْكُ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۖ لِلَّهِ ٱلْوَٰحِدِ ٱلْقَهَّارِ (١٦)
The day when they will emerge [from their graves], nothing about them will be hidden from Allah. ‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’ (16)
ٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ ۚ لَا ظُلْمَ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (١٧)
‘Today every soul shall be requited for what it has earned. There will be no injustice today. Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning.’ (17)

Commentary

The Day of Meeting!

These verses and the few verses following them provide an explanation and commentary for the "Day of Meeting" (Yawn al-Talaq), which is one of the names of the Day of Resurrection mentioned in the preceding verses.

In these two verses, several characteristics of the Resurrection are described, each more soul-shaking than the other.

First, He says: "The Day of Meeting is the day when they shall all come forth [and be exposed]" (يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ).

It is a day when all veils and curtains are cast aside.

On one hand, material obstacles such as mountains will be removed, and according to the Quran, the earth will become a qa’an safsafa (a level plain without any depression or elevation) (Ta-Ha: 106).

On the other hand, all human beings will raise their heads from their graves and emerge.

Thirdly, the inner secrets of everyone will be revealed: "The Day when secrets are tested [and laid bare]" (At-Tariq: 9).

And the earth will cast out what is within it: "And the earth throws out its burdens" (Al-Zalzalah: 2).

Fourthly, the records of deeds will be opened and their contents revealed: "And when the scrolls are laid open" (At-Takwir: 10).

Fifthly, the deeds that man has sent forward will be manifested before him: "The Day when man will see what his hands have sent forth" (An-Naba: 40).

Sixthly, matters that man insisted on hiding will become apparent: "Nay, but that has become manifest to them which they used to conceal before" (Al-An'am: 28).

Seventhly, the limbs of the human body and even the earth upon which deeds were performed will begin to disclose and recount the truths: "That Day it will declare its information" (Al-Zalzalah: 4).

In short, human beings will appear on that great scene with their entire being, existence, and identity, and nothing will remain hidden: "And they shall all appear before Allah" (Ibrahim: 21).

What a strange and terrifying scene it is! To understand what a commotion will be raised there, it is enough to imagine if such a scene were established in this world for a single moment—where the internal and external, the private and public of all humans became one. What a clamor would arise among the people? And how the bonds between people would be severed?!

Indeed, such is the nature of that world, and one must live in such a way that he has no fear of his secrets being exposed; he should perform his deeds and conduct himself such that if they were revealed in public today, he would not be anxious.

In the second description of that day, He adds: "Nothing of them will be hidden from Allah" (لا يَخْفى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْ‌ءٌ).

In this world and today as well, nothing is hidden from the All-Knowing and All-Powerful Allah. Fundamentally, for One whose existence is infinite and in whose Holy Essence no limitation finds a path, the manifest and the hidden, the unseen and the witnessed, are the same.

So why does the Quran mention the above sentence as an explanation and commentary for the phrase "يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ"?

The reason is clear: because the manifestation of things on that day carries greater emphasis. In a place where others also become aware of each other's secrets, the matter regarding Allah requires no debate or discussion.

The third characteristic of that day is the absolute sovereignty of the Lord, as He says in the continuation of this same verse: "On that day it will be said: To whom belongs the kingdom and sovereignty today?" (لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ‌).

And in response, they say: "To Allah, the One, the Irresistible" (لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ).

Who raises this question? And who gives that answer?

The verse does not explicitly mention this.

Some have said the question is raised by the Lord, and the answer is given by all believers and disbelievers [^1].

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses being discussed.

Some have also said: both the question and the answer are from Allah [^1], and some believe a divine herald raises this question openly and answers it himself.

However, it appears that this question and answer are not voiced by a specific individual; it is a question raised by the Creator and the created, angel and human, believer and disbeliever, and by every atom of existence and the very walls of the universe without exception. All of them answer it through the "language of their state" (zaban-e hal). That is, wherever you look, the effects of His sovereignty are visible, and upon whatever you gaze, the signs of His Irresistibility are manifest.

If you listen to the murmur of every atom, it says: "لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ" (To whom belongs the kingdom?), and in response, you hear from it: "لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ" (To Allah, the One, the Irresistible).

We see a very small example of this in this world: sometimes upon entering a house, a city, or a country, we feel the presence and power of a specific individual everywhere. It is as if everything says the owner and ruler here is such-and-forth person; even the walls bear witness to this fact.

Of course, today as well, Allah's ownership governs the entire universe, but on the Day of Resurrection, it finds a new manifestation and appearance. There, there is no news of the rule of tyrants, nor of the drunken shouts of the arrogant (taghutis).

No news of diabolical forces is heard, nor is any trace of the superficial display of power by Satan and his armies to be seen.

The fourth characteristic of that day is that it is a day of reward and punishment, as He says in the next verse: "This day every soul shall be recompensed for what it earned" (الْيَوْمَ تُجْزى‌ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ‌).

[^1]: Al-Mizan, under the verses being discussed.

Indeed, that manifestation and exposure, and that all-encompassing knowledge of Allah, and His sovereignty, ownership, and irresistibility, are all clear proofs of this great, hope-inspiring, and fear-inducing reality.

The fifth characteristic is what He adds in the next phrase: "No injustice shall there be this day" (لا ظُلْمَ الْيَوْمَ‌).

How could injustice possibly occur when injustice is either due to ignorance—while He has all-encompassing knowledge of everything—or due to weakness—while He is the Irresistible, the Owner, and the Ruler over all things? Thus, how could any injustice be committed in the Divine Presence on that day, especially since that day is the Day of God's Judgment, not a day of freedom for people's testing.

The sixth and final characteristic is the speed of the reckoning of the servants' deeds, as He says at the end of the verse: "Indeed, Allah is swift in taking account" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ‌).

The speed of His reckoning there is such that it is stated in a Hadith: "Indeed, Allah the Almighty reckons all creatures in the duration of a blink of an eye" [^1].

Fundamentally, by accepting the embodiment of deeds (tajassum al-a'mal) and the permanence of the effects of good and evil, the issue of reckoning is a resolved matter. Do the machines in this world that record scores while operating require time for auditing?

The repetition of the expression "Swift in taking account" in various verses of the Quran is perhaps for the purpose that some devilish characters do not whisper to simple-minded individuals: "Is the auditing of all creatures for deeds they performed over thousands of years possible with such ease?"

Beyond this, this expression is a warning to all human beings that on that day, no respite will be given to criminals, unlike the respite given in this world to a criminal or murderer for their case to be processed over several months or years.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under verse 202 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

Ayahs

وَأَنذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔازِفَةِ إِذِ ٱلْقُلُوبُ لَدَى ٱلْحَنَاجِرِ كَـٰظِمِينَ ۚ مَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ يُطَاعُ (١٨)
Warn them of the Approaching Day, when the hearts will be at the throats, choking with suppressed agony, [and] the wrongdoers will have no sympathizer, nor any intercessor who might be heard. (18)
يَعْلَمُ خَآئِنَةَ ٱلْأَعْيُنِ وَمَا تُخْفِى ٱلصُّدُورُ (١٩)
He knows the treachery of the eyes, and what the breasts hide. (19)
وَٱللَّهُ يَقْضِى بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ لَا يَقْضُونَ بِشَىْءٍ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ (٢٠)
Allah judges with justice, while those whom they invoke besides Him do not judge by anything. Indeed it is Allah who is the All-hearing, the All-seeing. (20)

Commentary

The Day When Souls Reach the Throats!

These verses continue the description of the Day of Resurrection. In truth, these verses outline seven additional characteristics of the Resurrection and its terrifying, horrific events—events that should make every believer reflect deeply:

First, He says: "And warn them of the Approaching Day" (وَ أَنْذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ الْآزِفَةِ).

"Azifah" (آزفة) literally means "that which is near." What a remarkable naming this is; instead of "Yawm al-Qiyamah" (The Day of Resurrection), "Yawm al-Azifah" is used so that the heedless do not say: "There is still a long time until the Resurrection; do not occupy your minds with it, for it is a distant promise!" If we look closely, the entire lifespan of this world is nothing more than a fleeting moment compared to the lifespan of the Hereafter. Since no date for it has been disclosed by Allah to anyone—not even to the Prophets—one must always be prepared to meet it.

The second description is: "When hearts reach the throats due to extreme horror and fear" (إِذِ الْقُلُوبُ لَدَى الْحَناجِرِ).

When a person is placed in severe straits, they feel as if their heart is being uprooted, as if it wants to leap out of their throat. The Arabs express this state with the phrase "the hearts reached the throats" (بلغت القلوب الحناجر). Perhaps the English equivalent would be "his soul reached his lips," otherwise, it is clear that the physical heart—the center of blood circulation—never actually moves from its place to reach the throat.

It is also possible that "heart" (قلب) is a metaphor for "soul" (جان), meaning the soul has reached the throat, as if the spirit is gradually leaving the body and only a little of it remains.

In any case, such horror and anxiety arise from the precise Divine reckoning, the fear of disgrace in the presence of all creation, and the entrapment in a painful punishment from which there is no escape, that it cannot be described by any words.

In the third description, He says: "Their existence becomes filled with grief and sorrow, yet they are unable to express it" (كاظمين).

"Kazim" (كاظم) is derived from the root "kazm" (كظم), which originally means closing the mouth of a water skin that is full of water. Subsequently, it has been applied to those who are filled with rage and anger but do not express it for various reasons.

If a person is afflicted with soul-crushing sorrow and grief but can scream, they might find some relief. But alas, there is no place for screaming or wailing there. That is the stage for the manifestation of all hidden secrets, the threshold of Truth's judgment, the presence of the Lord's justice, and the gathering of all creatures. What use is a scream?!

The fourth description is: "For the wrongdoers, there is no devoted friend" (ما لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ).

That group of fair-weather friends who, like flies around sweets, surrounded them during their time of power—those who presented themselves as loyal, self-sacrificing companions or hereditary servants through flattery and sycophancy—are all preoccupied with their own affairs and pay no heed to others. Indeed, on that day, there is neither a friend for man nor a sympathizer to whom he can vent his grievances.

In the fifth description, He says: "Nor an intercessor whose intercession is accepted" (وَ لا شَفِيعٍ يُطاعُ).

This is because the intercession of the true intercessors, such as the Prophets and the Awliya (Saints), is also by the permission of the Lord. Thus, He strikes a blow of nullification against the fancy of the idolaters who considered idols to be their intercessors before Allah.

In the sixth stage, He mentions one of the attributes of Allah, which serves as a description of the nature of the Resurrection: "He knows the treachery of the eyes and what the breasts conceal" (يَعْلَمُ خائِنَةَ الْأَعْيُنِ وَ ما تُخْفِي الصُّدُورُ) [1]. Yes, the God who is aware of the secret movements of the eyes and the secrets within the breasts will judge and administer justice among the creatures on that day. With this precise knowledge and awareness of His, the day of the sinners will be black and dark.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that when he was asked about the meaning of this verse, he said: "Have you not seen a man looking at something while pretending he is not looking at it? These are the treacherous glances!" [2]

Indeed, these glances—whether directed at the honors (women) of people or other matters that are forbidden to look at—are not hidden from Allah, from whose knowledge not even an atom's weight in the heavens or the earth is concealed: "Not an atom's weight escapes Him in the heavens or in the earth" (Saba, 3).

In another Hadith, it is mentioned: One of the companions of the Prophet was sitting in the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله beside one of the fierce opponents of Islam. After that opponent received a guarantee of safety from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and left, the companion asked: "Why did you not give a signal so that we could stand up and strike his neck before he received safety from you?" The Messenger of Allah said: "Indeed, a Prophet does not have treacherous glances!" [1]

Of course, the treachery of the eyes takes various forms: sometimes it is in the form of stolen glances and voyeurism toward unrelated women, and sometimes in the form of signals with the eye intended to belittle or find fault with others, or signals that are a prelude to conspiracies and satanic plots.

Truly, if a person believes in such a precise reckoning on the Day of Resurrection—where even glances, thoughts, and their underlying motives are all questioned and scrutinized—the highest level of piety (Taqwa) will be revived within them. What an effect this faith in the Hereafter, Divine surveillance, and the Resurrection's reckoning has on the cultivation of human souls!

It is said: One of the great scholars, after completing his studies in the seminary of Najaf, requested a piece of advice and an exhortation from his teacher as he was about to return to his country. The teacher said: "After all these efforts, my final counsel is the Word of Allah; never forget this verse: 'Does he not know that Allah sees?' (Al-Alaq, 14)."

Yes, from the perspective of a true believer, the entire universe is the presence of Allah, and all actions are performed in His presence. This sense of shame and awareness of His presence is sufficient to keep one away from sins.

[1] In the phrase "يَعْلَمُ خائِنَةَ الْأَعْيُنِ", there are two possibilities regarding the grammatical structure: First, that "khainah" (خائنة) has an infinitive meaning, signifying "treachery" (like 'kadhibah' and 'laghiyah' which mean falsehood and vanity). The second possibility is that it is a case of placing the attribute first, originally being "al-a'yun al-khainah" (the treacherous eyes), making it an "active participle" (ism fa'il). [2] Tafsir al-Safi, under the verse in question. [1] Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 8, pp. 47-57 (summarized).

In the seventh description of the Resurrection, which is also presented as a description of Allah, He says: "And Allah judges with truth" (وَ اللَّهُ يَقْضِي بِالْحَقِ).

"And those they invoke besides Him do not judge at all" (وَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ لا يَقْضُونَ بِشَيْ‌ءٍ).

Yes, on that day, the station of judgment belongs exclusively to Allah, and He judges only by the truth. This is because judging by injustice arises either from ignorance and lack of awareness—whereas He encompasses everything, even the secrets of the conscience—or from weakness and need, all of which are far removed from His Holy Essence.

Furthermore, this sentence is a proof for the Oneness of the One to be worshipped (Tawhid al-Ma'bud), for the one worthy of worship is the one in whose hand the final judgment lies. As for the idols, which have no benefit in this world and are not the reference for judgment in the Resurrection, how could they possibly be worthy of worship?

It is also noteworthy that "judging with truth" from Allah has a broad meaning that includes both the realm of creation (Takwin) and the realm of legislation (Tashri'). Just as the term "Qada" (decree/judgment) is used in the verses of the Quran in both cases. In one place we read: "And your Lord has decreed that you worship none but Him" (Al-Isra, 23); this is His legislative judgment.

And in another place He says: "When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, 'Be,' and it is" (Al-Imran, 47).

Finally, as an emphasis on what has passed in these verses, He concludes the discourse with this sentence: "Indeed, Allah is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ).

Rather, seeing and hearing in the true sense of the word—meaning the presence of all things heard and seen before Him—is exclusive to the Pure Essence of Allah. This is an emphasis on His knowledge and awareness of everything, and His judging with truth, for as long as one is not the Absolute All-Hearing and All-Seeing, one cannot be a Judge of Truth.

Ayahs

۞ أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَانُوا۟ هُمْ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً وَءَاثَارًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَمَا كَانَ لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَاقٍ (٢١)
Have they not travelled through the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them? They were greater than them in might and with respect to the effects [they left] in the land. But then Allah seized them for their sins, and they had no defender against Allah [’s punishment]. (21)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانَت تَّأْتِيهِمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَكَفَرُوا۟ فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ قَوِىٌّ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٢٢)
That was because their apostles used to bring them manifest proofs, but they defied [them]. So Allah seized them. Indeed He is all-strong, severe in retribution. (22)

Commentary

Behold the Painful End of the Oppressive Predecessors!

Since the method of the Quran in many verses is that after mentioning generalities regarding sensitive and fundamental issues, it blends them with specific and tangible matters, taking the hand of humanity and leading them to observe past and present events to investigate these issues, the verses under discussion—following the previous discourses on the Origin and the Resurrection, the precise accounting of deeds, and the sinister consequences of rebellion and sin—invite the people to study the states of those who came before, including the condition of Pharaoh and his people.

First, It says: "Have they not traveled through the land and observed how was the end of those who were before them?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ كانُوا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ‌).

This is not a compiled history whose authenticity and correctness might be doubted; this is a living history that cries out with a wordless tongue. The ruins of the palaces of the evildoers, the disaster-stricken cities of the rebellious, the decayed bones of those sleeping in the heart of the earth, and the palaces buried in the ground are crushing sentences that describe the true history without any addition or omission! Then It adds: "They were superior to them in strength and in the traces [they left] in the land" (كانُوا هُمْ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً وَ آثاراً فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

They possessed such powerful governments, vast armies, and brilliant material civilizations that the lives of the polytheists of Mecca are nothing more than a plaything in comparison! The expression "superior to them in strength" (أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً) reflects both their political and military power, as well as their economic and occasionally scientific power.

The expression "traces in the land" (آثاراً فِي الْأَرْضِ‌) may refer to their great agricultural progress, as stated in verse 9 of Surah Ar-Rum: أَ وَ لَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ كانُوا أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً وَ أَثارُوا الْأَرْضَ وَ عَمَرُوها أَكْثَرَ مِمَّا عَمَرُوها: "Have they not traveled through the earth and observed how was the end of those before them? They were greater than them in power, and they plowed the earth and built it up more than these have built it up."

It may also refer to the sturdy buildings and structures that some ancient nations built in the heart of mountains and in the middle of plains, as the Quran says regarding the people of 'Ad: أَ تَبْنُونَ بِكُلِّ رِيعٍ آيَةً تَعْبَثُونَ وَ تَتَّخِذُونَ مَصانِعَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَخْلُدُونَ‌: "Do you build on every high place a monument for vain delight? And do you take for yourselves fortresses [and palaces] as if you might live forever?" (Ash-Shu'ara: 128-129).

At the end of the verse, It recounts the fate of these rebellious nations in one short sentence: "So Allah seized them for their sins, and they had not against Allah any protector [to save them from Divine punishment]" (فَأَخَذَهُمُ اللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَ ما كانَ لَهُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ مِنْ واقٍ‌).

Neither their large numbers prevented the Divine punishment, nor did their power, majesty, and countless wealth.

Repeatedly in the verses of the Quran, "seizing" (اخذ) has appeared with the meaning of punishing; this is because, to carry out a heavy punishment, one first detains the party and then penalizes them.

In the next verse, It explains what was previously mentioned in brief and says: "That painful Divine punishment was because their messengers used to come to them with clear proofs, but they disbelieved" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كانَتْ تَأْتِيهِمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَكَفَرُوا).

It was not that they were heedless or unaware, or that their disbelief and sin resulted from a lack of completed proof (itmam al-hujjah); no, their messengers came to them continuously (as understood from the expression "كانت تاتيهم"), but they never submitted to the Divine commands. They broke the lamps of guidance, turned their backs on their compassionate messengers, and sometimes even killed them.

"So Allah seized them [and punished them]" (فَأَخَذَهُمُ اللَّهُ‌).

"Indeed, He is All-Strong, Severe in punishment" (إِنَّهُ قَوِيٌّ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ‌).

In the place of mercy, He is the "Most Merciful of the merciful," and in the place of wrath and anger, He is the "Severest of punishers."

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (٢٣)
Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs and a manifest authority (23)
إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ وَقَـٰرُونَ فَقَالُوا۟ سَـٰحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ (٢٤)
to Pharaoh, Haman and Korah, but they said, ‘A magician and a mendacious liar.’ (24)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ مِنْ عِندِنَا قَالُوا۟ ٱقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَبْنَآءَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ وَٱسْتَحْيُوا۟ نِسَآءَهُمْ ۚ وَمَا كَيْدُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ (٢٥)
So when he brought them the truth from Us, they said, ‘Kill the sons of the faithful who are with him, and spare their women.’ But the stratagems of the faithless only go awry. (25)
وَقَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ ذَرُونِىٓ أَقْتُلْ مُوسَىٰ وَلْيَدْعُ رَبَّهُۥٓ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يُبَدِّلَ دِينَكُمْ أَوْ أَن يُظْهِرَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلْفَسَادَ (٢٦)
And Pharaoh said, ‘Let me slay Moses, and let him invoke his Lord. Indeed I fear that he will change your religion, or bring forth corruption in our land.’ (26)
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰٓ إِنِّى عُذْتُ بِرَبِّى وَرَبِّكُم مِّن كُلِّ مُتَكَبِّرٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُ بِيَوْمِ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٢٧)
Moses said, ‘I seek the protection of my Lord and your Lord from every arrogant one who does not believe in the Day of Reckoning.’ (27)

Commentary

Let Me Kill Moses!!

Following the reference in the previous verses to the painful fate of past nations, these verses begin to detail one of those narratives, pointing specifically to the story of Moses, Pharaoh, Haman, and Korah.

It is true that the story of Moses and Pharaoh is repeated in many Surahs of the Quran, but an analysis shows that it is never truly repetitive; rather, in each instance, it is viewed from a specific angle. In the current discussion, the primary objective is to introduce the story of the "Believer from the Family of Pharaoh," while the rest serves as a background for this significant event.

First, He says: "We sent Moses with Our 'Signs' and a 'clear authority'" (وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِآيَاتِنَا وَسُلْطَانٍ مُبِينٍ).

"To Pharaoh, Haman, and Korah, but they said: [He is] a sorcerer, a great liar!" (إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَهَامَانَ وَقَارُونَ فَقَالُوا سَاحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ).

Regarding the difference between "Signs" (آيات) and "clear authority" (سلطان مبين), various interpretations have been offered by commentators:

Some consider "Signs" to refer to clear proofs and "clear authority" to refer to miracles.

Others consider "Signs" to refer to the verses of the Torah and "clear authority" to refer to miracles.

Some have also suggested that "Signs" includes all types of Moses' miracles, while "clear authority" refers to his prominent miracles, such as the staff and the white hand, which resulted in his manifest dominance over Pharaoh.

Others have interpreted "Signs" as miracles and "clear authority" as the overwhelming power and divine influence of Moses, which prevented his murder and the silencing of his call.

None of these interpretations have a definitive textual basis. What can be inferred from other Quranic verses is that "clear authority" usually means a clear and firm proof that leads to manifest dominance. As we read in Surah An-Naml, verse 21, in the story of Solomon and the hoopoe: Solomon says, "I do not see the hoopoe; why is he absent? I will surely punish him with a severe punishment or slaughter him, unless he brings me a 'clear authority' (سُلْطَانٍ مُبِينٍ) [a clear reason] for his absence."

And in Surah Al-Kahf, verse 15, we read: "Why do they not bring a 'clear authority' (بِسُلْطَانٍ مُبِينٍ) [clear proof] for them [their deities]?" Furthermore, "Signs" (آيات) is frequently used in the Quran to mean miracles.

Therefore, the expression "Signs" refers to "Moses' miracles," and "clear authority" refers to the powerful logic and crushing proofs that Moses presented against the Pharaohs.

In any case, Moses was equipped both with rational logic and with extraordinary acts that signaled his connection to the metaphysical world. However, the stance of the Pharaonic tyrants against him was nothing but accusing him of sorcery and lying.

The accusation of sorcery was in response to the signs and miracles, and the charge of lying was in response to the logical arguments. This itself is another witness for the interpretation we chose for these two expressions.

Indeed, the leaders of disbelief have always invented such false labels to neutralize the proofs of truth presented by men of God—examples of which we see in abundance with our own eyes today.

It is noteworthy that the names of three individuals are mentioned in this verse, each representing a symbol:

"Pharaoh": the symbol of rebellion, tyranny, and the rule of oppression and injustice.

"Haman": the manifestation of devilry and satanic schemes.

"Korah": the manifestation of the rebellious and exploitative wealthy man who hesitated at nothing to preserve his wealth.

Thus, Moses عليه السلام was commissioned to end the oppression of tyrannical rulers, the devilry of treacherous politicians, and the transgression of arrogant wealthy elites, and to build a society based on justice and equity in political, cultural, and economic terms. However, those whose illegitimate interests were at stake rose in fierce resistance.

The next verse recounts part of their satanic plans, saying: "When the truth came to them from Us, instead of seizing it as an opportunity, they rose to oppose it and said: 'Kill the sons of those who have believed with him and keep their women alive (for captivity and service)!'" (فَلَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ مِنْ عِنْدِنَا قَالُوا اقْتُلُوا أَبْنَاءَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ وَاسْتَحْيُوا نِسَاءَهُمْ).

This expression shows that the issue of killing male children and keeping daughters alive was not limited to the period before the birth of Moses عليه السلام; rather, it was repeated after his uprising and prophethood. Verse 129 of Surah Al-A'raf also supports this claim, where the Children of Israel said to Moses عليه السلام: "We have been harmed before you came to us and after you have come to us" (أُوذِينَا مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تَأْتِيَنَا وَمِنْ بَعْدِ مَا جِئْتَنَا).

The Children of Israel said this after the Pharaonic conspiracy to kill the children of the believers.

In any case, this is a sinister and continuous plan of satanic governments: to destroy active forces and keep inactive forces alive for exploitation. It is no surprise that this plan was implemented both before the birth of Moses among the Children of Israel—who were slaves in the hands of the Pharaohs—and after the uprising of Moses عليه السلام. This was a counter-revolutionary move to severely suppress the forces of the Children of Israel so they could never stand tall.

However, at the end of the verse, the Quran adds: "The plot of the disbelievers is only in error"—they are arrows fired into the darkness of ignorance and misguidance that hit a stone (وَمَا كَيْدُ الْكَافِرِينَ إِلَّا فِي ضَلَالٍ).

Since they never believe that disasters will overtake them, it is the Divine Will that the forces of truth shall ultimately prevail over the forces of falsehood.

The conflict and struggle between Moses عليه السلام and his followers on one side, and Pharaoh and his supporters on the other, intensified. Many events occurred during this time which the Quran omits at this point in the discussion. To reach a specific goal that we will soon discover, it moves to the point where, when matters became critical, Pharaoh decided to kill Moses عليه السلام to prevent the progress of his revolutionary movement. However, it seems his advisors and assembly (Mala') opposed this.

The Quran says: "Pharaoh said: 'Let me kill Moses, and let him call upon his Lord [to save him]!'" (وَقَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ ذَرُونِي أَقْتُلْ مُوسَىٰ وَلْيَدْعُ رَبَّهُ).

From this expression, it can be inferred that the majority of the advisors who were preventing the killing of Moses—or at least some of them—argued that, given Moses' extraordinary deeds, he might utter a curse and his God might send a punishment upon them. But the arrogant Pharaoh says: "I will kill him, come what may!" Of course, it is not clear what the real motive of the entourage and advisors was in this prevention. There are many possibilities here, all of which might be correct simultaneously:

First: Fear of the potential punishment of the Lord.

Second: Fear that after being killed, Moses would be shrouded in a halo of sanctity as a martyr and hero, and his religion would gain many believers and supporters. This is especially likely if this event occurred after the story of Moses' contest with the sorcerers and his amazing, extraordinary victory over them. Apparently, this is the case, because in his first encounter with Pharaoh, Moses had shown his two great miracles (the staff and the white hand), which led Pharaoh to call him a sorcerer and invite him to a contest with the assembly of sorcerers, hoping he could defeat Moses that way. Thus, he was waiting for the promised day.

Therefore, there is no reason for Pharaoh to have decided to kill Moses during that interval, or to have been in terror of the changing of the religion and creed of the Egyptian people [^1].

[^1]: In Tafsir al-Mizan, it is stated that verse 36 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara, "They said: 'Put him and his brother off [for a while]'" (قَالُوا أَرْجِهْ وَأَخَاهُ), is evidence that there was a group preventing Pharaoh from killing Moses. However, an examination of the verses related to the story of Moses shows that at that time, there was no decision to kill him; the only goal was to clarify their truthfulness or falsehood. The decision to kill came after the event of the sorcerers and Moses' influence deep into the hearts of a group of Egyptian people, which threw Pharaoh into severe terror.

In short, they believed that Moses personally was an "event," but if he were killed under those circumstances, he would turn into a "movement"—a great and passionate movement that would be very difficult to control.

Some other members of Pharaoh's entourage, who were not pleased with him, preferred Moses to remain alive and keep Pharaoh's mind occupied so they could live in peace and engage in exploitation away from Pharaoh's eyes. This is a perennial pattern: the entourage of kings always prefers the king's mind to be occupied with something so they can freely secure their illegitimate interests. Sometimes they even provoked foreign enemies to remain safe from the "King's" scrutiny!

Then, to justify the decision to kill Moses, Pharaoh mentions two reasons to his entourage: one has a so-called religious and spiritual aspect, and the other a worldly and material one. He says: "I fear that he will change your religion! And overturn the religion of your ancestors!" (إِنِّي أَخَافُ أَنْ يُبَدِّلَ دِينَكُمْ).

"Or that he will cause corruption to appear in the land" (أَوْ أَنْ يُظْهِرَ فِي الْأَرْضِ الْفَسَادَ). If I remain silent, Moses' creed will quickly penetrate the depths of the Egyptian people's hearts, and the sacred religion of idolatry—which protects your nationality and interests—will be replaced by a monotheistic religion against you! And if I remain silent today and take action against Moses after some time, he will have gained many supporters, and a severe conflict will arise, causing bloodshed, corruption, and unrest throughout the country. Therefore, it is in our best interest to kill him as soon as possible.

Of course, "religion" from the perspective of Pharaoh's thought was nothing but the worship of himself or other idols—a creed designed to drug and fool the people, and a means to sanctify the tyrannical rule of that bloodthirsty man! And "corruption," in his view, was the emergence of an anti-arrogance revolution to free the enslaved masses, erase the traces of idolatry, and revive monotheism.

Tyrants and corrupters have always resorted to these two false excuses to justify their crimes and fight against men of God—examples of which we see with our own eyes in various corners of the world today.

Now let us see what reaction Moses عليه السلام, who was apparently present in that assembly, showed. The Quran says in the next verse: "Moses said: 'I seek refuge in my Lord and your Lord from every arrogant one who does not believe in the Day of Account!'" (وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ إِنِّي عُذْتُ بِرَبِّي وَرَبِّكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ مُتَكَبِّرٍ لَا يُؤْمِنُ بِيَوْمِ الْحِسَابِ).

Moses spoke these words with a decisiveness and confidence born of strong faith and his reliance on the Pure Essence of the Lord, showing that he harbored no fear of such a threat.

This statement by Moses عليه السلام clearly shows that individuals possessing these two characteristics are considered dangerous: "arrogance" and "lack of faith in the Day of Resurrection"—and one must seek refuge in Allah from such people!

"Arrogance" causes a person to see nothing but himself and his own thoughts, to call God's signs and miracles "sorcery," to label reformers as "corrupters," and to consider the advice of friends and associates as "conservatism" and "weakness of soul." And "lack of faith in the Day of Account" causes there to be no accountability in his plans and actions, leading him to challenge even the unlimited power of the Lord with his own very insignificant power and to go to war against His prophets, because he believes there is no reckoning.

Now let us see where this threat of Pharaoh eventually led. The following verses pull back the curtain on this matter and clarify how Moses was saved from the clutches of that proud, arrogant man.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ رَجُلٌ مُّؤْمِنٌ مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَكْتُمُ إِيمَـٰنَهُۥٓ أَتَقْتُلُونَ رَجُلًا أَن يَقُولَ رَبِّىَ ٱللَّهُ وَقَدْ جَآءَكُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ وَإِن يَكُ كَـٰذِبًا فَعَلَيْهِ كَذِبُهُۥ ۖ وَإِن يَكُ صَادِقًا يُصِبْكُم بَعْضُ ٱلَّذِى يَعِدُكُمْ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ كَذَّابٌ (٢٨)
Said a man of faith from Pharaoh’s clan, who concealed his faith, ‘Will you kill a man for saying, ‘‘My Lord is Allah,’’ while he has already brought you manifest proofs from your Lord? Should he be lying, his falsehood will be to his own detriment; but if he is truthful, there shall visit you some of what he promises you. Indeed Allah does not guide someone who is a transgressor and liar. (28)
يَـٰقَوْمِ لَكُمُ ٱلْمُلْكُ ٱلْيَوْمَ ظَـٰهِرِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَمَن يَنصُرُنَا مِنۢ بَأْسِ ٱللَّهِ إِن جَآءَنَا ۚ قَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ مَآ أُرِيكُمْ إِلَّا مَآ أَرَىٰ وَمَآ أَهْدِيكُمْ إِلَّا سَبِيلَ ٱلرَّشَادِ (٢٩)
O my people! Today sovereignty belongs to you, and you are dominant in the land. But who will save us from Allah’s punishment should it overtake us?’ Pharaoh said, ‘I just point out to you what I see [to be advisable for you], and I guide you only to the way of rectitude.’ (29)

Commentary

Would someone be killed for inviting others toward Allah?!

From here begins another segment of the history of Musa عليه السلام and Pharaoh, which is mentioned in the Holy Quran only in this Surah. It is the story of the "Believer of the People of Pharaoh," who was a close relative of Pharaoh. He accepted Musa’s عليه السلام call to monotheism but did not disclose his faith, as he saw it as his duty to provide calculated support for Musa عليه السلام. When he observed that Musa’s عليه السلام life was in grave danger due to Pharaoh’s intense rage, he courageously stepped forward and, with his influential words, thwarted the plot to assassinate him.

In the first verse, it says: "And a believing man from the family of Pharaoh who concealed his faith said, 'Do you kill a man [merely] because he says, "My Lord is Allah"?'" (وَ قالَ رَجُلٌ مُؤْمِنٌ مِنْ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَكْتُمُ إِيمانَهُ أَ تَقْتُلُونَ رَجُلًا أَنْ يَقُولَ رَبِّيَ اللَّهُ‌).

"While he has brought you clear proofs from your Lord?" (وَ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ‌).

Can you deny his miracles, such as the miracle of the staff and the white hand? Did you not all see with your own eyes his victory over the sorcerers, to the extent that the sorcerers surrendered before him, ignored our threats, and laid down their lives for their faith in the God of Musa عليه السلام? Can such a person truly be called a sorcerer?! Think well, do not take a hasty and rushed action, and reflect properly on the consequences of your deeds, or else you will regret it.

Beyond all this, it is one of two cases: "And if he should be a liar, then upon him is [the consequence of] his lie; but if he should be truthful, there will strike you some of what he promises you" (وَ إِنْ يَكُ كاذِباً فَعَلَيْهِ كَذِبُهُ وَ إِنْ يَكُ صادِقاً يُصِبْكُمْ بَعْضُ الَّذِي يَعِدُكُمْ‌).

In short, if he is a liar, a lie has no lasting light; eventually, his hand will be revealed, he will be disgraced, and he will suffer the punishment for his lie. However, there is also the possibility that he is truthful and has a mission from Allah; therefore, his promises of punishment will inevitably come to pass. In such a state, killing him is far from reason and prudence.

He then added: "Indeed, Allah does not guide one who is a transgressor and a liar" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ كَذَّابٌ‌).

If Musa has taken the path of transgression, excess, and lying, he will certainly not be included in divine guidance. And if you are like this, you too will be deprived of His guidance.

Although this last phrase is double-edged, it is clear that the intention of the Believer of the People of Pharaoh was to describe the state of the Pharaohs. Nevertheless, his reliance on the Lordship of "Allah" in this phrase and the next indicates the reality that Pharaoh, or at least a group of the Pharaohs, had a general belief in "Allah." Otherwise, these expressions would have been considered signs of his faith in the God of Musa and cooperation with the Children of Israel, which would not have been consistent with the principles of "tactical Taqiyyah" (dissimulation) that he had adopted.

Here, two questions have been raised by some commentators:

First, if Musa عليه السلام were a liar, his lie would not only end to his own detriment but would also affect society, as it would cause their deviation.

Second, if he were truthful, all of his threats would be fulfilled, not just some of them.

Answer

In response to the first question, it can be said that the intention is only the punishment for the lie, which only affects the liar, and divine punishment is sufficient to repel his evil. How is it possible for someone to forge a lie against Allah and for Allah to leave him to himself to become a cause for the people's misguidance?! In response to the second question, it can be said: the intention is that he threatens you with punishments of this world and the Hereafter; therefore, if he is truthful, a part of it—which relates to this world—will strike you right now. Or, the intention is to state the minimum: that if you do not believe all his words, at least it is possible that a part of them is true.

In any case, the Believer of the People of Pharaoh entered through several paths to influence Pharaoh and his entourage through these words:

First, Musa’s عليه السلام action does not warrant such a severe reaction.

Second, do not forget that he has proofs with him whose appearance seems justified, and fighting such a man is dangerous.

Third, there is no need for your action, because if he is a liar, Allah will deal with him, but also consider the possibility that he is telling the truth and Allah will deal with us!

The Believer of the People of Pharaoh was not content with even this much and continued further. Addressing them in a friendly and well-wishing tone, he said: "O my people, sovereignty is yours today, [you being] dominant in the land; but who would help us from the punishment of Allah if it came to us?" (يا قَوْمِ لَكُمُ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ ظاهِرِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَمَنْ يَنْصُرُنا مِنْ بَأْسِ اللَّهِ إِنْ جاءَنا).

It is also possible that his intention was: today you have all kinds of power in your hands and can make any decision you want regarding Musa عليه السلام, but do not be deceived by this power, and do not forget its potential consequences.

These words apparently were not without effect on "Pharaoh's entourage"; they softened them and reduced their anger.

But here, Pharaoh did not consider silence permissible for himself. He interrupted him and said: "The matter is as I said. I do not show you except what I see [as correct]"—I believe that Musa must definitely be killed and there is no other way! (قالَ فِرْعَوْنُ ما أُرِيكُمْ إِلَّا ما أرى‌).

"And I do not guide you except to the way of right conduct" (وَ ما أَهْدِيكُمْ إِلَّا سَبِيلَ الرَّشادِ).

And such is the state of all tyrants and despots throughout history, in the past and today, who always consider their own opinion to be the correct one and do not allow anyone to express an opinion against theirs. In their imagination, they are the "universal intellect," and others have absolutely no reason or knowledge! And this is the ultimate height of ignorance and folly.

Key Points

1- Who was the Believer of the People of Pharaoh?

From the verses of the Quran, it is understood that he was a man from the Pharaohs who had believed in Musa but kept his faith hidden. In his heart, he loved Musa and saw it as his duty to defend him.

He was a vigilant, precise, and punctual man, and very powerful and strong in terms of logic, who rushed to Musa’s aid at sensitive moments and, as will follow in these verses, saved him from a dangerous assassination plot.

However, in Islamic narrations and the words of commentators, more descriptions have been given about him.

Among them, some have said: he was the cousin or the maternal aunt's son of Pharaoh, and they considered the expression "Al-Pharaoh" (People of Pharaoh) as evidence for this meaning, because the term "Al" is usually used for relatives, although it is also said for friends and associates.

Others consider him one of the prophets of God named "Hazbil" or "Hezekiah" [1]. Some have narrated that he was the treasurer (overseer of the treasuries and riches) of Pharaoh [2]. It is narrated from Ibn Abbas that among the Pharaohs, only three people believed in Musa:

[1]: This meaning is narrated in a tradition from the Noble Prophet of Islam (Amali al-Saduq as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 519). However, considering that Hezekiah is one of the prophets of the Children of Israel, this possibility seems unlikely, and the aforementioned narration is also weak in terms of its chain of transmission, unless he is not the famous Hezekiah of the Children of Israel but another person by this name.

[2]: This meaning is also mentioned in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim (Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 518).

the Believer of the People of Pharaoh, Pharaoh's wife, and the man who, before Musa’s prophethood, informed him that the Pharaohs intended to kill him for the killing of one of their followers and told him to leave Egypt as soon as possible (Al-Qasas: 20).

However, there are indications showing that after the event of Musa with the sorcerers, a significant group believed in Musa, and it appears that the story of the Believer of the People of Pharaoh occurred after the event of the sorcerers.

Some have also suggested the possibility that he was from the Children of Israel who lived among the Pharaohs and was trusted by them, but this possibility seems very weak because it is not consistent with the expression "Al-Pharaoh" and also "O my people" (يا قوم). In any case, his effective role in the history of Musa and the Children of Israel is completely clear, even if all the details of his life are not clear to us today.

2- Taqiyyah: An Effective Means of Struggle

"Taqiyyah" or "concealing one's inner belief," contrary to what some imagine, does not mean weakness, fear, or conservatism; rather, it is often used as an effective means for struggling against the powerful, despots, and oppressors. Discovering the enemy's secrets is not possible except through individuals who use the method of Taqiyyah.

Surprise blows to the body of the enemy do not take place except through Taqiyyah and the concealment of struggle plans and designs.

The Taqiyyah of the Believer of the People of Pharaoh was also for serving the religion of Musa عليه السلام and defending his life in difficult and critical moments. What could be better than for a person to have a believer within the enemy's apparatus who penetrates to the depths of their organization, becomes aware of everything, informs friends in a timely manner, and even, when necessary, influences the thoughts of the tyrants and alters their plans?! If the Believer of the People of Pharaoh had not used the method of Taqiyyah, would he ever have had the ability to perform these services?

Therefore, it is stated in a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"Taqiyyah is my religion and the religion of my fathers, and he who has no Taqiyyah has no religion. Taqiyyah is Allah’s shield on earth, for if the Believer of the People of Pharaoh had disclosed his Islam, he would have been killed." [1]

Especially at a time when the population of believers in a region is in the minority and caught in the clutches of an illogical and ruthless majority, no reason allows them to waste their active forces by declaring their faith except in cases of necessity. Rather, at this specific stage, they must organize and concentrate their forces by concealing their belief and prepare for the final uprising.

The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله himself had a secret call for several years at the beginning of his mission and used this very method, and after a while, when his companions increased and the main core became strong, he officially announced Islam.

Among other prophets, Ibrahim عليه السلام, with all the courage and heroism he had, used the method of Taqiyyah when deciding to break the idols and concealed his plan from the idolaters; otherwise, he would never have succeeded.

Abu Talib, the uncle of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, perhaps did not abandon the method of Taqiyyah until the end of his life. He only revealed his faith at specific junctures, but at other times he did not say anything explicitly so that he could fulfill his effective role in protecting the life of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله against the stubborn, ruthless, and vengeful idolaters.

In any case, what some ignorant and uninformed people have imagined—that Taqiyyah is exclusive to the Shia school of thought, or that Taqiyyah is a sign of weakness and abasement—is completely baseless and far from logic. Taqiyyah exists in all schools of thought without exception.

For further explanation, refer to Volume 2 of this Tafsir, page 373 (under verse 28 of Al-Imran) and Volume 11, page 423 (under verse 106 of Surah An-Nahl).

3- Who are the Siddiqun (The Truthful)?

In some narrations, it is quoted from the Noble Prophet of Islam:

"The Siddiqun (the foremost truthful ones) are three: Habib al-Najjar, the Believer of the People of Ya-Sin, who said: 'Follow the messengers; follow those who do not ask you for a reward'; and Hezekiah, the Believer of the People of Pharaoh; and Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام, and he is the most virtuous of them."

This tradition is found in both Shia and Sunni sources [1]. Truly, all three of them believed in the divine prophets at the most critical moments and were pioneers and frontrunners, and they are worthy of the name "Siddiq." They stand at the head of those who testified to the truth of the divine prophets, especially Ali عليه السلام, who from the beginning of his life until the end was always the helper and companion of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and expressed sacrifice and selflessness to the ultimate degree during the Prophet's life and even after his passing.

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 8, p. 521 (under the verses discussed).

[1]: Saduq in "Amali" and Ibn Hajar also mentioned it in the second section of Chapter 9 of "Al-Sawa'iq."

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنَ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُم مِّثْلَ يَوْمِ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ (٣٠)
He who had faith said, ‘O my people! Indeed I fear for you [a day] like the day of the [heathen] factions; (30)
مِثْلَ دَأْبِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَعَادٍ وَثَمُودَ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ ۚ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ ظُلْمًا لِّلْعِبَادِ (٣١)
like the case of the people of Noah, of Ad and Thamud, and those who came after them, and Allah does not desire any wrong for [His] servants. (31)
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلتَّنَادِ (٣٢)
O my people! I fear for you a day of mutual distress calls, (32)
يَوْمَ تُوَلُّونَ مُدْبِرِينَ مَا لَكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ عَاصِمٍ ۗ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْ هَادٍ (٣٣)
a day when you will turn back [to flee], not having anyone to protect you from Allah, and whomever Allah leads astray has no guide. (33)

Commentary

I Warn You!

Since the people of Egypt were relatively civilized and literate at that time, they had heard the accounts of historians regarding previous nations—nations such as the people of Nuh, ‘Ad, and Thamud, whose lands were often not far from them—and they were more or less aware of their painful fates.

Therefore, after the Believer from the Family of Pharaoh opposed the plot to kill Musa and faced Pharaoh's stubborn resistance—who reaffirmed the order for the execution—he did not cease his efforts, nor should he have. Thus, he thought of taking the hand of this rebellious people and leading them into the depths of the history of those who came before, warning them against the repetition of such calamities for themselves, so that perhaps they might wake up and reconsider their decision. He began his speech from here: "And he who believed said: 'O my people! Indeed, I fear for you a day like the day of the [previous] parties'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِي آمَنَ يا قَوْمِ إِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ مِثْلَ يَوْمِ الْأَحْزابِ).

Then he proceeded to elaborate on this statement, saying: "I fear for you a fate like the habitual state of the people of Nuh and ‘Ad and Thamud and those after them" (مِثْلَ دَأْبِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَ عادٍ وَ ثَمُودَ وَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ). [1]

[1]: Da’b (on the weight of darb) originally means the continuation of a journey, and da’ib refers to a being that continues its path. Subsequently, it was applied to any continuous habit. What is meant here by "the da’b of the people of Nuh..." is the continuous habit they possessed, which was persistence in polytheism, rebellion, oppression, and ingratitude.

The habit of these nations was polytheism, disbelief, and rebellion, and we saw what fate befell them. One group was destroyed by a crushing flood, another by a terrifying gale, some by heavenly thunderbolts, and others by devastating earthquakes! Do you not consider it possible that you too, with this insistence on disbelief and rebellion, might be caught by one of these great Divine afflictions?! So allow me to say that I fear such a grim future for you! Do you have any reason to believe you are a "separate fabric" and that such Divine punishments will not reach you? What had they done to be so afflicted?

Was it anything other than standing against the call of God's prophets, sometimes killing the prophets, or denying them?

But know that whatever befalls you is from your own side, "for Allah does not desire injustice for [His] servants" (وَ مَا اللَّهُ يُرِيدُ ظُلْماً لِلْعِبادِ).

He created them by His grace and generosity, bestowed upon them countless blessings, and sent His prophets for their guidance; it is the opposition and rebellion of the servants that causes those painful punishments.

Then he added: "O my people! Indeed, I fear for you the Day of Calling" (وَ يا قَوْمِ إِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ يَوْمَ التَّنادِ). "Al-Tanad" (originally "Al-Tanadi," where the ya was omitted and the kasra on the dal remains as an indicator) is from the root nida, meaning to call out.

The well-known view among commentators is that "the Day of Calling" (Yawm al-Tanad) is one of the names of the Resurrection, and each has mentioned a reason for naming the Resurrection by this name, which are very similar to one another.

One says: It is because of the calling of the people of Hell to the people of Paradise, as the Quran says: "And the companions of the Fire will call out to the companions of Paradise, 'Pour upon us some water or from whatever Allah has provided you.'" They will reply: "Indeed, Allah has forbidden them both to the disbelievers" (Al-A'raf: 50). [1] Or it is because people will call out to one another, seeking refuge and help from each other.

Or it is because the angels will call them for the reckoning, and they will seek help from the angels.

Or it is because the callers of the gathering will cry out: "Unquestionably, the curse of Allah is upon the wrongdoers" (Hud: 18).

Or it is because the believer, when he sees his record of deeds, cries out in joy: "Here, read my record!" (Al-Haqqah: 19).

And the disbeliever at that same moment cries out in terror: "Oh, I wish I had not been given my record!" (Al-Haqqah: 25).

However, a broader meaning can be considered for this verse, such that "the Day of Calling" also includes this world. For the concept of "the Day of Calling" is simply "the day of calling out to one another," and this expression is a sign of ultimate helplessness and misery at a time when the knife reaches the bone, and individuals who are cut off from all sides call out to one another, yet their cries lead nowhere.

In this world too, there are many "Days of Calling"—days when Divine punishment descends, days when societies reach a dead end due to their sins and errors, days when crises and harsh events pressure everyone; they flee and seek a refuge, but no refuge exists, and everyone cries out!

The next verse, in explaining the Day of Calling, says: "The Day you will turn your backs and flee; there is not for you from Allah any protector" (يَوْمَ تُوَلُّونَ مُدْبِرِينَ ما لَكُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ مِنْ عاصِمٍ). Indeed, "and whoever Allah leaves astray—for him there is no guide" (وَ مَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللَّهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ هادٍ).

In this world, they are led astray from the path of guidance and sink into a veil of ignorance and misguidance, and in the Hereafter, they will be led away from the path of Paradise and Divine blessings.

The above expression may be an implicit reference to Pharaoh's statement when he said: "I do not guide you except to the way of right conduct" (three verses prior in this same Surah).

[1]: This same meaning is narrated in a Hadith in Ma'ani al-Akhbar by Saduq from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ جَآءَكُمْ يُوسُفُ مِن قَبْلُ بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَمَا زِلْتُمْ فِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّا جَآءَكُم بِهِۦ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا هَلَكَ قُلْتُمْ لَن يَبْعَثَ ٱللَّهُ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ رَسُولًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ مُّرْتَابٌ (٣٤)
Certainly Joseph brought you manifest proofs earlier, but you continued to remain in doubt concerning what he had brought you. When he died, you said, ‘‘Allah will never send any apostle after him.’’ That is how Allah leads astray those who are unrestrained and skeptical. (34)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَـٰنٍ أَتَىٰهُمْ ۖ كَبُرَ مَقْتًا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَعِندَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَطْبَعُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ قَلْبِ مُتَكَبِّرٍ جَبَّارٍ (٣٥)
Those who dispute the signs of Allah without any authority that may have come to them—[that is] greatly outrageous to Allah and to those who have faith. That is how Allah seals the heart of every arrogant tyrant.’ (35)

Commentary

The Arrogant Tyrants are Deprived of Correct Understanding

In these verses, the discourse of the Believer from the Family of Pharaoh continues:

In a brief overview of the preceding and succeeding verses, as well as the verses under discussion, it appears that the "Believer from the Family of Pharaoh" presented his words in five stages and forms to penetrate the dark hearts of Pharaoh and his people and to remove the rust of arrogance and disbelief from them:

The "first stage" consisted of bilateral and cautious remarks, inviting that rebellious, disbelieving people to avoid potential harm, based on the logic that: "If Moses is lying, it will fall back upon him, and if he is telling the truth, it will afflict us; fear and do not abandon caution."

In the "second stage," he invites them to travel and study the states of previous nations, warning them against meeting such a grim fate.

In the "third stage," which is presented in the verses under discussion, he reminds them of a part of their own history—a history not far removed from them, whose ties and connections remain intact—and that is the issue of the prophethood of "Joseph," who was among the ancestors of Moses, and he highlights the manner in which they dealt with his call.

In the first verse, he says: "And Joseph had already come to you before with clear proofs for your guidance" (وَلَقَدْ جَاءَكُمْ يُوسُفُ مِنْ قَبْلُ بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ) [^1] "but you remained in doubt about what he brought to you" (فَمَا زِلْتُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِمَّا جَاءَكُمْ بِهِ).

[^1]: This verse is the only instance in the Quran that explicitly indicates the prophethood of "Joseph," although there are allusions to this meaning in Surah Yusuf, they are not as explicit.

This was not because his call was complex or his signs and proofs were insufficient, but rather because of your continued self-will and stubbornness, you persistently expressed doubt and hesitation.

Then, in order to free yourselves from any obligation and responsibility and to continue your self-will and indulgence, "when Joseph died, you said: 'Allah will never send a messenger after him'" (حَتَّىٰ إِذَا هَلَكَ قُلْتُمْ لَنْ يَبْعَثَ اللَّهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ رَسُولًا).

And because of this improper approach, you were not included in Divine guidance; indeed, "Thus does Allah lead astray him who is a transgressor and a doubter" (كَذَلِكَ يُضِلُّ اللَّهُ مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ مُرْتَابٌ).

On one hand, you took the path of extravagance and transgressing Divine limits, and on the other, you doubted and hesitated in everything; these two actions caused Allah to withdraw His grace from you and leave you in the wilderness of deviation, and no fate other than this awaited you.

Now, if you take the same approach toward the call of Moses and do not engage in investigation and research, he might be a prophet from Allah, but the light of His guidance will never shine upon your covered and veiled hearts.

The next verse proceeds to define the "transgressing doubters," saying: "Those who dispute concerning the signs of Allah without any authority having come to them" (الَّذِينَ يُجَادِلُونَ فِي آيَاتِ اللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَانٍ أَتَاهُمْ) [^1]

[^1]: Although "الَّذِينَ" (those who) is a substitute for "transgressor, doubter" and the "substituted from" is singular, the substitute is plural because it refers not to a specific individual, but to the genus/category.

Without having any clear proof from reason or tradition for their words, they take a stand against the clear signs of Allah, and with far-fetched possibilities, baseless whispers, and excuse-making, they continue their opposition.

Then, to demonstrate the ugliness of this act, it adds: "Such baseless dispute against the truth is greatly hateful in the sight of Allah and in the sight of those who have believed" (كَبُرَ مَقْتًا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ وَعِنْدَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا) [^1] because "disputing with falsehood" and taking a stand without proof or logic against the signs of Allah causes both the deviation of the disputers and the misguidance of others; it extinguishes the light of truth in the environment and strengthens the foundations of the dominion of falsehood.

And at the end of the verse, it points to the reason for their refusal to submit to the truth, saying: "Thus does Allah set a seal over every heart of an arrogant tyrant"! (كَذَلِكَ يَطْبَعُ اللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ قَلْبِ مُتَكَبِّرٍ جَبَّارٍ) [^2] Stubbornness and enmity toward the truth cast a dark veil over human thought and strip away the sense of discernment. It reaches a point where the heart becomes like a sealed container; neither its corrupt contents come out, nor do any correct and life-giving contents enter it.

[^1]: The subject of "كَبُرَ" (is great) is "the dispute," which is understood from the previous sentence; "مَقْتًا" (hatred) is the specification (tamyiz). Some commentators have also suggested that the subject might be the "transgressing doubter," but the first meaning seems better.

[^2]: It is noteworthy that in this verse, "arrogant and tyrant" are mentioned as descriptions of the heart (even though in the form of an annexation/idafa), rather than descriptions of the person, indicating that the root of arrogance and tyranny is in the heart, and it is from there that it spreads to the entire being of the person, and all limbs take on the color of arrogance and tyranny.

Indeed, those who, due to possessing these two ugly traits of "arrogance and tyranny," have decided to stand against the truth and accept no reality, Allah takes away the spirit of seeking truth from them, such that truth tastes bitter to them and falsehood tastes sweet.

With these statements, the Believer from the Family of Pharaoh achieved his purpose, and as can be inferred from the subsequent verses, he shook Pharaoh's decision to kill Moses (peace be upon him), or at least delayed it—the very delay that ultimately removed the danger from Moses. This was a great mission that this wise and brave man performed at this sensitive stage, and as we shall see later, he likely gave his life for this cause.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ يَـٰهَـٰمَـٰنُ ٱبْنِ لِى صَرْحًا لَّعَلِّىٓ أَبْلُغُ ٱلْأَسْبَـٰبَ (٣٦)
Pharaoh said, ‘O Haman! Build me a tower so that I may reach the routes (36)
أَسْبَـٰبَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ فَأَطَّلِعَ إِلَىٰٓ إِلَـٰهِ مُوسَىٰ وَإِنِّى لَأَظُنُّهُۥ كَـٰذِبًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِفِرْعَوْنَ سُوٓءُ عَمَلِهِۦ وَصُدَّ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ ۚ وَمَا كَيْدُ فِرْعَوْنَ إِلَّا فِى تَبَابٍ (٣٧)
—the routes of the heavens—and take a look at the God of Moses, and indeed I consider him a liar.’ The evil of his conduct was thus presented as decorous to Pharaoh, and he was kept from the way [of Allah]. Pharaoh’s stratagems only led him into ruin. (37)

Commentary

I Want to Go to the Heavens to Seek News of the God of Moses!!

Although the words of the "Believer from the Family of Pharaoh" had the effect of deterring Pharaoh from his decision to kill Moses (peace be upon him), they could not bring Pharaoh down from the steed of arrogance, stop his devilry, or force him to submit to the Truth. This was because Pharaoh lacked the merit and capacity for it. Therefore, in continuation of his diabolical actions, he embarked on a new scheme: the construction of a lofty tower to ascend to the heavens and gain knowledge of the God of Moses! As we read in the verses under discussion:

"Pharaoh said: 'O Haman! Build for me a lofty tower so that I may reach the means'" (وَ قالَ فِرْعَوْنُ يا هامانُ ابْنِ لِي صَرْحاً لَعَلِّي أَبْلُغُ الْأَسْبابَ‌).

"The means of reaching the heavens, so that I may look upon the God of Moses, though I surely think he is a liar" (أَسْبابَ السَّماواتِ فَأَطَّلِعَ إِلى‌ إِلهِ مُوسى‌ وَ إِنِّي لَأَظُنُّهُ كاذِباً). Indeed, "Thus the evil of Pharaoh’s deeds was made fair-seeming to him, and he was barred from the [right] path" (كَذلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِفِرْعَوْنَ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ وَ صُدَّ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ‌).

"And the plot of Pharaoh led only to ruin" (وَ ما كَيْدُ فِرْعَوْنَ إِلَّا فِي تَبابٍ‌).

"Sarh" (صرح) originally means clarity and manifestness, and "Tasrih" (تصريح) means to make something clear. Subsequently, this word was applied to tall buildings and beautiful, lofty palaces because they are completely clear, visible, and manifest. Many commentators and lexicographers have explicitly stated this meaning.

And "Tabab" (تباب) means loss and destruction.

The first thing that comes to mind here is: what was Pharaoh's objective in doing this?

Was he truly so foolish as to think that the God of Moses (peace be upon him) was in the sky, and that by building a tall structure—which, compared to the mountains on the Earth's surface, has a very negligible height—he could reach the heavens?! This seems very unlikely, for Pharaoh, despite all his pride and arrogance, was a clever man and a politician who had kept a great nation in bondage for many years and ruled over them with power. In the case of such individuals, every move has a diabolical aspect; one must first analyze the diabolical political motivation behind this act.

It appears that Pharaoh undertook this task with several objectives in mind:

  1. He wanted to provide a means to occupy the people's thoughts and distract their minds from the issue of Moses' (peace be upon him) prophethood and the uprising of the Israelites. The construction of this lofty building—which, according to some commentators, was built on a vast area of land with fifty thousand masons, architects, and many workers to provide the materials—could overshadow other issues. As the building rose higher, it would attract more of the people's attention, becoming the talk of every gathering and the news of the day, temporarily consigning to oblivion the victory of Moses over the sorcerers, which had dealt a massive blow to the power of the Pharaohs.

  2. He wanted to provide material and economic aid to the hardworking masses through this means, creating jobs—even if temporary—for the unemployed so they might somewhat forget his oppressions, and so the people's economic dependence on his treasury would increase.

  3. The plan was that after the completion of the building, he would go atop it, look at the sky, and perhaps place an arrow in a bow and shoot it, then return and say to the foolish people: "Whatever the God of Moses was, it is finished! Go about your business and be at ease!" Otherwise, it was clear to Pharaoh that his massive building, which did not exceed a few hundred meters, was nothing; even from the peaks of very high mountains, if one looks at the sky, the view is the same as what is seen from flat ground, without the slightest change.

It is noteworthy that Pharaoh, by speaking these words and giving this command, takes a step back in the face of Moses (peace be upon him) and says: "I want to investigate the God of Moses" (فَأَطَّلِعَ إِلى‌ إِلهِ مُوسى‌) and adds: "though I surely think he is a liar." In this way, he descends from the stage of certainty of the opposite to the stage of suspicion and doubt.

It is also noteworthy that the Quran, with the sentence "Thus the evil of Pharaoh’s deeds was made fair-seeming to him, and he was barred from the [right] path, and the plot of Pharaoh led only to ruin," first states the root cause of Pharaoh's deviation—which is his evil deeds appearing beautiful to him due to pride, arrogance, and selfishness. Then it mentions the result, which is being barred from the path of truth, and in the third stage, it announces the final failure of his plans. Three short sentences with three rich contents.

Certainly, these political games can be effective for a short time, but in the long run, they will definitely face failure.

In some narrations, it is mentioned that Haman raised the construction of the Pharaonic tower so high that strong winds no longer allowed the builders to continue. He came to Pharaoh and said: "We are no longer able to increase the height of the building." Not long after, a violent wind blew and toppled the structure [1], and it became clear that all of Pharaoh's display of power depended on a single wind!

[1]: "Bihar al-Anwar," Volume 13, Page 125 (quoted from the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim).

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱتَّبِعُونِ أَهْدِكُمْ سَبِيلَ ٱلرَّشَادِ (٣٨)
And he who had faith said, ‘O my people! Follow me; I will guide you to the way of rectitude. (38)
يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنَّمَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا مَتَـٰعٌ وَإِنَّ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ هِىَ دَارُ ٱلْقَرَارِ (٣٩)
O my people! This life of the world is only a [passing] enjoyment, and indeed the Hereafter is the abiding home. (39)
مَنْ عَمِلَ سَيِّئَةً فَلَا يُجْزَىٰٓ إِلَّا مِثْلَهَا ۖ وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا مِّن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ يُرْزَقُونَ فِيهَا بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (٤٠)
Whoever commits a misdeed shall not be requited except with its like, but whoever acts righteously, whether male or female, should he be faithful—such shall enter paradise, and they will be provided therein without any reckoning. (40)

Commentary

Follow Me So That I May Guide You to the Right Path

We have stated that the "Believer from the People of Pharaoh" delivered his words in several stages. In these verses, the fourth stage of his speech is presented, where he pursues his objective through another approach: drawing attention to the "instability of worldly life" and the "issue of the Resurrection and the Gathering." Undoubtedly, reflecting upon these matters has a profound impact on the cultivation of human beings.

First, he says: "The one who had believed called out: O my people! Follow me so that I may guide you to the path of right guidance" (وَ قالَ الَّذِي آمَنَ يا قَوْمِ اتَّبِعُونِ أَهْدِكُمْ سَبِيلَ الرَّشادِ).

A few verses prior to this, we read that Pharaoh claimed: "What I say to you is the path of growth and rectitude." With this statement, the Believer from the People of Pharaoh rose to confront and refute Pharaoh, making the masses understand that they should not be deceived by Pharaoh's seductive words, for his programs lead only to failure and misery. The true path is the one I am telling you—the path of piety and the worship of God.

He then added: "O my people! Do not set your hearts on this world, for this worldly life is but a fleeting enjoyment, and the Hereafter is the permanent and eternal abode" (يا قَوْمِ إِنَّما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا مَتاعٌ وَ إِنَّ الْآخِرَةَ هِيَ دارُ الْقَرارِ). Suppose that through a thousand deceits and charms we emerge victorious, cast the truth behind us, stretch out our hands to commit all kinds of oppression, and stain our skirts with the blood of the innocent; how long will our lives in this world last? These few days of life pass quickly, and the claws of death will seize everyone, dragging them down from magnificent palaces into the dust. The true dwelling place of our life is elsewhere.

The issue is not merely the transience of this world and the permanence of the Hereafter; the crucial matter is the issue of Reckoning and Recompense: "Whoever does an evil deed will not be recompensed except by the like thereof; but whoever does a righteous deed, whether male or female, while being a believer—those will enter Paradise, wherein they will be provided for without account" (مَنْ عَمِلَ سَيِّئَةً فَلا يُجْزى‌ إِلَّا مِثْلَها وَ مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى‌ وَ هُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ يُرْزَقُونَ فِيها بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ‌).

In these calculated words, he points, on one hand, to the justice of Allah regarding criminals, who are penalized only to the extent of their crime.

On the other hand, he points to His infinite grace, whereby believers are given a reward "without account" in exchange for a single righteous deed, and no proportion will be strictly observed therein—a reward that no eye has seen, no ear has heard, and has not even crossed the mind of any human.

Thirdly, he reminds them of the necessity of the conjunction of faith and righteous deeds.

Fourthly, he highlights the equality of man and woman before Allah and in human values.

In any case, with this brief statement, he expresses the reality that although the enjoyment of this world is meager and unstable, it can serve as a means to attain an immeasurable reward. What transaction could be more profitable than this?! Furthermore, the expression "the like thereof" (مثلها) indicates that the punishments of the next world are similar to the very actions a person performed in this world—a complete and perfect similarity.

The expression "without account" (بغير حساب) may refer to the fact that keeping account of gifts is for those who possess limited resources and fear that if they do not keep track, they will face a shortage. However, He whose treasuries of blessings are unlimited and endless, and no matter how much He bestows, no deficiency occurs (for no matter how much is taken from infinity, it remains infinity!), has no need for accounting.

Here, a question arises: Does this verse contradict the verse that says: "Whoever brings a good deed shall have ten times the like thereof" (مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثالِها) [Al-An'am: 160]?

Answer

In response, one must note that "ten times" is the minimum divine reward; hence, in the case of charity (infaq), it increases to seven hundred times and more, and finally reaches the stage of "reward without account," the limit of which no one knows except Allah.

Ayahs

۞ وَيَـٰقَوْمِ مَا لِىٓ أَدْعُوكُمْ إِلَى ٱلنَّجَوٰةِ وَتَدْعُونَنِىٓ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ (٤١)
O my people! [Think,] what makes me invite you to deliverance, while you invite me toward the Fire? (41)
تَدْعُونَنِى لِأَكْفُرَ بِٱللَّهِ وَأُشْرِكَ بِهِۦ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ وَأَنَا۠ أَدْعُوكُمْ إِلَى ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْغَفَّـٰرِ (٤٢)
You invite me to defy Allah and to ascribe to Him partners of which I have no knowledge, while I call you to the All-mighty, the All-forgiver. (42)
لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّمَا تَدْعُونَنِىٓ إِلَيْهِ لَيْسَ لَهُۥ دَعْوَةٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَلَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَأَنَّ مَرَدَّنَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَأَنَّ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ هُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ (٤٣)
Undoubtedly, that to which you invite me has no invitation in the world nor in the Hereafter, and indeed our return will be to Allah, and indeed it is the transgressors who will be inmates of the Fire. (43)
فَسَتَذْكُرُونَ مَآ أَقُولُ لَكُمْ ۚ وَأُفَوِّضُ أَمْرِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بَصِيرٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ (٤٤)
Soon you will remember what I tell you, and I entrust my affair to Allah. Indeed Allah watches His servants.’ (44)
فَوَقَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِ مَا مَكَرُوا۟ ۖ وَحَاقَ بِـَٔالِ فِرْعَوْنَ سُوٓءُ ٱلْعَذَابِ (٤٥)
Then Allah saved him from their evil schemes, while a terrible punishment besieged Pharaoh’s clan: (45)
ٱلنَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْهَا غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا ۖ وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوٓا۟ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ ٱلْعَذَابِ (٤٦)
the Fire, to which they are exposed morning and evening. On the day when the Hour sets in, Pharaoh’s clan will enter the severest punishment. (46)

Commentary

The Final Word!

In the fifth and final stage, the "Believer from the People of Pharaoh" cast aside the veils and could no longer keep his faith concealed. He said what needed to be said, and they—as we shall see—took a dangerous decision regarding him.

It appears from the context that those stubborn, arrogant, and egoistic people did not remain silent in the face of this brave and faithful man's words; instead, they spoke of the "advantages" of polytheism and invited him to idol worship.

Thus, he cried out and said: "O my people! How is it that I call you to salvation, while you call me to the Fire?" (وَيَا قَوْمِ مَا لِي أَدْعُوكُمْ إِلَى النَّجَاةِ وَتَدْعُونَنِي إِلَى النَّارِ).
