I seek your prosperity, yet you seek my misery; I call you to the highway of guidance, while you call me to the wilderness of deviation.

Indeed, "You call me to disbelieve in Allah and to associate with Him partners of which I have no knowledge, while I call you to the Almighty, the All-Forgiving" (تَدْعُونَنِي لِأَكْفُرَ بِاللَّهِ وَأُشْرِكَ بِهِ مَا لَيْسَ لِي بِهِ عِلْمٌ وَأَنَا أَدْعُوكُمْ إِلَى الْعَزِيزِ الْغَفَّارِ). It is clearly understood from various verses of the Quran and the history of the Egyptian people that, in addition to worshipping the Pharaohs, they had numerous idols. As we read in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 127, Pharaoh's entourage said to him: "Will you leave Moses and his people to cause corruption in the land and abandon you and your gods?" (أَتَذَرُ مُوسَىٰ وَقَوْمَهُ لِيُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَيَذَرَكَ وَآلِهَتَكَ). Similarly, Yusuf (peace be upon him) said to his fellow prisoners in the Pharaohs' jail: "Are diverse lords better, or Allah, the One, the Irresistible?" (أَأَرْبَابٌ مُتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ اللَّهُ الْوَاحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ) (Yusuf, verse 39).

In any case, the Believer from the People of Pharaoh reminded them through a clear comparison: "Your invitation is an invitation toward polytheism—something for which there is not the slightest evidence, and it is a dark and dangerous path. But I invite you to a clear path, the path of the Almighty and Powerful God, the path of the All-Forgiving and Merciful Lord."

The use of the terms "Almighty" (Aziz) and "All-Forgiving" (Ghaffar) is, on one hand, an allusion to these great sources of fear and hope, and on the other hand, a negation of the divinity of idols and Pharaohs, in whom there is neither true might nor any pardon or forgiveness!

He then added: "Assuredly, that to which you call me has no claim [to be worshipped] in this world or in the Hereafter (these idols have never sent messengers to the people to invite them to themselves, nor can they have any sovereignty over anything in the Hereafter)" (لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّمَا تَدْعُونَنِي إِلَيْهِ لَيْسَ لَهُ دَعْوَةٌ فِي الدُّنْيَا وَلَا فِي الْآخِرَةِ) [1]. These senseless and unconscious beings have never been, and will never be, the source of any movement; they do not speak, they have no messengers, and they have no court or tribunal. In short, they neither untie anyone's knot nor can they tie one. For this reason, you must know that "Our return in the Resurrection is only to Allah" (وَأَنَّ مَرَدَّنَا إِلَى اللَّهِ).

It is He who sent His messengers for the guidance of humanity, and it is He who rewards or punishes them for their deeds.

Furthermore, you must know that "the transgressors are the inhabitants of the Fire" (وَأَنَّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ هُمْ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ).

In this way, the "Believer from the People of Pharaoh" finally made his faith manifest, separating his monotheistic line from the polytheistic line of those people. He rejected those strangers to the truth and stood alone with his eloquent logic against them all.

[1]: "La Jarama," as we have mentioned before, is a combination of "La" and "Jaram" (on the weight of Haram), which originally means to cut or pick fruit. This combination implies that nothing can cut off or prevent this matter; thus, taken together, it means "certainly," "inevitably," and is sometimes used as an oath.

In his final words, he said with a meaningful threat: "Soon you will remember what I am saying to you today; and when the fire of Divine wrath and anger seizes you in this world and the next, you will realize the truth of my words" (فَسَتَذْكُرُونَ مَا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ).

But alas, that time will be too late. If it is in the Hereafter, there is no way back; and if it is in this world, all doors of repentance are closed at the moment the punishment descends.

He then added: "I entrust my affair to Allah, for He is ever Watchful of His servants" (وَأُفَوِّضُ أَمْرِي إِلَى اللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ بَصِيرٌ بِالْعِبَادِ).

For this reason, I neither fear your threats, nor does your numbers and power or my loneliness terrify me, for I have surrendered myself entirely to One whose power is infinite and who is well-aware of the state of His servants.

This expression was also a polite prayer from this faithful man who was caught in the clutches of a powerful and ruthless people—a polite request from the presence of the Lord to place him under His protection in these circumstances.

Allah did not leave this faithful, striving servant alone, and as we read in the next verse: "So Allah protected him from the evils of their plots" (فَوَقَاهُ اللَّهُ سَيِّئَاتِ مَا مَكَرُوا). The expression "the evils of their plots" indicates that they had devised various conspiracies against him. What were these conspiracies? The Quran mentions them broadly; naturally, they included various punishments, tortures, and ultimately murder and execution, but Divine grace neutralized them all.

In some commentaries, it is mentioned that he took advantage of a suitable opportunity to reach Moses (peace be upon him) and crossed the sea with the Children of Israel. It is also said that when they decided to kill him, he fled to a mountain and disappeared from sight [1]. These two are not contradictory; he might have first hidden outside the city and later joined the Children of Israel.

Part of these plots might have been the attempt to impose idol worship and force him out of the line of monotheism, which Allah also averted from him, making him firm in the path of faith, monotheism, and piety.

However, in contrast, "a terrible punishment encompassed the people of Pharaoh" (وَحَاقَ بِآلِ فِرْعَوْنَ سُوءُ الْعَذَابِ) 2. All Divine punishment is painful, but the expression "terrible punishment" (Su' al-Adhab) shows that Allah chose a more agonizing punishment for this group. This is what He refers to in the following verse.

He says: "Their painful punishment is the Fire; they are exposed to it morning and evening" (النَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْهَا غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا) 3 "and on the Day the Hour is established, [it will be said]: 'Cast the people of Pharaoh into the severest punishment'" (وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ الْعَذَابِ).

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," under the verse in question.

It is noteworthy that, firstly, He uses the term "People of Pharaoh," referring to his family, entourage, and misguided companions; when they meet such a fate, the fate of Pharaoh himself is clear.

Secondly, He says they are exposed to the Fire morning and evening, but on the Day of Resurrection, He enters them into the severest punishment. This clearly indicates that the first punishment is the "Barzakh" (purgatory) punishment, which occurs after this world and before the Resurrection. Its nature is being "exposed" and brought near to the fire of Hell—an exposure that makes both the soul tremble and affects the body.

Thirdly, the expression "morning and evening" (ghuduwwan wa 'ashiyyan) either refers to the continuity of this punishment—as we say someone bothers us "morning and evening," meaning always—or it refers to the intermittent nature of the Barzakh punishment, occurring specifically at times of morning and evening, which were the times of the Pharaohs' displays of power and their indulgence.

One should not be surprised by the terms "morning" and "evening"—asking if such things exist in the world of Barzakh—because it is understood from Quranic verses that even in the Hereafter, there are mornings and evenings. As we read in Surah Maryam, verse 62: "And they will have their provision therein, morning and evening" (وَلَهُمْ رِزْقُهُمْ فِيهَا بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا). This does not contradict the permanence of heavenly blessings, as stated in Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 35: "Its fruit is eternal, and its shade," for it is possible that while the blessings are continuous, special provisions and graces are granted to the inhabitants of Paradise at these two specific times.

Key Points

1- The Story of the Believer from the People of Pharaoh: A Great Lesson in Struggling Against Tyrants

Divine religions and heavenly schools of thought that appeared against tyrants and oppressors were initially presented by a small group. If they had been terrified by their small numbers and considered the majority of opponents as proof of the opponents' righteousness, these schools would never have grown.

The fundamental principle governing all their programs is what the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), stated in his profound words: "O people, do not be terrified on the path of guidance due to the small number of its followers" [1]. The Believer from the People of Pharaoh was a symbol of this school and a traveler among the pioneers of this path. He showed that a human being with a firm resolve born of faith can influence even the will of tyrannical Pharaohs and save a great Prophet from danger.

The life story of this brave and vigilant man shows that the movements of the supporters of truth must always be calculated. Sometimes faith must be declared and shouted; sometimes, for "short-term" and "long-term" goals, faith must be concealed. And "Taqiyyah" (dissimulation) is nothing other than a person concealing their belief at a specific juncture for the sake of sacred goals.

Just as being equipped with physical weapons is necessary to crush the enemy, the sharp weapon of logic is also essential, as its impact is far greater than physical weapons. Thus, what the Believer from the People of Pharaoh achieved with his logic in those specific circumstances could not have been achieved by any physical weapon.

[1]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Sermon 201.

Ultimately, the story of the Believer from the People of Pharaoh shows that God does not leave such faithful individuals alone and places them under the protection of His grace against dangers.

It is also noteworthy that, according to some narrations, the "Believer from the People of Pharaoh" was eventually martyred. When the Quran says Allah saved him from the evil plots of the Pharaohs, it means He preserved him from deviation in belief and from having disbelief and polytheism imposed upon him [1].

2- Entrusting Affairs to Allah (Tafwid)

Regarding the importance of entrusting one's affairs to Allah and relying on the Lord, it suffices to mention a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him): "Faith has four pillars: reliance (Tawakkul) on Allah, entrusting (Tafwid) one's affair to Allah the Almighty, being pleased (Rida) with Allah's decree, and submission (Taslim) to Allah's command" 2. Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "The one who entrusts his affair to Allah is in eternal ease and a permanent, prosperous life; and the one who truly entrusts is one who is above any concern other than Allah" 3. "Tafwid," as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, means "to hand over."

[1]: In the book "Al-Mahasin al-Barqi," it is mentioned that Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) was asked about the interpretation of "So Allah protected him from the evils of their plots." He said: "Indeed, they attacked him and killed him, but do you know what He protected him from? He protected him from being tempted away from his religion" (Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 521). 2: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 68, p. 341. 3: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 387, root "f-w-d."

Therefore, "entrusting the affair to Allah" means handing over one's work to Him. This does not mean that a person should stop striving and making an effort—which is certainly a distortion of the meaning of "Tafwid"—but rather it means that one should employ the utmost effort, struggle, and Jihad, and when faced with harsh obstacles, one should not be terrified, panicked, or discouraged, but rather entrust the outcome to Allah and continue the struggle with firm resolve.

Although "Tafwid" is very similar in concept to "Tawakkul" (reliance), it is a higher stage. The "reality of Tawakkul" is considering Allah as one's representative (Wakil), but "Tafwid" implies absolute handing over to Him. This is because a person often chooses a representative but continues their own supervision; however, in the state of Tafwid, one holds no personal opinion of their own.

3- The World of Barzakh

"Barzakh," as its name suggests, is a world that acts as an intermediary between this world and the next. In the Holy Quran, while the world of Resurrection is discussed extensively, the discussion on Barzakh is limited. For this reason, it is surrounded by a veil of ambiguity, and its characteristics and details are not very clear. The truth is that knowledge of the specifics of Barzakh does not significantly impact matters of belief, which is perhaps why it is discussed less in the Book of Allah. However, it must not be forgotten that the Quran explicitly states the existence of the world of Barzakh.

Among the verses that clearly inform of the existence of such a world are the verses under discussion, where it says: "The people of Pharaoh are punished by being exposed to the Fire every morning and evening before the Day of Resurrection." This is nothing other than "Barzakh punishment."

On the other hand, verses that speak of the eternal life of martyrs after death and discuss their extraordinary rewards are evidence of the existence of "Barzakh blessings."

It is noteworthy that in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), we read: "When one of you dies, his place is shown to him morning and evening; if he is of the people of Paradise, then from Paradise, and if he is of the people of the Fire, then from the Fire. It is said to him: 'This is your place when Allah resurrects you on the Day of Judgment!'" (And this very matter causes joy or torment to his soul) [1]. Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) says: "That is in this world before the Day of Resurrection, because in the Fire of Resurrection there is no morning and evening." Then he said: "If they were punished in the Fire only morning and evening [in the Hereafter], then between those times they would be among the happy ones. No, but this is in the Barzakh before the Day of Resurrection. Have you not heard the words of the Almighty: 'And on the Day the Hour is established, [it will be said]: Cast the people of Pharaoh into the severest punishment'?" 2.

[1]: This narration is recorded by Bukhari and Muslim in their Sahihs (as quoted by Tabarsi, Al-Durr al-Manthur, and Qurtubi under the verses in question). In Sahih Muslim, a chapter is dedicated to this subject containing several narrations; see Sahih Muslim, Vol. 4, p. 2199. 2: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 8, p. 526.

The Imam does not say there is no morning and evening in the Resurrection; rather, he says the Fire of Hell is eternal and does not have [intermittent] morning and evening. That which has punishment in the morning and evening is the world of Barzakh. He then argues using the subsequent sentence, which speaks of the Resurrection, as evidence that the preceding sentence pertains to Barzakh.

We have discussed Barzakh and its proofs in detail in Volume 14, page 314 onwards (under verse 100 of Surah Al-Mu'minun).

Ayahs

وَإِذْ يَتَحَآجُّونَ فِى ٱلنَّارِ فَيَقُولُ ٱلضُّعَفَـٰٓؤُا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُنَّا لَكُمْ تَبَعًا فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّغْنُونَ عَنَّا نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ (٤٧)
When they argue in the Fire, the oppressed will say to the oppressors, ‘We used to follow you; will you avail us against any portion of the Fire?’ (47)
قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُلٌّ فِيهَآ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ حَكَمَ بَيْنَ ٱلْعِبَادِ (٤٨)
The oppressors will say, ‘We are all in it [together]; Allah has judged between [His] servants.’ (48)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى ٱلنَّارِ لِخَزَنَةِ جَهَنَّمَ ٱدْعُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ يُخَفِّفْ عَنَّا يَوْمًا مِّنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ (٤٩)
Those who are in the Fire will say to the keepers of hell, ‘Invoke your Lord to lighten for us [at least] a day’s punishment.’ (49)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَوَلَمْ تَكُ تَأْتِيكُمْ رُسُلُكُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ ۚ قَالُوا۟ فَٱدْعُوا۟ ۗ وَمَا دُعَـٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ (٥٠)
They will say, ‘Had not your apostles brought you manifest proofs?’ They will say, ‘Yes.’ They will say, ‘Then invoke [Him] yourselves.’ But the invocations of the faithless only go awry. (50)

Commentary

The Dispute Between the Weak and the Arrogant in Hell!

Since the Believer of the People of Pharaoh concluded his speech by drawing the attention of Pharaoh’s folk to the issue of the Resurrection and the punishment of Hell, the verses under discussion take up the thread of the discourse in this same context, reflecting scenes of the aggressive dialogue of the denizens of Hell within the heart of the Fire.

First, He says: "Recall when they dispute and argue with one another in the Fire of Hell," the "weak" say to the "arrogant": "We were your followers; will you now take from us a portion of the punishment and a share of the fire of Hell in our stead?"

(وَ إِذْ يَتَحاجُّونَ فِي النَّارِ فَيَقُولُ الضُّعَفاءُ لِلَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا إِنَّا كُنَّا لَكُمْ تَبَعاً فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُغْنُونَ عَنَّا نَصِيباً مِنَ النَّارِ) [^1] The term "weak" (ضعفا) refers to those who lacked sufficient knowledge and intellectual independence, following the leaders of disbelief with closed eyes and ears—those whom the Quran refers to as the "arrogant" (مستكبران).

Undoubtedly, these followers know there that their leaders are themselves caught in the punishment and possess not the slightest ability to defend them. But why do these followers seek refuge in them and request that they accept a portion of their punishment?! Some have said this is because they were accustomed in this world to seeking refuge in them during hardships; thus, they are subconsciously drawn to this act there as well.

However, it is better to say that this is a form of mockery, reproach, and blame toward them, so that it becomes clear that all their claims were hollow and devoid of truth. ^2 It is noteworthy that it is narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, that on one of the days of "Ghadir," he delivered a sermon. While inviting the people to Divine Monotheism, he drew their attention to the obedience of those whom Allah has commanded to be obeyed and reminded them of the above verse, then added:

"Do you know what arrogance (استکبار) is? It is abandoning obedience to those whom you were commanded to obey, and considering oneself superior to those whom you were urged to follow. The Quran speaks much of this matter; if a contemplator reflects upon it, it restrains and admonishes him!" (In reality, the Imam عليه السلام, with these vivid and clear expressions, intends to warn those who forgot the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله instructions on the day of Ghadir and followed others). [^3]

In any case, the arrogant do not remain silent in response to this speech, but they give an answer that reflects the ultimate degree of their weakness and humiliation, as the Quran points out in the next verse, saying: "The arrogant say: 'We and you are all in this fire of Hell, and we share a common fate; Allah has indeed judged between His servants with justice!'" (قالَ الَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا إِنَّا كُلٌّ فِيها إِنَّ اللَّهَ قَدْ حَكَمَ بَيْنَ الْعِبادِ).

If we could solve a problem for you, we would have solved it for ourselves. Nothing can be achieved by us here—neither averting the punishment from you nor from ourselves, nor even accepting a part of your penalty.

It is noteworthy that in verse 21 of Surah Ibrahim, this same proposal by the weak to the arrogant is mentioned, and there we read that the arrogant reply:

لَوْ هَدانَا اللَّهُ لَهَدَيْناكُمْ سَواءٌ عَلَيْنا أَ جَزِعْنا أَمْ صَبَرْنا ما لَنا مِنْ مَحِيصٍ‌:

"Had Allah guided us (to a way of escape from the punishment), we would have guided you. It is all the same for us whether we show impatience or remain patient; there is for us no place of escape!" It is evident that these two answers are not contradictory but rather complement each other.

When their hands are cut off from every means, they turn toward the keepers of Hell and the officials of punishment: "And those in the Fire will say to the keepers of Hell, 'Supplicate your Lord to lighten for us the punishment for even one day.'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ فِي النَّارِ لِخَزَنَةِ جَهَنَّمَ ادْعُوا رَبَّكُمْ يُخَفِّفْ عَنَّا يَوْماً مِنَ الْعَذابِ‌) [^4]

They know that the Divine punishment cannot be removed; their only request is that the Divine punishment be lifted from them for a single day—to get a day's reprieve, to catch their breath, and to rest a little—and they are content with just this!

But the guardians of Hell "say: 'Did there not come to you your messengers with clear proofs?'" And was the argument not sufficiently established against you? (قالُوا أَ وَ لَمْ تَكُ تَأْتِيكُمْ رُسُلُكُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ‌). In response, "they say: 'Yes!'" (قالُوا بَلى‌).

But the keepers of Hell say to them: "Since it is so, supplicate as much as you wish, but know that the supplication of the disbelievers leads nowhere and is wasted and lost!" (قالُوا فَادْعُوا وَ ما دُعاءُ الْكافِرِينَ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ‌).

You yourselves admit that the Divine messengers came with clear proofs, but you paid them no heed and disbelieved. Therefore, no matter how much you supplicate, it will be of no use, for Allah does not accept the supplication of the disbelievers.

A group of commentators has said regarding the interpretation of the final sentence that it means: "You yourselves supplicate, for we cannot supplicate without the permission of the Lord," implying that when we do not have such permission, you should know that the doors of salvation are closed to you. It is true that the disbeliever becomes a believer on the Day of Resurrection, but this faith does not diminish any of the effects of his disbelief; hence, the name "disbeliever" still applies to him.

However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

[^1]: Some have imagined that the pronoun in "يتحاجون" refers to the folk of Pharaoh, but the existing context in these verses shows that the verse has a broad meaning and includes all disbelievers.

[^3]: "Misbah al-Shaykh" (as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 526). [^4]: "خزنه" is the plural of "خازن", meaning protector and guardian.

Ayahs

إِنَّا لَنَنصُرُ رُسُلَنَا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَيَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلْأَشْهَـٰدُ (٥١)
Indeed We shall help Our apostles and those who have faith in the life of the world and on the day when the witnesses rise up, (51)
يَوْمَ لَا يَنفَعُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مَعْذِرَتُهُمْ ۖ وَلَهُمُ ٱللَّعْنَةُ وَلَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلدَّارِ (٥٢)
the day when the excuses of the wrongdoers will not benefit them, the curse will lie on them, and for them will be the ills of the [ultimate] abode. (52)
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَأَوْرَثْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ (٥٣)
Certainly We gave guidance to Moses and We gave the Children of Israel the Book as a legacy, (53)
هُدًى وَذِكْرَىٰ لِأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (٥٤)
as guidance and admonition for those who possess intellect. (54)
فَٱصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنۢبِكَ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ وَٱلْإِبْكَـٰرِ (٥٥)
So be patient! Allah’s promise is indeed true. Plead [Allah] for forgiveness of your sin, and celebrate the praise of your Lord morning and evening. (55)

Commentary

We Support the Believers

Since the previous verses discussed the arguments and dialogues of the denizens of Hell—who can neither help one another nor find anyone to rush to their aid—and since the verses before those mentioned the "Believer from the Family of Pharaoh," that peerless mujahid and fighter, and God's support for him, the verses under discussion state His support for the Prophets and the believers in this world and the Hereafter as a general law.

He says: "Indeed, We will surely support Our messengers and those who have believed during the life of this world and on the Day when the witnesses will stand" (إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ).

This is an unsparing support, emphasized with various forms of reinforcement; a support that is unconditional and, for this reason, brings about all kinds of victories. This includes victory in logic and discourse, victory in wars, the sending of divine punishment upon opponents to destroy them, or unseen aids (imdad al-ghaybi) that strengthen the heart and make the soul powerful and robust through Divine grace.

Here, we encounter a new expression regarding the Day of Resurrection: "يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ" (the Day when the witnesses stand).

"Ashhad" (أَشْهاد) is the plural of "Shahid" (شاهد) or "Shaheed" (شهيد) (just as "Ashraf" is the plural of "Sharif"). In any case, it means "witnesses."

There are various interpretations as to who these witnesses are, all of which can be integrated:

  1. It refers to the angels who monitor human deeds.
  2. It refers to the Prophets, who are witnesses over their nations.
  3. It refers to the angels, Prophets, and believers who are witnesses to human actions.

However, the possibility that the limbs of the human body are included in this specific term seems unlikely, because although the word "Ashhad" has a broad meaning, the expression "the Day when the witnesses stand" (يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ) does not quite fit them.

This expression points to an interesting nuance: it intends to say that on the Day when all creation is gathered, the witnesses will stand in that great assembly. Disgrace there is the worst of disgraces, and victory there is the supreme victory. On that Day, We shall help Our believers and messengers and increase their honor in that great presence.

But that day is the day of disgrace and misery for the disbelievers and the oppressors, as the next verse adds: "The Day when their excuse will not benefit the wrongdoers, and for them is the curse, and for them is the evil home" (يَوْمَ لا يَنْفَعُ الظَّالِمِينَ مَعْذِرَتُهُمْ وَ لَهُمُ اللَّعْنَةُ وَ لَهُمْ سُوءُ الدَّارِ).

On one hand, their apologizing before the witnesses will lead nowhere, and disgrace in that great assembly will overtake them.

On the other hand, they are far from Allah's mercy, and the "curse"—which is spiritual distance from mercy—will seize them.

And thirdly, from a physical standpoint, they are in torture and punishment, in the worst of places: the fire of Hell!

Question

A significant question arises here: If Allah has emphatically promised victory to the Prophets and the believers, why then throughout history have we witnessed the mass slaughter of Prophets and believers at the hands of faithless disbelievers? Why were they sometimes severely cornered? Or why did they witness military defeats? Can the Divine promise be broken?!

The answer to this question becomes clear by noting one point: the scale many people use to evaluate the concept of "victory" is very limited. They consider victory only as pushing back the enemy and holding power for a few days.

They do not take into account victory in the objective and the superiority of the school of thought. They do not consider a martyred mujahid becoming a role model for present and future generations. They regard as nothing the honor and pride in the eyes of all the free people of the world and the attainment of Allah's pleasure and satisfaction.

Naturally, in such a limited evaluation, this objection has no answer. But if we broaden our vision, open the horizon of our thoughts, and consider real values, we will then realize the deep meaning of the verse.

"Sayyid Qutb" in the commentary "Fi Zilal" has a statement here that serves as a valuable witness to this point. He gives the example of the hero of the field of Karbala, the beloved Husayn عليه السلام, and says:

"Husayn (may Allah be pleased with him) drank the draught of martyrdom in a scene that was great on one hand and painful on the other. Was this victory or defeat? On a small scale and in outward appearance, it was defeat; but in the face of pure truth and large scales, it is considered a great victory.

For every martyr on earth, the pure hearts of humans tremble, love and emotions are stirred, and zeal and sacrifice are set in motion within souls, just as Husayn (may Allah be pleased with him) did.

This is a statement upon which both Shias and non-Shias among other Muslims, and even a large group of non-Muslims, agree and hold the same conviction.

How many martyrs there are who, had they lived for a thousand years, could not have helped their creed and school of thought as much as their martyrdom did. They would not have had the power to leave so many great human concepts as a legacy in hearts, nor to compel thousands of people toward great deeds through their final words written with their blood.

Yes, these words and final sermons written in the script of blood remain eternally alive, moving future children and generations, and perhaps influencing the entirety of history throughout the centuries and ages." [^1]

We must add to this statement that we Shias see with our own eyes every year the signs of the life of the beloved Imam Husayn عليه السلام and his martyred companions in Karbala. What great movements do their mourning sessions originate?! We were witnesses and observers of the movement of millions of awakened Muslims during the days of Husayni Ashura to uproot the palace of oppression, tyranny, and colonialism.

We saw with our own eyes how this self-sacrificing generation, who had learned their lesson in the school of Imam

[^1]: "Tafsir Fi Zilal," Vol. 7, p. 189.

Husayn عليه السلام and his memorial gatherings and the days of Ashura, pulled down the most powerful tyrannical monarchs from their thrones with hands empty of any weapons.

Yes, we saw with our own eyes how the blood of Husayn flowed in their veins and disrupted all the political and military calculations of the superpowers.

Was it not the victory of Husayn عليه السلام and his companions that they could demonstrate such power after thirteen centuries?

Another Question

Another question is raised here: the above verse says that on the Day of Resurrection, the wrongdoers' excuses are not effective, while in Surah Al-Mursalat, verse 36, we read that on that Day they are not even permitted to offer excuses: "Nor will it be permitted for them to make an excuse" (وَ لا يُؤْذَنُ لَهُمْ فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ). How are these two reconciled?

In response, two points should be noted: First, the Day of Resurrection has various stages (stations) whose conditions differ. In some stations, the tongue ceases to function, and the hands, feet, and limbs speak and bear witness. In other stations, the tongue is loosened and the person speaks (as evidenced by verse 65 of Surah Ya-Sin on one hand, and the previous verses of this Surah regarding the dialogues and disputes of the denizens of Hell on the other).

Therefore, there is no contradiction in them not being permitted to excuse themselves in some stations, while in other stations they offer excuses but to no avail.

The second point is that sometimes a person speaks, but it is useless and futile. In such cases, it is as if they have not spoken at all. Therefore, the phrase "they will not be permitted to make an excuse" might mean that their excusing themselves is futile.

Then, the Quran mentions one of the instances of supporting the messengers and their victory over enemies under Divine protection, saying: "And We certainly gave Moses guidance, and We caused the Children of Israel to inherit the Scripture (the Torah)" (وَ لَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْهُدى‌ وَ أَوْرَثْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْكِتابَ‌).

The "guidance" that Allah bestowed upon Moses has a broad meaning that includes the station of prophethood and revelation, the Heavenly Book (the Torah), the guidance given to him in the path of fulfilling his mission, and the miracles placed at his disposal.

The use of the term "inheritance" regarding the "Torah" is because the Children of Israel received it generation after generation. They could benefit from it without having toiled for it, much like ordinary inheritances that come into a person's possession without labor—even though they wasted this great Divine legacy.

In the next verse, it adds: "As a guide and a reminder for those of understanding" (هُدىً وَ ذِكْرى‌ لِأُولِي الْأَلْبابِ‌). [^1]

The difference between "guidance" (huda) and "reminder" (dhikra) is that guidance is at the beginning of the process, while "reminder" serves as a recollection of matters that a person has previously heard and believed in but which have faded from the page of their memory. In other words, Heavenly Books are both the initiators of guidance and its continuators.

However, both at the beginning and in the continuation of the process, the real benefit is reaped by "those of understanding" (ulu al-albab) and the possessors of intellect, not the stubborn ignorant or the blind and deaf fanatics.

In the final verse under discussion, He gives three important commands to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, which are in fact general and universal commands, even though the addressee is the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

First, He says: "So be patient; indeed, the promise of Allah is truth" (فَاصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌ‌).

Be patient against the enmity and stubbornness of the foes and their sabotage.

Be patient against the ignorance of a group of friends, their lethargy, negligence, and occasional harassment.

And be patient against the inclinations of the self, rebellious desires, and anger.

In short, the key to your victory in all fields is patience and steadfastness.

Know that Allah's promise regarding your victory and that of your Ummah is unbreakable. This faith in the truth of the Divine promise will make you encouraged and steadfast in your path and will make enduring hardships easy for you and the believers.

In the Holy Quran, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is commanded to be patient many times. Sometimes it is in an absolute sense, like the verse under discussion and some other verses, and sometimes it is mentioned in specific cases, such as verses 39 and 40 of Surah Qaf: "So be patient over what they say" (فَاصْبِرْ عَلى‌ ما يَقُولُونَ)—meaning against the unworthy attributions they make to you. Elsewhere it says: "And keep yourself patient [by being] with those who call upon their Lord morning and evening..." (Al-Kahf: 28).

All the victories achieved by the Prophet and the early Muslims were under the shadow of this very patience and steadfastness, and today as well, overcoming numerous enemies and great problems is impossible without it.

In the second command, He says: "And ask forgiveness for your sin" (وَ اسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنْبِكَ).

It is certain that the Prophet, due to the station of infallibility (Ismah), did not commit any sin. However, as we have said, such expressions in the Holy Quran regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and other Prophets عليهم السلام refer to "relative sins." This is because sometimes actions that are considered worship and good deeds for ordinary people are considered "sins" for the great Prophets, for "the good deeds of the righteous are the misdeeds of the near ones" (hasanat al-abrar sayyi'at al-muqarrabin).

A single moment of inattention or even a "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla) is not befitting for them. Because of their lofty station and high level of gnosis, they must be free from all such matters, and whenever such occurs, they seek forgiveness for it.

However, the view of some who say this refers to the sins of the Ummah, or sins others committed against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, or that seeking forgiveness here is merely a ritualistic act of devotion, seems unlikely.

In the final command, He says: "And exalt [Allah] with praise of your Lord in the evening and the morning" (وَ سَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَ الْإِبْكارِ).

"Ashi" (عشى) means from the afternoon until before sunset, and "Ibkar" (ابكار) refers to the time between dawn and sunrise.

It is possible that "Ashi" and "Ibkar" refer to two specific times—evening and morning—when a person is prepared for Divine praise and glorification, as they have not yet started their daily work or have just finished it.

It is also possible that it means the continuity of praise and glorification throughout the entire day and night; it is a common expression to say, for example, "Watch over him morning and evening," meaning always.

Some have interpreted this praise and glorification as referring to the morning and afternoon prayers, or all five daily prayers, while the literal expression of the verse has a broader concept than that, and the prayers can be just one instance of it.

In any case, these three comprehensive commands are in the field of self-building and preparation for victories under the shadow of Divine grace. This is the provision on the path to reaching great goals.

Before everything else: endurance and patience against hardships and obstacles; then, purifying the page of the heart from the rust of sin and every pollution; and after that, adorning it with the remembrance of the Lord—an adornment that includes "Tasbih" (glorification) and "Hamd" (praise), meaning declaring Allah free from any defect or deficiency and praising Him for every beauty and perfection.

A praise and glorification that is directed toward the Creator, but its light also falls upon the heart of the creature, purifying him from defects and adorning him with perfect attributes.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَـٰنٍ أَتَىٰهُمْ ۙ إِن فِى صُدُورِهِمْ إِلَّا كِبْرٌ مَّا هُم بِبَـٰلِغِيهِ ۚ فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ (٥٦)
Indeed those who dispute the signs of Allah without any authority that may have come to them—there is [an ambition for] greatness in their breasts, which they will never attain. So seek the protection of Allah; indeed, He is the All-hearing, the All-seeing. (56)
لَخَلْقُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٥٧)
The creation of the heavens and the earth is surely more prodigious than the creation of mankind, but most people do not know. (57)
وَمَا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَلَا ٱلْمُسِىٓءُ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٥٨)
The blind one and the seer are not equal, neither are the evildoers and those who have faith and do righteous deeds. Little is the admonition that you take! (58)
إِنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ لَـَٔاتِيَةٌ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهَا وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٩)
Indeed the Hour is bound to come; there is no doubt in it. But most people do not believe. (59)

Commentary

The "Blind" and the "Seeing" are Not Equal, Nor are the "Disbeliever" and the "Believer"

In the preceding verses, Allah invited the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to patience and steadfastness in the face of opponents, their discordant tunes, and their sinister conspiracies. The verses under discussion explain the motives behind their disputes and contentions against the Truth.

In the first verse, He says: "Indeed, those who dispute concerning the signs of Allah without [any] authority having come to them—there is nothing in their breasts except pride" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُجادِلُونَ فِي آياتِ اللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطانٍ أَتاهُمْ إِنْ فِي صُدُورِهِمْ إِلَّا كِبْرٌ).

"Mujadalah" (dispute)—as we have previously noted—means contention and stubbornness in speech and illogical arguments. Although it is sometimes used in a broad sense encompassing both false and truthful dialogue, the expression "without [any] authority having come to them" serves as an emphasis on what is usually understood from the meaning of "dispute," for "Sultan" means a proof or evidence that grants a person authority over the opposing party.

The expression "having come to them" refers to proofs revealed by Allah through revelation; since revelation is the most reliable way to establish truths, it is emphasized here.

The "signs of Allah" (Ayat Allah) concerning which they disputed were the "miracles and verses of the Quran" and discussions regarding the Origin and the Resurrection. They would sometimes call them sorcery, sometimes a sign of madness, and at other times "tales of the ancients" (Asatir al-Awwalin)! Thus, the verse is a living witness to the fact that the primary source of dispute is pride, arrogance, and self-centeredness. Arrogant and self-centered individuals, due to their intense self-love, pay no heed to others; they consider their own thoughts as the truth and the views of others—whatever they may be—as falsehood. Therefore, they persist in their false speech.

The use of "In" (nothing but) indicates that the exclusive factor in such cases is pride and a sense of superiority; otherwise, how could a person persist so much in their speech without any evidence or document?

"Sudur" (breasts) here refers to the hearts, and by heart is meant the soul, spirit, and intellect, a meaning frequently used in the verses of the Quran.

Some commentators have interpreted "Kibr" (pride) in the above verse as "Hasad" (envy), considering the motive for their dispute to be their envy toward the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his spiritual and apparent status. However, linguistically, pride does not carry this meaning, though it may be its concomitant, as arrogant and self-centered individuals are usually envious as well; they want all blessings for themselves and are troubled by others benefiting from them.

Then it adds: "They will never reach it [their goal]" (ما هُمْ بِبالِغِيهِ).

Their goal is to see themselves as great, to boast, and to rule over society, but they shall gain nothing but humiliation and being subordinates. They will neither reach the goal they seek through pride and arrogance, nor the goal they have for their false and baseless disputes—which is to nullify the Truth and establish falsehood [1].

[1]: Regarding the referent of the pronoun in "balighih" (reaching it), commentators have offered two possibilities: First, that it refers to "Kibr" (pride), as the phrase "ma hum bi-balighih" is a description for "Kibr," and the meaning of the sentence becomes that they do not reach the requirement and goal of their pride (in fact, a "mudaf" is omitted here—"they are not reachers of the requirement of their pride"). Second, that the pronoun refers to "Jidal" (dispute), which is understood from the verb "yujadilun," meaning they do not reach the goal of their false dispute, which is the nullification of the Truth. However, in this case, the sentence cannot be an attribute of "Kibr" and must be conjoined to the preceding sentences with the conjunction omitted.

At the end of the verse, it commands the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to seek refuge in Allah from the evil of such arrogant, selfish, and illogical individuals, saying: "So seek refuge in Allah. Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Seeing" (فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللَّهِ إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ).

He hears their baseless words and sees their conspiracies and ugly deeds.

Not only the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), but all wayfarers of the path of Truth must entrust themselves to Allah during the storms of events and in the face of illogical contenders.

Thus, Yusuf (عليه السلام), the great Prophet of God, when faced with the fierce storm of Zulaikha's desires, said: "I seek refuge in Allah! Indeed, He is my Lord, who has made my stay honorable" (مَعاذَ اللَّهِ إِنَّهُ رَبِّي أَحْسَنَ مَثْوايَ). And in the previous verses of this same Surah, we read from the tongue of Musa (عليه السلام): "Indeed, I have sought refuge in my Lord and your Lord from every arrogant one who does not believe in the Day of Account" (إِنِّي عُذْتُ بِرَبِّي وَ رَبِّكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ مُتَكَبِّرٍ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِيَوْمِ الْحِسابِ) (Ghafir: 27).

Since one of the major points of dispute by the disbelievers against the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) was the issue of the Resurrection and the reviving of humans after death, in the next verse, He mentions the issue of Resurrection with a clear statement: "The creation of the heavens and the earth is greater than the creation of mankind, but most of the people do not know" (لَخَلْقُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ النَّاسِ وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ).

He who has the power to create these massive celestial bodies and vast, expansive galaxies with all that grandeur, and to manage and govern them—how could He be incapable of reviving the dead? It is the ignorance of a group of people that does not allow them to perceive these truths.

Most commentators have considered this verse a response to the polytheists' dispute regarding the "Resurrection" [1], but some have also suggested it is a response to the pride of the arrogant ones who thought highly of themselves and their narrow thoughts, while they were nothing more than an insignificant particle compared to the grandeur of the universe. This meaning is not far from the context of the verses, but considering the subsequent verses, the first meaning seems more appropriate.

In any case, this verse mentions another factor of false "dispute," which is "ignorance," whereas the previous verses discussed "pride." These two are closely related, for the source of "pride" is "ignorance" and a lack of recognition of one's own worth or the extent of one's knowledge.

In the next verse, in a clear comparison, He clarifies the state of these ignorant arrogant ones against the aware believers, saying: "The blind and the seeing are not equal" (وَ ما يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى‌ وَ الْبَصِيرُ). "Nor are those who believe and do righteous deeds [equal] to the evildoer" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَ لَا الْمُسِي‌ءُ) [1]. But you, due to selfishness and ignorance, "little do you remember" (قَلِيلًا ما تَتَذَكَّرُونَ) [2].

The blind is that ignorant and unaware person upon whose eyes the veils of pride and arrogance have fallen, preventing him from perceiving truths. The seeing is one who observes the Truth in the light of knowledge and logical reasoning. Are these two equal?! This is regarding faith and belief. As for action, how could "righteous believers" be equal to "sinful evildoers"? In fact, the first comparison is in terms of recognition and knowledge, and the second comparison is in terms of its reflection in their actions.

Yes, the seeing perceive both their own smallness and the grandeur of the world around them, and for this reason, they are aware of their position and worth. But the "blind" neither knows his position in time and space nor sees the world around him; therefore, he always errs in evaluating his own existence and becomes trapped in pride and arrogance, and this very pride and arrogance drive him toward evildoing.

This point may also be derived from the two sentences of the above verse in connection with each other: that faith and righteous deeds make the eye of the heart see, while disbelief and ugly deeds make a person blind and strip him of the sense to distinguish truth from falsehood.

[1]: The phrasing of the verses might initially suggest that "la" (not) in the phrase "wa la al-musi'" should not be mentioned, but the emphasis on negation on one hand, and the fact that this phrase is the primary objective on the other, necessitated the repetition of the negation, especially since the length of the sentence sometimes causes one to forget the negation mentioned at its beginning. [2]: "Ma" in the phrase "qalilan ma tatadhakkarun" is extra (za'idah) and is for emphasis.

In the final verse under discussion, He informs of the occurrence of the Resurrection with decisiveness and clarity, saying: "Indeed, the Hour is coming—no doubt about it—but most of the people do not believe" (إِنَّ السَّاعَةَ لَآتِيَةٌ لا رَيْبَ فِيها وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ).

The word "Inna" (Indeed), the "Lam" in "la-atiyah," and the phrase "la rayba fiha" (no doubt in it) are all repeated emphases on the content of this sentence, which is the establishment of the Resurrection. Since many proofs for this great Resurrection have been established in the verses of the Quran, in some instances, it is discussed and passed over as a definitive matter without mentioning the proof.

"Sa'ah" (the Hour), according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, originally means "a portion of the parts of time," and because the occurrence of the Resurrection and the accounting of humans on that day will happen swiftly, it is referred to as "the Hour."

This expression is used dozens of times in the Holy Quran with this same meaning; however, it is sometimes used for the Resurrection itself, and sometimes for the end of the world and the preliminaries of the Resurrection. Since both are linked and both occur suddenly, both are referred to as "the Hour" [1]. As for the statement "most of the people do not believe," it is not because the issue of the Resurrection is a hidden or ambiguous matter; rather, one of the major causes for denying the Resurrection is the desire for freedom in the unconditional enjoyment of the world and every kind of whim and passion. Furthermore, long-term worldly desires prevent a person from thinking about the Resurrection and expressing faith in it.

[1]: Regarding the naming of the Resurrection as "the Hour," we have provided a more detailed discussion under verse 12 of Surah Ar-Rum (Volume 16, page 380).

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 20, p. 143

Key Point: The Arrogant Jews!

Some commentators have mentioned a "circumstance of revelation" (Shan al-Nuzul) for the first of the above verses, the summary of which is: The Jews used to say, "Soon the 'Messiah, the Dajjal' will appear, and we will help him to crush Muhammad and his companions, and we will be relieved of them!" [1]. This statement can have two meanings: First, that they wanted to express the appearance of the "Messiah" and his victory over the "Dajjal" whom they were expecting, considering the Messiah as one of their own and—Allah forbid—applying the term Dajjal to the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله).

Second, that they were truly waiting for the "Dajjal" himself and considered him one of their own.

For the word "Masih" (Messiah), as Raghib noted in Al-Mufradat and Ibn Manzur in Lisan al-Arab, is applied both to Prophet Isa (عليه السلام)—due to his traveling on the earth or because he would "wipe" (mas-h) the sick with his hand and heal them by Allah's command—and it is also applied to the Dajjal because he has only one eye, and the place of his other eye is "mamsuh" (wiped) and smooth.

It is possible that the Jews, out of extreme anger due to successive defeats after the appearance of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), were truly waiting for the Dajjal—that lying and deceptive man—to join him and rid themselves of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his companions! Or that they were waiting for the Messiah (عليه السلام), as it is understood from the Holy Bible that not only Christians but also Jews are waiting for the appearance of the Messiah; they believe that the "Messiah" will fight the "Dajjal" and crush him, and they wanted to apply this belief to the appearance of Islam!

In any case, some have taken the existence of this circumstance of revelation for the above verse as evidence that this verse and the one following it were revealed in Medina, unlike the other verses of this Surah which are all Meccan. However, since the basis of this circumstance of revelation is not established, and furthermore, its content is ambiguous, this conclusion is not acceptable.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 528, under the verses discussed.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ رَبُّكُمُ ٱدْعُونِىٓ أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِى سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ دَاخِرِينَ (٦٠)
Your Lord has said, ‘Call Me, and I will hear you!’ Indeed those who are disdainful of My worship will enter hell in utter humiliation. (60)
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ لِتَسْكُنُوا۟ فِيهِ وَٱلنَّهَارَ مُبْصِرًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٦١)
It is Allah who made the night for you, that you may rest in it, and the day to provide visibility. Indeed Allah is gracious to mankind, but most people do not give thanks. (61)
ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ (٦٢)
That is Allah, your Lord, the creator of all things, there is no god except Him. Then where do you stray? (62)
كَذَٰلِكَ يُؤْفَكُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ (٦٣)
Those who were used to impugning the signs of Allah are thus made to go astray. (63)

Commentary

Call Upon Me, I Will Answer You!

Since the previous verses contained threats against the faithless, the arrogant, and the conceited, these verses blend those warnings with grace and kindness, opening the embrace of His mercy to the repentant. First, He says: "Your Lord has said: 'Call upon Me, I will answer you'" (وَ قالَ رَبُّكُمُ ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ).

Many commentators have interpreted "supplication" (du'a) and "calling" here in its well-known conventional sense. The phrase "I will answer you" (أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ), as well as numerous narrations mentioned under this verse regarding supplication and its rewards—which we will refer to later—also attest to this meaning.

Meanwhile, some others, following the famous commentator Ibn Abbas, have suggested the possibility that "supplication" here means monotheism (Tawhid) and the worship of the Lord; meaning, "Worship Me and acknowledge My Oneness." However, the first interpretation is more apparent.

In any case, several points can be derived from the above verse:

  1. Supplication is beloved by Allah and is His own desire.

  2. A promise of fulfillment is given following the supplication, but we know this promise is conditional and not absolute. A prayer reaches the goal of fulfillment only when the necessary conditions regarding the "supplication," the "supplicant," and the "subject requested" are met. We have explained this subject in detail, along with the philosophy of "prayer and supplication" and its "true concept," under verse 186 of Surah Al-Baqarah, and there is no need for repetition. [^1]

  3. Supplication is itself a form of worship, as the term "worship" ('ibadah) is applied to it in the latter part of the verse.

At the end of the verse, He issues a severe threat against those who refrain from supplication, saying: "Indeed, those who disdain My worship will soon enter Hell in humiliation" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِي سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ داخِرِينَ). [^2]

The Importance of Supplication and Conditions for Fulfillment

Numerous narrations transmitted from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the other great leaders عليه السلام fully clarify the importance of supplication:

  1. In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated: "الدعاء هو العبادة" "Supplication is worship." [^3]

  2. In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that one of his companions asked: "What do you say regarding two men who both entered the mosque; one performed more prayers (Salah), and the other performed more supplication (Du'a)? Which of the two is superior?" He replied: "Both are good."

The questioner asked again: "I know both are good, but which one is superior?"

The Imam replied: "The one who supplicates more is superior. Have you not heard the words of Allah the Almighty: 'Call upon Me, I will answer you; indeed, those who disdain My worship will soon enter Hell in humiliation'?"

He then added: "It is the greatest worship" (هى العبادة الكبرى). [^1]

  1. In another hadith narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, in response to the question of which worship is superior, he said: "There is nothing superior in the sight of Allah than that He be asked and requested for what is with Him. And no one is more hated and loathed by Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, than those who are too arrogant for His worship and do not ask for what is with Him!" [^2]

  2. In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated: "There are stations with Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, that cannot be reached except through supplication: 'Indeed, with Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, is a station that is not attained except by asking. If a servant were to close his mouth and not ask, he would not be given anything. So ask, and you shall be given; for indeed, there is no door that is knocked upon except that it is about to be opened for its seeker!'" [^3]

  3. In some narrations, supplicating is considered even superior to reciting the Quran, as narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, who said: "Supplication is superior to the recitation of the Quran" (الدعاء افضل من قراءة القران). [^4]

Through a brief analysis, one can reach the depth of the content of these hadiths, because supplication, on one hand, invites man toward the recognition of the Lord (Ma'rifat Allah), which is the greatest asset of any human being.

On the other hand, it causes him to see himself as needy of Him and to humble himself before Him, descending from the mount of pride and arrogance—which is the source of all kinds of miseries and disputation regarding the signs of Allah—and to claim no independent existence for himself before His Pure Essence.

Thirdly, it leads him to see all blessings as coming from Him, to love Him, and through this, his emotional bond with His Holy Presence becomes strengthened.

Fourthly, since he sees himself as needy and indebted to Allah's blessings, he considers himself obligated to obey His command.

Fifthly, since he knows that the fulfillment of this supplication is not unconditional, but rather requires sincerity of intention, purity of heart, repentance from sin, and fulfilling the needs of the needy and friends as its conditions, he engages in self-improvement and walks the path of self-education.

Sixthly, supplication gives him self-confidence, prevents him from despair and hopelessness, and invites him to greater effort and striving. [^5]

A "key point" that must be reminded at the end of this concise discussion is that, according to Islamic narrations, supplication is specifically for cases where human efforts and strivings do not yield results, or in other words, man should do whatever is within his power and ask Allah for the rest.

Therefore, if a person makes supplication a substitute for effort and striving, it will certainly not be answered.

Thus, in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "There are four groups whose supplication is not answered: A man sitting in his house saying: 'O Allah, provide for me,' so it is said to him: 'Did I not command you to seek?' And a man who had a wife (who constantly troubled him) and he prayed against her, so it is said to him: 'Did I not place her matter in your hands (the right of divorce)?' And a man who had wealth and wasted it, then says: 'O Allah, provide for me,' so it is said to him: 'Did I not command you to be economical? Did I not command you to manage wealth properly?' And a man who had wealth and lent it to another without witnesses, (but the borrower denied it, and he prays: 'O Allah, soften his heart and make him pay the debt'), so it is said to him: 'Did I not command you to take witnesses when lending?'" [^6]

It is clear that in all these cases, the person did not exercise the necessary effort and prudence and became entangled in the consequences; in the face of this negligence, shortcoming, and abandonment of effort, his supplication will not be answered.

From here, one of the reasons for the non-fulfillment of many supplications becomes clear, as a group of people want to abandon necessary effort and take refuge in supplication; such prayers are not answered—this is a Divine Sunnah (law).

Of course, the non-fulfillment of some supplications has other causes and factors as well, including the fact that man often errs in identifying his own interests and harms. Sometimes he asks Allah with his whole being for something that is in no way in his best interest; he may even become aware of this later. This is exactly like a patient or a child asking their caregivers for colorful foods which, if granted, would increase their illness or even endanger their life. In such cases, the Compassionate and Merciful Allah does not grant the supplication and stores it as a treasure for his hereafter.

Furthermore, the fulfillment of supplication has conditions mentioned in the verses of the Quran and Islamic narrations, which we have discussed in detail in the first volume of this Tafsir (Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 186).

Obstacles to the Fulfillment of Supplication

In some narrations, various sins are mentioned as obstacles to the fulfillment of supplication, including: evil intention, hypocrisy, delaying prayer (Salah) past its time, foul language, consuming forbidden (haram) food, and abandoning charity and spending in the way of Allah. [^7]

We conclude this discussion with a meaningful hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: The late Tabarsi in "Al-Ihtijaj" narrates from him: "He was asked: 'Does Allah not say: "Call upon Me, I will answer you"? Yet we see the desperate person calling Him and not being answered, and the oppressed seeking His help against his enemy but He does not help him.' The Imam replied: 'Woe to you! No one calls Him except that He answers him. As for the oppressor, his supplication is rejected until he repents. As for the one on the right, when he supplicates, He answers him and averts calamity from him in ways he does not even know, or He stores for him a great reward for the day of his need (the Day of Resurrection). And if the matter the servant asked for is not good for him, He withholds it from him.'" [^8]

Since supplication and requesting from Allah is a branch of the knowledge of Allah, in the next verse He speaks of realities that raise the level of human knowledge and increase one of the conditions of supplication, which is hope for fulfillment.

He says: "Allah is He who made for you the night that you may rest therein" (اللَّهُ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ لِتَسْكُنُوا فِيهِ).

For the darkness of the night, on one hand, causes the forced cessation of daily programs, and on the other hand, darkness itself is soothing and a source of rest for the body, nerves, and soul, while light is the source of stir and movement.

Therefore, He adds immediately after: "and the day visible and bright" (وَ النَّهارَ مُبْصِراً).

So that it may illuminate the living environment of humans and prepare it for every kind of activity.

It is noteworthy that "mubsir" means "seeing," and describing the day as "seeing" is actually a type of emphasis and hyperbole in making the people see. [^9]

Then He adds: "Indeed, Allah is full of bounty to mankind, yet most people do not give thanks" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَشْكُرُونَ).

This precise system of night and day, and the alternating program of light and darkness, is one of the examples of the Lord's bounty and grace upon His servants, and is among the vital factors for the life of humans and living creatures.

If there were no light, there would be no life, existence, or movement; and if there were no alternating darkness, the intensity of light would exhaust, weaken, and wear out all creatures, and would scorch and destroy plants. Yet most people pass by these great Divine blessings heedlessly and do not offer Him thanks.

Interestingly, the rule would dictate that instead of the second "mankind" (al-nas), a pronoun should be used, saying "but most of them do not give thanks," but the mention of the word "mankind" instead of a pronoun is perhaps an indication that the nature of man (the unrefined) is to be ungrateful for blessings, as we also read in verse 34 of Surah Ibrahim: "Indeed, man is very unjust and ungrateful" (إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ). [^10]

But if a person has a seeing eye and a knowing heart to see the table of Allah's unstinting blessings spread everywhere, and observes His countless rain of mercy reaching every place, he involuntarily opens his tongue in thanks and praise of Him, seeing himself as small and indebted before all this greatness and mercy. [^11]

The next verse begins with the Oneness of the Lordship (Tawhid al-Rububiyyah) of the Lord and ends with the Oneness of His Creatorship and Lordship. It says: "That is Allah, your Lord, the One who has bestowed all these blessings upon you" (ذلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ). "The same Allah who is the Creator of all things" (خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ). "There is no deity except Him" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ).

In reality, the existence of abundant Divine blessings is a proof of His Lordship and Governance. And being the Creator of all things is another proof of His Oneness in Lordship, because the Creator of beings is their Owner and Sustainer. For we know that Allah's Creatorship does not mean that He creates beings and then steps aside; rather, moment by moment, the grace of existence is bestowed from His side upon all beings in the universe, and such Creatorship cannot be separated from Lordship.

Naturally, only such a Being is worthy of worship and Divinity. Therefore, the phrase "Creator of all things" (خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ) serves as a proof for "That is Allah, your Lord" (ذلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ), and "There is no deity except Him" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ) serves as its conclusion (take note).

At the end of the verse, He adds: "How then are you deluded?" (فَأَنَّى تُؤْفَكُونَ). [^12] And why do you turn from the worship of the One and Only Allah toward idols?! Note that "tu'fakun" is in the passive voice, meaning "you are being turned away" from the path of truth, as if the idolaters are so devoid of will that they have no choice of their own in this path.

In the last verse under discussion, as an explanation and emphasis of the previous points, He says: "Thus were those who used to reject the signs of Allah turned away" (كَذلِكَ يُؤْفَكُ الَّذِينَ كانُوا بِآياتِ اللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ).

"Yajhadun" is from the root "jahd," which originally means to deny something that exists in the heart; that is, a person believes in something yet denies it, or believes in its negation but affirms it with the tongue. Miserly and uncharitable people who often profess poverty are called "jahd," and "ard jahdah" means land with little vegetation. [^13]

Some other lexicographers have also interpreted "jahd" and "juhud" as follows: "Juhud is denial coupled with knowledge" (الجحود الانكار مع العلم). [^14]

Therefore, in the concept of "jahd," there is always a kind of stubbornness or malice against the truth. Naturally, anyone who encounters realities with such a trait will have no fate other than deviation from the path of truth, because as long as a person is not a seeker of truth and submissive to facts, he will not reach them.

For this reason, reaching the truth requires prior self-improvement, and this is the very "piety before faith" that the Quran refers to in Surah Al-Baqarah, saying: "This is the Book about which there is no doubt, a guidance for the pious" (ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ).
