Ayahs

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ قَرَارًا وَٱلسَّمَآءَ بِنَآءً وَصَوَّرَكُمْ فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ وَرَزَقَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ ۖ فَتَبَارَكَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦٤)
It is Allah who made for you the earth an abode and the sky a canopy. He formed you and perfected your forms, and provided you with all the good things. That is Allah, your Lord! Blessed is Allah, Lord of all the worlds! (64)
هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَٱدْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ ۗ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦٥)
He is the Living One, there is no god except Him. So supplicate Him, putting exclusive faith in Him. All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds. (65)
۞ قُلْ إِنِّى نُهِيتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ لَمَّا جَآءَنِىَ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ مِن رَّبِّى وَأُمِرْتُ أَنْ أُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦٦)
Say, ‘I have been forbidden to worship those whom you invoke besides Allah, as manifest proofs have come to me from my Lord, and I have been commanded to submit to the Lord of all the worlds.’ (66)

Commentary

This is Your Lord

These verses continue the discussion regarding the great divine blessings and their encompassment of His servants, in order to grant them both greater gnosis (recognition) and increased hope, so that in its light, they may attain the station of supplication and benefit from its fulfillment.

Interestingly, while the previous verses spoke of "temporal blessings"—namely night and day—here the discourse shifts to "spatial blessings," meaning the dwelling place of the earth and the elevated roof of the sky. It says: "It is Allah who made for you the earth a place of settlement and stability" (اللَّهُ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ قَراراً).

Indeed, He has created all the conditions necessary for a secure and peaceful dwelling on the planet Earth. It is a stable place, free from any agitation, harmonious with the structure of the human soul and body, possessing diverse resources, containing all the means required by man, vast, accessible, and provided without cost.

Then it adds: "And the sky as a ceiling and a dome above your heads" (وَ السَّماءَ بِناءً).

"Bina" (بناء), as Ibn Manzur states in Lisan al-Arab, refers to the dwellings used by the desert-dwelling Arabs, such as tents, canopies, and the like.

What a beautiful expression, likening the sky to a tent that surrounds the earth. Of course, "the sky" here refers primarily to the atmosphere and the compressed air that encompasses the earth, stretched like a tent over the entire globe.

This great divine tent reduces the intensity of the sun's radiation; for if it were not for the atmosphere, the sun's rays—as well as lethal cosmic rays—would leave no living creature on earth. For this reason, space travelers are forced to constantly use special, heavy, and expensive suits against these radiations.

Furthermore, this tent prevents the fall of meteorites that are constantly attracted to the earth; upon first contact, due to their speed and pressure, it ignites them so that their ashes settle gently on the ground.

And this is exactly what is referred to in Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 32, as a "protected roof": وَ جَعَلْنَا السَّماءَ سَقْفاً مَحْفُوظاً [1]. Then, moving from the signs in the horizons (afaqi) to the signs within the self (anfusi), it says: "He is the One who formed you and perfected your forms" (وَ صَوَّرَكُمْ فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ).

A balanced and upright stature, with a beautiful and pleasant face, in ultimate order and strength—the superiority of which over the forms of other living creatures and various animals is clear and evident at first glance. This very specific structure enables man to engage in various types of delicate or heavy crafts and industries, and with various limbs, to live comfortably and benefit from the gifts of life.

[1]: For further explanation, refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 13, p. 398.

Unlike most animals that consume food and water with their mouths, man picks up food and drink with his hands with precision and delicacy and places them in his mouth. This greatly assists in selecting pure food over food contaminated with external or redundant parts; he peels fruits easily and discards the unusable parts.

Some commentators have taken "form" (صورت) here in a general sense, including both the external and internal form, referring to the various talents and tastes that Allah has created in man, granting him superiority over other living beings.

Finally, in stating the fourth and last blessing in this series, it raises the subject of pure provisions, saying: "And He provided you with good things" (وَ رَزَقَكُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ).

"Tayyibat" (good/pure things) has a very broad meaning that includes everything pure, whether food, clothing, spouses, houses, mounts, or even pure speech and conversations.

Man may, due to ignorance, defile these pure gifts, but Allah created them pure in the world of creation.

At the end of the verse, after mentioning these four great blessings—half of which relate to the heavens and the earth, and half to human beings—it says: "That is Allah, your Lord" (ذلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ) [1]. "And since it is so, blessed and eternal is Allah, the Lord of the worlds" (فَتَبارَكَ اللَّهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ).

[1]: "Dhalikum" (ذلكم) originally indicates distance, which in such cases is an allusion to the loftiness of station and greatness. However, since this expression is not common in Persian [or English], we interpret it in translations as a reference to the near.

Indeed, the One who has bestowed all these gifts upon humans is the Manager of the universe, and He alone is worthy of servitude and worship.

The next verse pursues the issue of the Oneness of worship (Tawhid al-Ubudiyyah) through another path, which is the exclusivity of life in its true sense to Allah. It says: "He is the Ever-Living" (هُوَ الْحَيُّ).

For His life is from His essence and is not dependent on another; it is a life in which death has no way and is eternal. Only Allah is such, and all living beings other than Him have a life mixed with death, and they receive this limited and temporary life from the Pure Essence of Allah.

It is clear that one must worship the One who is alive and possesses absolute life; therefore, it adds immediately: "There is no deity except Him" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ).

"And now that it is so, call upon Him alone, making your religion sincere for Him" (فَادْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ).

And set aside whatever is other than Him, for they all perish, and even during their life, they are constantly changing. "That which does not accept change is He," and "That which has not died and shall not die is He!" The verse ends with this sentence: "Praise be to Allah, the Lord of the worlds" (الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

This sentence is, in fact, a teaching for the servants to praise and thank Allah for the blessings mentioned in the previous verses—blessings that encompass the entire existence of man—especially the blessing of life.

In the last verse under discussion, as a conclusion to the previous monotheistic discussions and to despair the polytheists and idolaters, the address is turned to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "Say: I have been forbidden from worshipping those you call upon besides Allah, since clear proofs and evidences have come to me from my Lord" (قُلْ إِنِّي نُهِيتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ لَمَّا جاءَنِي الْبَيِّناتُ مِنْ رَبِّي).

Not only have I been forbidden from worshipping other than Him, but "I have been commanded to submit to the Lord of the worlds" (وَ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

On one hand, there is the prohibition of worshipping idols, followed by logical and clear evidence and manifest proofs from reason and revelation that have come from the Lord. On the other hand, there is the command to submit to the "Lord of the worlds," which itself is another proof of the objective; for being the Lord of the worlds is sufficient reason for submitting to His Pure Essence.

It is noteworthy that in this verse, the subject of the command and the prohibition are distinct: the command is to submit to Allah, and the prohibition is against worshipping idols. This difference may be because, regarding idols, the only thing conceivable is worship, which is forbidden; but regarding Allah, in addition to worship and adoration, one must be submissive to His commands and decrees.

Therefore, we read in Surah Az-Zumar, verses 11 and 12: "Say: I have been commanded to worship Allah, making the religion sincere for Him, and I have been commanded to be the first of those who submit."

In any case, the expressions in the above verse, which have parallels in other surahs of the Quran, are exceptionally soft, gentle, and polite expressions used against stubborn and fierce enemies, so that if they have the slightest readiness to accept the truth, they may be influenced.

Observe: it says, "I have been so commanded and I have been so forbidden," meaning you should judge for yourselves, without provoking their sense of stubbornness.

A final word regarding the above verses is that in three consecutive verses, the description of Allah as "Lord of the worlds" is repeated.

First, it says: "Blessed is Allah, the Lord of the worlds" (فَتَبارَكَ اللَّهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ).

Then it says: "Praise be to Allah, the Lord of the worlds" (الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

And then it says: "I have been commanded to submit to the Lord of the worlds" (أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

A kind of logical order is observed among them: for first, the talk is of Allah being eternal and blessed; then, of the exclusivity of all praise and thanks to His Pure Essence; and finally, the exclusivity of servitude and worship to His Holy Essence.

Ayahs

هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ عَلَقَةٍ ثُمَّ يُخْرِجُكُمْ طِفْلًا ثُمَّ لِتَبْلُغُوٓا۟ أَشُدَّكُمْ ثُمَّ لِتَكُونُوا۟ شُيُوخًا ۚ وَمِنكُم مَّن يُتَوَفَّىٰ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَلِتَبْلُغُوٓا۟ أَجَلًا مُّسَمًّى وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٦٧)
It is He who created you from dust, then from a drop of [seminal] fluid, then from a clinging mass, then He brings you forth as infants, then [He nurtures you] so that you may come of age, and then that you may become aged—though there are some of you who die earlier—and complete a specified term, and so that you may exercise your reason. (67)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۖ فَإِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (٦٨)
It is He who gives life and brings death. When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is. (68)

Commentary

The Seven Stages of Human Creation

Once again, in continuation of the verses on Monotheism (Tawhid), the text addresses a portion of the "Signs within the Self" (ayat al-anfusi), describing the stages of human evolutionary creation from dust, the embryonic period, and the duration of life in this world until the moment of death, in seven stages. This is done both to clarify the greatness of His power and Lordship, and to highlight His favors and blessings upon His servants.

He says: "He is the One who created you from dust, then from a drop of fluid (sperm-drop), then from a clinging clot (something resembling congealed blood), then He brings you forth as a child from your mother’s womb, then [He nurtures you] so that you may reach your maturity and strength, then that you may become old—though some of you are taken [in death] before that—and that you may reach a specified term, and perhaps you may reflect" (هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقكُمْ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ عَلَقَةٍ ثُمَّ يُخْرِجُكُمْ طِفْلًا ثُمَّ لِتَبْلُغُوا أَشُدَّكُمْ ثُمَّ لِتَكُونُوا شُيُوخاً وَ مِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُتَوَفَّى مِنْ قَبْلُ وَ لِتَبْلُغُوا أَجَلًا مُسَمًّى وَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ).

Accordingly, the first stage is the stage of turab (dust), which refers to the creation of Adam—our first ancestor—from dust, or the creation of all humans from dust, since the nutrients that form the human being and even his sperm-drop, whether from animal or plant sources, all originate from the soil.

The second stage is the stage of the nutfah (sperm-drop), which pertains to all humans except Adam and his wife, Eve.

The third stage is the stage where the nutfah has evolved and grown significantly, becoming a piece of clotted blood (alaqah).

After this stage come the stages of the mudghah (something resembling chewed flesh), the appearance of limbs, and the stage of sensation and movement, though the Quran does not mention these three stages here, even though it has referred to them in other verses.

Here, the fourth stage is mentioned as the birth of the fetus. The fifth stage is the stage of the perfection of physical power and strength, which some consider to be the age of thirty, when the maximum growth of physical faculties is achieved, while others have suggested it to be slightly less or more. Of course, this may vary among individuals, and the Quran expresses it as "reaching maturity" (bulugh al-ashudd).

From then on, the stage of decline and loss of strength begins, and gradually the period of old age, which is the sixth stage, arrives.

Finally, the end of life, which is the last stage, arrives, and the transition from this abode to the eternal abode takes place.

With all these orderly and calculated changes and evolutions, is there still room for doubt regarding the power and greatness of the Originator of the universe and His graces and favors?

It is noteworthy that in the first four stages—pertaining to creation from dust, the sperm-drop, the clot, and the birth of the child—the expression "He created you" (khalaqakum) is used, attributing no role to the human being himself. However, in the three stages after birth—namely, reaching the peak of physical strength, then old age, and then the end of life—the expressions "so that you may reach" (li-tablughu) and "so that you may be" (li-takunu) are used. This is both an indication of the human's existential independence after birth and possibly an allusion to the fact that these three periods may be hastened or delayed by the human's own good or bad management; sometimes premature aging or premature death may overtake a person. This shows how precise and calculated the Quranic expressions are.

The use of the term yutawaffa regarding death—as we have mentioned before—indicates that "death" in the logic of the Quran does not mean annihilation or non-existence. Rather, the angels of death "receive" the human soul and transfer it to the world after death. This expression, repeated many times in the Quran, clearly demonstrates the Islamic view of death, completely removing it from the material concept of annihilation and introducing it as a gateway to the world of eternity.

The phrase "and some of you are taken [in death] before that" (wa minkum man yutawaffa min qabl) may refer to dying before reaching the stage of old age, or it may refer to all previous stages—meaning that before reaching any of the stages, the possibility of death exists.

It is also noteworthy that all these stages are linked to one another with the word thumma (then), which indicates a sequence with an interval, except for the final stage—reaching the end of life—which is linked by the conjunction wa (and). This difference in expression may be because reaching the end of life does not always follow old age, as "many a youth has died while not one old man did," or even individuals die before reaching the period of youth.

(Regarding the "specified term" (ajal musamma), we have discussed it in Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 148; Volume 6, page 157; and Volume 11, page 281).

In the last verse under discussion, the text mentions the most important manifestations of the Lord's power, namely the issue of life and death—the two phenomena that, despite all the progress of human science, remain among the unsolved mysteries. He says: "He is the One who gives life and causes death" (هُوَ الَّذِي يُحْيِي وَ يُمِيتُ).

Yes, life and death in the broad sense of the word—whether in plants, various types of animals, or humans—are in the hands of Allah. Life appears in various forms and diverse types.

Interestingly, from single-celled living organisms to giant animals, and from the depths of dark and gloomy oceans to birds that fly at the peak of the heavens; from the tiny microscopic plant floating amidst the ocean waves to trees tens of meters tall—each possesses a type of life and specific conditions, and accordingly, their deaths are also different. Without doubt, the facets of life are the most diverse and amazing facets of the world of creation.

Particularly, the transition from the inanimate world to the world of living beings, and the transition from the world of life to death, contains wonders that reveal the secrets of creation, and each is a sign among the signs of Allah's power.

However, it is noteworthy that none of these important and complex matters pose any difficulty or problem for His power; they occur as soon as He wills and commands.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, He says: "When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, 'Be,' and it is!" (فَإِذا قَضى‌ أَمْراً فَإِنَّما يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ).

Even the expression "Be" (kun) followed by "and it is" (fa-yakun) is due to the limitation of words; otherwise, there is not even a need for the sentence "Be." Allah's will is identical to the realization of the beings. [^1]

[^1]: Regarding the interpretation of the phrase "Be, and it is" (kun fa-yakun), we have discussed it in detail in Volume 1, under verse 117 of Surah Al-Baqarah, page 418 (new edition).

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ أَنَّىٰ يُصْرَفُونَ (٦٩)
Have you not regarded those who dispute the signs of Allah, where they are being led away [from Allah’s way]? (69)
ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَبِمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا بِهِۦ رُسُلَنَا ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ (٧٠)
—Those who deny the Book and what we have sent with Our apostles. Soon they will know (70)
إِذِ ٱلْأَغْلَـٰلُ فِىٓ أَعْنَـٰقِهِمْ وَٱلسَّلَـٰسِلُ يُسْحَبُونَ (٧١)
when they are dragged [with] iron collars and chains around their necks (71)
فِى ٱلْحَمِيمِ ثُمَّ فِى ٱلنَّارِ يُسْجَرُونَ (٧٢)
into scalding waters and then set aflame in the Fire. (72)
ثُمَّ قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ (٧٣)
Then they will be told, ‘Where are they whom you used to worship as deities (73)
مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ ضَلُّوا۟ عَنَّا بَل لَّمْ نَكُن نَّدْعُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ شَيْـًٔا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٧٤)
besides Allah?’ They will say, ‘They have forsaken us. No, we did not invoke anything before.’ That is how Allah leads the faithless astray. (74)
ذَٰلِكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَفْرَحُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَمْرَحُونَ (٧٥)
‘That [punishment] is because you used to boast unduly on the earth and because you used to strut. (75)
ٱدْخُلُوٓا۟ أَبْوَٰبَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ فَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ (٧٦)
Enter the gates of hell, to remain in it [forever].’ Evil is the [final] abode of the arrogant. (76)

Commentary

The Fate of Arrogant Adversaries

Once again, these verses speak of those who rise in dispute against the Divine signs and do not submit to the proofs of prophethood and the content of the prophets' invitation. In these verses, the fate of such individuals is clearly depicted.

First, It says: "Have you not seen those who dispute concerning the signs of Allah—how they are turned away [from the truth]?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ يُجادِلُونَ فِي آياتِ اللَّهِ أَنَّى يُصْرَفُونَ).

This disputation and dialogue coupled with stubbornness and animosity, these blind imitations, and baseless prejudices cause them to be drawn away from the Straight Path into devious ways, for truths are revealed only in the light of a truth-seeking spirit.

In fact, expressing this matter in the form of a wonder-filled question addressed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله indicates that any impartial person looking at their situation would be struck with amazement at their deviation: how, despite all these clear signs and indications, do they not see the truth?!

Then, providing further explanation about them, He adds: "Those who deny the Book and that with which We sent Our messengers" (الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِالْكِتابِ وَ بِما أَرْسَلْنا بِهِ رُسُلَنا). It is noteworthy that in this Surah, "those who dispute concerning the signs of Allah" are mentioned several times, appearing in three instances as (الَّذِينَ يُجادِلُونَ فِي آياتِ اللَّهِ) (Verse 35, 56, and the verse under discussion). Contextual evidence shows that "the signs of Allah" (آياتِ اللَّهِ) primarily refers to the signs of prophethood and the content of the Heavenly Books. Of course, since the signs of Monotheism (Tawhid) and matters related to the Resurrection were also presented in the Heavenly Books, they too fell within the realm of their disputation.

Is this repetition for the emphasis of this important matter, or was a new point intended in each instance?

The second possibility seems closer to the truth, as each of these three verses pursues a specific point.

In Verse 56, the talk is about the motivation for such disputes, which is pride, arrogance, and self-superiority.

While in Verse 35, it discusses their worldly punishment, which is the matter of Allah setting a seal upon their hearts.

And in the verse under discussion, the dialogue is about their Hereafter punishment and the various types of Hellish penalties.

It is also necessary to note that "yujadilun" (يُجادِلُونَ) is in the present continuous tense, indicating persistence; it suggests that these individuals who have denied the Divine signs constantly engage in baseless disputes and dialogues to justify their beliefs and ugly actions.

In any case, at the end of the verse, He threatens them with these words: "But they are going to know" (فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ).

"When the shackles are around their necks and the chains; they will be dragged" (إِذِ الْأَغْلالُ فِي أَعْناقِهِمْ وَ السَّلاسِلُ يُسْحَبُونَ) [1]

"Into the scalding water; then in the Fire they will be burned"! (فِي الْحَمِيمِ ثُمَّ فِي النَّارِ يُسْجَرُونَ). "Yusjarun" (يُسْجَرُونَ) is derived from "sajr" (سجر). According to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, it means to kindle a fire and make it flame up. According to a group of other

[1]: "Aghlal" (أغلال) is the plural of "ghull" (غل), meaning a collar placed around the neck or hands and feet. It originally comes from the root "ghalal" (غلل), meaning water that flows among trees. If betrayal is called "ghulul" and the heat resulting from thirst is called "ghalil," it is because of their gradual penetration into the human interior. "Salasil" (سلاسل) is the plural of "silsilah" (سلسلة), meaning chain. "Yushabun" (يسحبون) is from the root "sahb" (سحب), meaning to drag.

lexicographers and commentators, it means to fill an oven with fire [1]. For this reason, some have understood from the verse that this group of disbelievers themselves become the fuel for Hell, just as we read in Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 24: فَاتَّقُوا النَّارَ الَّتِي وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ وَ الْحِجارَةُ: "Fear the Fire, whose fuel is men and stones." A group has understood from this expression that their entire being will be filled with fire (of course, these two meanings are not contradictory).

This type of punishment for the disputers and stubborn arrogant ones is, in fact, a reaction appropriate to their actions in this world. Out of pride and arrogance, they denied the Divine signs and trapped themselves in the chains of blind imitations and prejudices. On that Day, with ultimate humiliation and abasement, shackles will be placed on their necks, they will be dragged through scalding water, and then they will turn into the fuel of Hell.

In addition to these physical torments, they are punished with a series of painful spiritual torments. Among them is what is mentioned in the following verse, where He says: "Then it will be said to them, 'Where are those you used to associate [with Allah]?'" (ثُمَّ قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَيْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ).

"Other than Allah?" (مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ). So that they may intercede for you and deliver you from these painful torments and the turbulent waves of the fire

[1]: Tafsir al-Safi, Ruh al-Ma'ani, and Al-Kashshaf under the verses discussed. In Lisan al-Arab, the primary meaning of "sajr" is given as "to fill," stating "sajartu al-nahr" means "I filled the river with water."

of Hell. Did you not say many times that we worship these so that they may be our intercessors? "So where has their intercession gone?!" But they, with bowed heads and broken spirits, will say in response: "They have departed from us; they have vanished and perished such that there is no news of them" (قالُوا ضَلُّوا عَنَّا) [1]. Undoubtedly—as mentioned in other verses of the Quran—these false deities are in Hell, and perhaps they are right beside them, but since they have no role, influence, or effect, it is as if they are lost and gone! Then they see that the very confession of worshipping idols is a brand of shame on their foreheads; therefore, they move to deny it and say: "Rather, we did not used to invoke anything before"! (بَلْ لَمْ نَكُنْ نَدْعُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ شَيْئاً).

These were nothing but delusions and fantasies that we imagined to be realities; they were mirages in the desert of our lives that we mistook for water. But today it has become clear to us that they are names without named entities and words without meaning or concept, and worshipping them was nothing but deviation, wandering, and futility. Therefore, they are recounting an undeniable reality.

There is another possibility in the interpretation of this verse: that they resort to lying, thinking they can save themselves from disgrace with a lie, as we read in Surah Al-An'am, Verses 23 and 24: ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُنْ فِتْنَتُهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا وَ اللَّهِ رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ انْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَذَبُوا عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَ ضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ:

[1]: Commentators have given two meanings for "dallu" (ضَلُّوا) here: some have taken it to mean "they were lost" (ضاعوا) and "they perished" (هلكوا), and some to mean "they disappeared" (غابوا), as we say "dallat al-dabbah," meaning "it disappeared and its location is unknown."

"Then their only plea will be to say, 'By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists.' See how they will lie about themselves, and how that which they used to invent will fail them!" At the end of the verse, He says: "Thus does Allah leave the disbelievers to stray" (كَذلِكَ يُضِلُّ اللَّهُ الْكافِرِينَ).

Their disbelief and stubbornness become a veil and a curtain over their hearts and minds; thus, they leave the straight path of truth and step into devious ways. In the Resurrection as well, they are deprived of the path to Paradise and are drawn into the devious path of Hell. Yes, thus does Allah leave the disbelievers to stray.

The next verse points to the cause of this group's entanglement in all this calamity, misfortune, and punishment, saying: "That is because you used to exult upon the earth without right and because you used to behave insolently [out of pride and the intoxication of desires]!" (ذلِكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَفْرَحُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ وَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَمْرَحُونَ). You took pleasure in opposing the prophets, killing the believers, and pressuring the deprived and the oppressed. You felt pride and exaltation in committing sins and breaking laws. Now you must pay the atonement for all that misplaced joy, pride, negligence, and intoxication of desire amidst these shackles and chains, and within the flames of fire.

"Tafrahun" (تَفْرَحُونَ) is from the root "farah" (فرح), meaning joy and happiness, which is sometimes praiseworthy and appropriate, as mentioned in Surah Ar-Rum, Verses 4-5: وَ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَفْرَحُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ بِنَصْرِ اللَّهِ: "And on that day the believers will rejoice in the victory of Allah."

And sometimes it is blameworthy and based on falsehood, as we read in the story of Qarun in Surah Al-Qasas, Verse 76:

إِذْ قالَ لَهُ قَوْمُهُ لا تَفْرَحْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْفَرِحِينَ: "Recall when his people said to him, 'Do not exult. Indeed, Allah does not like the exultant [those who rejoice arrogantly].'"

Of course, this difference must be recognized from the context, and it is clear that in the verse under discussion, the second type of "farah" is intended.

"Tamrahun" (تَمْرَحُونَ) is from the root "marah" (مرح). According to a group of lexicographers and commentators, it means intensity of joy and its expansiveness.

Some have considered it to mean joy over baseless matters.

While others have considered it to mean joy accompanied by a kind of vanity and using Divine blessings in the path of falsehood.

It appears that all these meanings return to one point, for intensity of joy and excess in it lead to all these issues and are accompanied by various sins, impurities, debauchery, and sensuality [1]. Yes, such joys accompanied by pride, negligence, and unawareness, and joined with sensuality and lust, quickly distance man from Allah and prevent him from perceiving truths. They make realities seem like a joke and truths seem metaphorical. Such people have no fate other than what was stated in the above verses.

It is here that they are addressed: "Enter the gates of Hell to abide eternally therein" (ادْخُلُوا أَبْوابَ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدِينَ فِيها). "And wretched is the residence of the arrogant" (فَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى الْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ).

[1]: Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Al-Farah is the expansion of the chest with immediate pleasure, and it most often occurs in physical pleasures. Al-Marah is the intensity of joy and expansion in it."

This sentence is a renewed emphasis that the primary source of their misfortunes was that very pride and arrogance—the same pride that is the mother of all corruption, a veil before the truth-seeing eyes of man, the factor of resistance against the prophets, and the cause of persistence in the path of falsehood.

In this verse, we again encounter "abwab jahannam" (the gates of Hell). Does entering through the gates of Hell mean that each group enters through a specific gate? Or does one group pass through multiple gates? Meaning that Hell, like some terrifying and nested prisons, consists of various wards or levels; a group of the most stubborn of the misguided must pass through all these levels and be placed in the "lowest depth" (al-dark al-asfal) and the bottom of Hell! Evidence for this is a hadith narrated from the Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام in the interpretation of the verse: لَهَا سَبْعَةُ أَبْوَابٍ لِكُلِّ بَابٍ مِنْهُمْ جُزْءٌ مَقْسُومٌ (Al-Hijr: 44), where he said:

"Indeed, Hell has seven gates, levels one above the other," then he placed one of his hands over the other and said, "Like this!" [1]

There is also another interpretation here, the summary of which is: the gates of Hell—like the gates of Paradise—refer to the various factors that draw man toward Hell or Paradise. Every type of sin and every type of good deed is considered a gate. This is also pointed out in Islamic narrations. According to this interpretation, the number 7 is for "multiplicity" rather than a specific "count," and the statement that Paradise has eight gates refers to the factors of mercy exceeding the factors of punishment and wrath (reflect on this).

These two interpretations are not in conflict with each other [1].

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 5 & 6, p. 338 (under Verse 44 of Surah Al-Hijr). Other narrations have also been cited in this regard, which the late Allamah Majlisi included in Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 8, pp. 289, 301, and 285. [1]: We have provided more explanation on this in Vol. 11, p. 77 (under Verse 44 of Surah Al-Hijr).

Ayahs

فَٱصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ ۚ فَإِمَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِلَيْنَا يُرْجَعُونَ (٧٧)
So be patient! Allah’s promise is indeed true. Whether We show you a part of what We promise them, or take you away [before that], [in any case] they will be brought back to Us. (77)
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلًا مِّن قَبْلِكَ مِنْهُم مَّن قَصَصْنَا عَلَيْكَ وَمِنْهُم مَّن لَّمْ نَقْصُصْ عَلَيْكَ ۗ وَمَا كَانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَن يَأْتِىَ بِـَٔايَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ قُضِىَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَخَسِرَ هُنَالِكَ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ (٧٨)
Certainly We have sent apostles before you. Of them are those We have recounted to you, and of them are those We have not recounted to you. An apostle may not bring any sign except by Allah’s permission. Hence, when Allah’s edict comes, judgment is made with justice, and it is thence that the falsifiers become losers. (78)

Commentary

Be Patient Once More

Following the previous discussions regarding the obstructions of the disbelievers, their arrogance, pride, and their denial of Divine verses, the two verses under discussion comfort the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and command him to practice patience and steadfastness in the face of these hardships.

First, it says: "So be patient; indeed, the promise of Allah is truth" (فَاصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌ).

Both the promise of victory given to you, and the promise of painful punishment for the arrogant, proud, and deniers, are true and will undoubtedly be realized.

Then, so that the enemies of truth do not imagine that if there is a delay in their punishment, they can escape the grasp of Divine retribution and torment, it adds:

"Whether We show you some of what We have promised them during your lifetime, or We take you [in death] before they are seized by punishment, it does not matter, for in any case, to Us they will be returned" and We will fulfill Our promises regarding them (فَإِمَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ الَّذِي نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِلَيْنا يُرْجَعُونَ) [1]. Your duty is only the clear delivery of the message and the completion of the proof (atmam al-hujjah) for everyone, so that awakened hearts may be enlightened by your preaching, and no excuse or pretext remains for the opponents. You should have no attachment to anything other than fulfilling this duty, and do not even be concerned with whether the burning fire of your heart regarding this rebellious group is soothed by immediate Divine punishment.

This statement, meanwhile, is a clear threat to them so they may know that in any case, they will be caught in the grasp of Divine punishment. Just as a group of them met their retribution in this world on the field of Badr and similar scenes, a larger group will see the recompense for their deeds on the Day of Resurrection.

[1]: A similar meaning is also mentioned in Surah Yunus, verse 46.

Again, for further consolation and to comfort the Prophet, it refers to the similar situation of previous prophets, who were also entangled in such problems but continued their path and embraced victory. It says: "We have already sent messengers before you. Among them are those whose stories We have related to you, and among them are those whose stories We have not related to you" (وَ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا رُسُلًا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ قَصَصْنا عَلَيْكَ وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ لَمْ نَقْصُصْ عَلَيْكَ). Each of them grappled with scenes like these and grueling hardships, and they faced many stubborn, arrogant, and proud nations, but ultimately the religion of truth prevailed and the oppressors and criminals were defeated.

And since the pretext-seeking and stubborn polytheists and disbelievers demanded their desired miracles from the prophets every day—and the polytheists of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) time repeated this same method—the Quran adds following this statement: "It was not for any messenger to bring a sign [miracle] except by the permission of Allah!" (وَ ما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ).

Fundamentally, all miracles are in the hands of Allah and cannot be a plaything for the disbelievers. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) can never submit to their "suggested miracles." Whatever is necessary and essential for guiding people and finding the truth, He makes manifest through the hands of His prophets.

Then, with a serious and threatening tone, it warns those who said, "If you are truthful, why does the Divine punishment not come to us?": "When the command of Allah for the punishment of these stubborn deniers is issued, it will be judged between them in truth, and the followers of falsehood will then be the losers" (فَإِذا جاءَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ قُضِيَ بِالْحَقِّ وَ خَسِرَ هُنالِكَ الْمُبْطِلُونَ).

On that day, the doors of repentance will be closed, the paths of return blocked, and moans, cries, and wails will lead nowhere. It is on that day that the travelers of the path of falsehood clearly see that they have lost all the capital of their existence and bought no commodity; rather, they have been caught by the wrath, anger, and painful punishment of Allah. So why do they insist so much that this irreversible day should arrive?! According to this interpretation, the above verse refers to the "punishment of eradication" (adhab al-istisal).

However, a group of commentators have considered this verse to refer to the command of punishment on the Day of Resurrection. It is there that the judgment of truth occurs among everyone, and the followers of falsehood become aware of their loss and being losers from every aspect.

The expression in verse 27 of Surah Al-Jathiyah also supports this interpretation, where it says: وَ يَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَخْسَرُ الْمُبْطِلُونَ: "And the Day the Hour is established—that Day the followers of falsehood will lose."

However, the expression "امر اللَّه" (the command of Allah) and its likes have been used in several verses regarding worldly punishment [1]. It is also possible that the verse has a broad meaning that includes both worldly punishment and the punishment of the Hereafter, and in both scenes, the loss of the followers of falsehood becomes apparent.

It is noteworthy that we read in a hadith: In the city of Medina, there was a jester who made people laugh and would sometimes state, "This man (Imam Sajjad Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام) has exhausted me; I have not been able to make him laugh until now." So one day, as the Imam (عليه السلام) was passing by, that man came and took the Imam's cloak from his blessed shoulders and left. The Imam (عليه السلام) paid him no attention. Those with him went after the man, took the cloak, and placed it back on the Imam's shoulders. The Imam (عليه السلام) asked, "Who was this person?" They said, "He is a jester who makes the people of Medina laugh." He said, "Tell him: Indeed, Allah has a day in which the followers of falsehood will lose!" (ان للَّه يوما يخسر فيه المبطلون) [2].

[1]: Like Hud 43, 76, and 101. [2]: "Amali al-Saduq" as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 537.

Key Point: The Number of Divine Prophets

Many commentators, in accordance with the above verses, have raised a discussion here regarding the number of Divine prophets, and various narrations have been quoted in this regard.

The famous narration in this field indicates the number 124,000, while in some narrations, the number of prophets is counted as 8,000, of whom 4,000 were from the Children of Israel and 4,000 from others [1]. In a hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام), it is stated that the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) said:

"Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, created 124,000 prophets, of whom I am the most honorable to Allah, and I say this without pride. And Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, created 124,000 successors (awsiya), of whom Ali (عليه السلام) is the most honorable and superior to Allah." [2]

In another narration from Anas ibn Malik from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), it is quoted: "I was sent following 8,000 prophets, 4,000 of whom were from the Children of Israel." [3] These two hadiths do not contradict each other, as it is possible that the second hadith refers to the major prophets (as the late Allamah Majlisi explained in clarifying this statement).

Again, in another hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read that in response to Abu Dharr's question about the number of Divine prophets, he stated the number 124,000, and following the question about the number of messengers (rasul) among them, he mentioned the number 313 [4]. Also, in another hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), after mentioning the number 124,000, we read: "Five of them were Ulul-Azm (Arch-Prophets): Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله)." [5] Other narrations have also been quoted in this regard that confirm the above number.

In any case, from what we have said, it becomes clear that this narration is not a "khabar wahid" (isolated report)—as "Bar-Sawi" quoted from some scholars in the commentary "Ruh al-Bayan"—rather, numerous and widely transmitted (mustafid) reports confirm that the Divine prophets were 124,000, reports that have entered various Islamic sources.

It is noteworthy that the number of prophets whose names are explicitly mentioned in the Quran is only 26, and they are:

Adam, Noah, Idris, Salih, Hud, Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Joseph, Lot, Jacob, Moses, Aaron, Shu'ayb, Zechariah, John, Jesus, David, Solomon, Elijah, Elisha, Dhul-Kifl, Job, Jonah, Ezra (Uzair), and Muhammad (عليهم الصلاة و السلام).

However, there are other prophets to whom references are made in the Quran without their names being explicitly stated, such as "Samuel," who is referred to in verse 248 of Surah Al-Baqarah as وَ قالَ لَهُمْ نَبِيُّهُمْ (And their prophet said to them).

And "Jeremiah," who is referred to in verse 259 of Al-Baqarah as أَوْ كَالَّذِي مَرَّ عَلى‌ قَرْيَةٍ (Or like the one who passed by a township...) [6]. And "Joshua," who is referred to in verse 60 of Surah Al-Kahf as وَ إِذْ قالَ مُوسى‌ لِفَتاهُ (And when Moses said to his servant) (assuming Joshua was among the prophets).

And "Khidr," who is mentioned in verse 65 of Al-Kahf as فَوَجَدا عَبْداً مِنْ عِبادِنا (And they found a servant from among Our servants).

And also the "Tribes of Israel" (Al-Asbat), who were the leaders of the tribes, and it is explicitly stated in verse 163 of Surah An-Nisa that Divine revelation descended upon them: وَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلى‌ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ إِسْماعِيلَ وَ إِسْحاقَ وَ يَعْقُوبَ وَ الْأَسْباطِ (And We revealed to Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the Tribes...).

And if there were prophets among the brothers of Joseph, their situation is repeatedly referred to in Surah Yusuf.

In short: the number of prophets whose stories and biographies Allah has alluded to is much more than 26; this number only pertains to those whose names are explicitly mentioned.

Ayahs

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمَ لِتَرْكَبُوا۟ مِنْهَا وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ (٧٩)
It is Allah who created the cattle for you that you may ride some of them, and some of them you eat; (79)
وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ وَلِتَبْلُغُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا حَاجَةً فِى صُدُورِكُمْ وَعَلَيْهَا وَعَلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ (٨٠)
and there are [numerous] uses in them for you, and that over them you may satisfy any need that is in your breasts, and you are carried on them and on ships. (80)
وَيُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ فَأَىَّ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ تُنكِرُونَ (٨١)
He shows you His signs. So which of the signs of Allah do you deny? (81)

Commentary

The Various Benefits of Livestock

Once again, these verses return to the signs of Allah’s power and His extensive blessings toward humanity, explaining another aspect of them to both familiarize people with His greatness and to stir within them a sense of gratitude, which serves as a means to attain the knowledge of Allah (Ma'rifat Allah).

He says: "Allah is He who created livestock for you, so that you may ride some of them, and from some of them you eat" (اللَّهُ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَنْعامَ لِتَرْكَبُوا مِنْها وَ مِنْها تَأْكُلُونَ).

Some of them are used solely for food, such as sheep, while others are used for both riding and food, like the camel, which is both the "ship of the dry and scorching deserts" and a source of nourishment for humans! The word "An'am" (livestock) is the plural of "Na'am" (on the pattern of Qalam). Originally, it referred specifically to camels, but its meaning later expanded to include camels, cows, and sheep. This word is derived from the root "Ni'mah" (blessing), because livestock are considered among the greatest blessings for mankind. Even today, despite the invention of so many high-speed air and land vehicles, there are still cases where one must exclusively use livestock. In sandy deserts where the passage of vehicles is extremely difficult, and in certain narrow mountain passes, the only means of travel remains livestock! Fundamentally, the creation of livestock with such diverse physical forms, and especially with a spirit of submission and the capacity to be tamed—despite often being stronger than the most powerful humans—is itself one of the great signs of Allah.

We know of small, tiny animals that are extremely dangerous to humans due to their wild nature, yet sometimes the reins of a long train of massive camels are handed to a child, "and he leads them wherever he desires!"

Beyond this, there are many other uses for them, as pointed out in the following verse: "And for you in them are [other] benefits" (وَ لَكُمْ فِيها مَنافِعُ).

You utilize their milk, wool, skin, and other parts, and even their waste is useful in agriculture and elsewhere. In short, nothing in the existence of these animals is without use; everything is beneficial and advantageous. Even certain medicinal substances are extracted from the bodies of livestock.

(Note that the indefinite form of "Manafi'" [benefits] is used to express their great importance).

Then He adds: "And that you may reach through them a goal [that is] in your hearts" (وَ لِتَبْلُغُوا عَلَيْها حاجَةً فِي صُدُورِكُمْ).

Some commentators have interpreted this phrase as referring to the transportation of "cargo" by livestock, which was not mentioned in the previous phrases. However, it is also possible that "a goal in your hearts" refers to personal and non-general objectives, such as recreational use, migration, travel, racing, and sometimes gaining prestige and the like.

And since these are all means of travel on land, at the end of the verse He adds: "And upon them and upon ships you are carried" (وَ عَلَيْها وَ عَلَى الْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ) [1]. The expression "upon them" (referring to livestock), even though they were mentioned previously, serves as a preamble to mentioning "Fulk" (ships). That is, Allah has placed at your disposal the means of travel and transportation of goods on both land and sea so that you may easily reach your destinations.

He created a property in ships that allows them to remain atop the water despite all their weight and heaviness,

[1]: Regarding the benefits of animals, we discussed this in detail in Volume 11, page 158 onwards (under verse 5 of Surah An-Nahl).

and He has regulated the flow of winds such that they can be used continuously along specific routes to "reach the meeting of a loved one."

In the final verse under discussion, to emphasize and extract an admission from everyone, He says: "And He shows you His signs; then which of the signs of Allah will you deny?" (وَ يُرِيكُمْ آياتِهِ فَأَيَّ آياتِ اللَّهِ تُنْكِرُونَ).

Can you deny His signs and indicators in the "horizons" (the external world) or His signs within the "souls" (the internal world)? Can you deny His signs in your own creation from dust, then passing through the embryonic stages, and the stages after birth? Or His signs in the matter of life and death?

And can the divine signs in the heavens and the earth, and the creation of night and day, be viewed with an eye of denial? Or the creation of the means for sustaining life, such as cattle and livestock?

Wherever you look, the traces of Allah are manifest; "blind be the eye that does not see Him."

Truly, while His signs and indicators are clear to everyone, why does a group choose the path of denial?

The great commentator, the late Tabarsi, says in response to this question: This denial may stem from three things:

1- "Whim-worship" (following desires): This causes a person to cover the face of truth with baseless doubts (to continue following their whims and desires, because accepting the truth limits them; on one hand, it defines duties for them, and on the other, it imposes restrictions. But those who worship their whims and do not want to accept those duties or these restrictions rise to deny the truth, even if its proofs are clear and manifest).

2- "Blind imitation" (of others, especially ancestors), which also casts a veil over the face of truth.

3- "False prejudices" and corrupt prior beliefs that have become ingrained in the mind, preventing an impartial investigation and study of the signs of truth; thus, the person remains incapable of perceiving them.

Ayahs

أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَانُوٓا۟ أَكْثَرَ مِنْهُمْ وَأَشَدَّ قُوَّةً وَءَاثَارًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَمَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٨٢)
Have they not travelled over the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them? They were more numerous than them and were greater [than them] in power and with respect to the effects [they left] in the land. But what they used to earn did not avail them. (82)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَرِحُوا۟ بِمَا عِندَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٨٣)
When their apostles brought them manifest proofs, they boasted about the knowledge they possessed, but they were besieged by what they used to deride. (83)
فَلَمَّا رَأَوْا۟ بَأْسَنَا قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَحْدَهُۥ وَكَفَرْنَا بِمَا كُنَّا بِهِۦ مُشْرِكِينَ (٨٤)
Then, when they sighted Our punishment, they said, ‘We believe in Allah alone, and disavow what we used to take as His partners.’ (84)
فَلَمْ يَكُ يَنفَعُهُمْ إِيمَـٰنُهُمْ لَمَّا رَأَوْا۟ بَأْسَنَا ۖ سُنَّتَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِى قَدْ خَلَتْ فِى عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ وَخَسِرَ هُنَالِكَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (٨٥)
But their faith was of no benefit to them when they sighted Our punishment—Allah’s precedent, which has passed among His servants, and it is thence that the faithless will be losers. (85)

Commentary

At the Time of the Descent of Punishment, Faith is Futile

These verses, which constitute the final verses of Surah al-Mu’min, are in fact a kind of conclusion to the preceding discussions. After presenting all those divine signs in the horizons and within the souls, and all those subtle and heart-touching exhortations and dialogues regarding the Hereafter and the great court of Resurrection, Allah confronts the stubborn deniers and arrogant disbelievers with severe threats coupled with reasoning and logic, clearly stating their ultimate end.

First, He says: "Have they not traveled through the land and observed how was the end of those before them?" (أَ فَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ).

If they harbor doubts about the authenticity of recorded history and what is inscribed upon pages, they cannot doubt the authenticity of the traces left upon the face of the earth—the ruined palaces of kings, the decayed bones beneath the soil, and the ruins of disaster-stricken cities—which narrate their stories with eloquent clarity.

"They were more numerous than them and mightier in strength and in traces [left] in the land" (كانُوا أَكْثَرَ مِنْهُمْ وَ أَشَدَّ قُوَّةً وَ آثاراً فِي الْأَرْضِ).

The abundance of their numbers can be recognized from their graves, and their power and traces in the land from what they have left behind as a legacy!

The expression "آثاراً فِي الْأَرْضِ" (traces in the land)—as we mentioned in the commentary of verse 21 of this same Surah, which is similar to this—might refer to their extensive agricultural progress (as mentioned in Surah ar-Rum, verse 9) or to the magnificent buildings and sturdy structures of past nations carved into mountains or built upon plains (as described in Surah ash-Shu'ara, verses 128 and 129).

However, in any case, "the powers they acquired never availed them or saved them when the storm of calamity and divine punishment blew" (فَما أَغْنى‌ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ) [1]. Rather, all these powers were crushed in a few short moments; palaces collapsed and were ruined, and vast, powerful armies fell to the ground like autumn leaves, or were buried amidst mountain-like waves! When those with such immense power met such a fate, what are these weak and helpless polytheists of Mecca thinking, who are considered nothing in comparison to them?!

In the next verse, Allah refers to how they dealt with the Prophets and their miracles and clear proofs, saying: "When their messengers came to them with clear proofs, they turned away from them and were happy and content only with the knowledge they possessed, and they considered anything else as naught" (فَلَمَّا جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَلِحُوا بِما عِنْدَهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ).

And this very matter caused "that which they used to mock"—the divine punishment and threats—"to encompass them" (وَ حاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ).

Regarding what is meant by this "knowledge" (علم) in which they were prideful and because of which they saw themselves as independent of the teachings of the Prophets, commentators have offered various possibilities, all of which can be integrated:

  1. They considered vain doubts and baseless sophistries to be "knowledge" and relied upon them, multiple examples of which are reflected in the verses of the Quran. Sometimes they would say: "مَنْ يُحْيِ الْعِظامَ وَ هِيَ رَمِيمٌ" (Who can give life to these bones while they are decayed?) (Ya-Sin: 78).

And sometimes they said: "أَ إِذا ضَلَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَ إِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ" (When we are lost within the earth, will we indeed be in a new creation?) (As-Sajdah: 10).

At times they added: "ما هِيَ إِلَّا حَياتُنَا الدُّنْيا نَمُوتُ وَ نَحْيا وَ ما يُهْلِكُنا إِلَّا الدَّهْرُ" (There is nothing but our worldly life; we die and live, and nothing destroys us except time!) (Al-Jathiyah: 24).

And similar vain claims without proof which they imagined to be knowledge.

  1. It refers to sciences related to the world and the management of life, just as Qarun (Korah) claimed, saying: "إِنَّما أُوتِيتُهُ عَلى‌ عِلْمٍ عِنْدِي" (I was only given this because of knowledge I have!) (Al-Qasas: 78).

  2. It refers to sciences and disciplines such as rational and philosophical arguments, whether in formal or informal forms, whereby a group, possessing such knowledge, sees themselves as independent of the Prophets—whether in the past or the present! As we said, these interpretations do not contradict one another; the point is that by relying on limited human knowledge, whether in rational sciences and beliefs or in worldly matters, or vain doubts they deemed as knowledge, they rejected the sciences originating from the source of Revelation and mocked them. They were happy with their meager knowledge and saw themselves as completely independent of the Prophets [1].

But the Quran describes the result of this selfishness and pride in the following verses:

"When they saw Our punishment"—the punishment that had descended to uproot them and carried the definitive command of the Lord for their destruction—they repented for what they had done, saw themselves as weak and helpless beings, turned toward the threshold of Truth, and their cry rose: "They said: 'We believe in Allah alone, and we disbelieve in that which we used to associate with Him!'" (فَلَمَّا رَأَوْا بَأْسَنا قالُوا آمَنَّا بِاللَّهِ وَحْدَهُ وَ كَفَرْنا بِما كُنَّا بِهِ مُشْرِكِينَ).

"But their faith was not to benefit them once they saw Our punishment" (فَلَمْ يَكُ يَنْفَعُهُمْ إِيمانُهُمْ لَمَّا رَأَوْا بَأْسَنا).

This is because when the "punishment of extirpation" (عذاب استیصال) descends, the doors of repentance are closed. Fundamentally, such forced faith cannot have the benefit of voluntary faith, as it is born of those extraordinary circumstances; for this reason, whenever the storm of calamity subsides, they resume their previous path.

It is also for this reason that the faith of Pharaoh was not accepted when he fell among the waves of the Nile.

This ruling is not specific to certain individuals or nations; rather, as the Quran says following this statement: "[This is] the established way of Allah which has occurred among His servants [before]" (سُنَّتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي قَدْ خَلَتْ فِي عِبادِهِ).

Then, the final verse under discussion concludes with this sentence: "And there the disbelievers lost" (وَ خَسِرَ هُنالِكَ الْكافِرُونَ).

On that day, they realized they had no capital but a handful of pride and delusions, and what they imagined to be the water of life was nothing more than a mirage. They had wasted the capital of their existence in this detour of life and had produced nothing but sin and the painful punishment of Allah. What loss and ruin could be greater than this?! Thus, Surah al-Mu’min, which began with a description of the state of the arrogant disbelievers, ends with the painful conclusion of their lives!

Key Point: Those Arrogant of Their Knowledge

In various verses of this Surah, as explained, the primary source of deviation and misery for a large group of people is identified as pride and arrogance.

An arrogance that sometimes stems from possessing financial means, or a large number of people and military force, and sometimes from possessing a small amount of information which one perceives as vast.

We see a vivid example of this in our own era, following the scientific and industrial victories in advanced materialist societies. We know that one of the effective factors in the rejection of religion and the turning toward atheistic schools of thought is that very "scientific pride" that emerged in recent centuries for a group of natural scientists. By discovering secrets of nature and gaining access to windows of knowledge, they became so intoxicated and arrogant that they imagined nothing exists in this universe except what they know. Since they did not find God present in their laboratories, they took the path of denial! This scientific pride expanded to the point that they considered religion and the revelation of the Prophets to be the product of human ignorance or fear, saying: "With the arrival of the era of scientific flourishing, there is no longer a need for these matters!" They even went further and divided the intellectual life of humanity into four eras:

  1. The era of myths. 2. The era of religion! 3. The era of philosophy. 4. The era of science—by which they meant natural and empirical sciences! Of course, the fact that the religions existing in the environment of these scientists were filled with many superstitions also aided this false objective (referring mainly to the superstitions of the church authorities). Thus, in their imagination, they removed religion and the teachings of the Prophets from the scene of human life forever.

Fortunately, however, this intoxication and pride did not last long. Other factors joined hands and drew a line of cancellation through these baseless imaginations. As per the aforementioned verses: "When they became arrogant with their knowledge, the punishment of Allah encompassed them and their cries reached nowhere."

On one hand, the First and Second World Wars showed that human scientific and industrial progress not only failed to make him happy but brought him closer than ever to the edge of the precipice.

On the other hand, the emergence of various moral and social corruptions, and the rise of various disorders, murders, killings, mental illnesses, and various financial and sexual transgressions, showed that human sciences alone could never prevent disorders; rather, the misguidance that is usually inseparable from them has sometimes increased their scope.

Thirdly, the numerous enigmas that appeared in the sciences which man found himself unable to solve, and the vast worlds that manifested before his eyes (both the immensely large and the extraordinarily small) which he found himself incapable of knowing, caused him to once again reach out to the teachings of the Prophets. A vast group returned to the shadow of Revelation and sought the cure for these agonizing diseases in the instructions of the Prophets. Churches gained renewed vigor, and religious teachings became part of the life program for many.

In the midst of this, "Islam," with its unique, progressive, and comprehensive teachings, found a new manifestation, and movements toward understanding authentic Islam began.

It is hoped that before the punishment of Allah once again encompasses a group of people in this world, this awakening becomes universal, and the traces of that pride are erased and destroyed so that it does not lead to loss and ruin.

O Lord! Bring us down from the mount of pride, and protect us from arrogance, stubbornness, and selfishness, which are the source of destruction, misery, and shame.

O God! Awaken our world as well, and before Your severe punishment encompasses the people of our era, return them to the merciful shadow of the teachings of Your Prophets.

O Allah! Place us among those who take lessons from the fate of others, so that our fate does not become a lesson for others.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-Mu’min. Night of 27th Muharram al-Haram, 1405 (October 23, 1984).
